Title: Black Women Earn Less Than White Men in Every State and Won’t Reach Pay Equity With White Men Nationally Until 2144
MainIdea: This article is a fact sheet that highlights the results of research conducted on the pay equity between black women and white men. Results show that black women earned 63.7 cents for every dollar earned by white men. Pay disparities varied highly depending on the state- but in every state black women were paid substantially less than white men.
Problems: The COVID pandemic highlighted The extreme racial and gendered inequalities that black women face in The labor market. + The article identified several reasons behind black women’s lower earnings: discrimination in recruitment/hiring/pay/promotions, undervaluation of care work, insufficient investment in public care infrastructure, and lack of well-developed employment rights.
Solutions: The article recommends equity-focused policies to address low pay and achieve equal pay. + Strengthened and updated equal pay laws, wage transparency, minimum wage increase, and better enforcement assistance in the workplace.
Implement: Recommend equity-focused policies that can help address wage inequalities and examine current workplace harassment laws to see what can be strengthened within. + Advocate for minimum wage increase and for wage transparency.

Title: Sentencing Repeat Offenders of Violent and Serious Crime in Florida (PRR)
MainIdea: Florida’s Prison Release Reoffender (PRR) law mandates maximum sentencing without parole for individuals who commit violent felonies within three years of release from prison, the background, application, and critiques of which this report details.
Problems: High recidivism among a small group of violent, repeat offenders; low engagement in reentry and rehabilitative programs; limited sentencing incentives for rehabilitation participation;  PRR laws remove judicial discretion and do not account for root causes like trauma, poverty, or addiction; reentry barriers (e.g. housing, employment) increase risk of re-offense; PRR statute mandates full sentence without parole for specific repeat violent offenses; programs DO exist within FDOC (e.g., PRIDE job training, education, life skills) but have very low participation (under 10%)
Solutions: Increasing incentives for program engagement (e.g., reducing sentence for participation)
Implement: Advocate for reentry programming tied to meaningful incentives (e.g., sentence reduction or housing/job guarantees); partner with employers and nonprofits to expand post-release employment, housing, and case management; fund/target trauma-informed, cognitive-behavioral, substance use recovery programs pre- and post-release; incorporate second-chance hiring initiatives and transitional housing into THRIVE’s economic mobility strategies

Title: Planetary Health is Public Health
MainIdea: Planetary health as a concept examines the multilayered effects on the environment caused by human behavior, demographics, and other complex societal and governmental systems. The interconnectedness of public and planetary health calls for the careful consideration of our impact upon Earth’s various systems.
Problems: The various issues identified within this article are not necessarily Central Florida-specific, but instead may or may not have relevance in The region + Air pollution, biodiversity loss, chemical pollution, land use, nutrient overloading, marine degradation, resource scarcity, and climate change are all amongst The human-caused environmental changes that affect public health.
Solutions: Promote The limitation of human-caused environmental changes: limit vehicle emissions and power plants, use of natural sources that are at risk of biodiversity loss, and cosmetics that contain toxic chemicals.
Implement: Find and empower public health communicators to use community stories to relate The issue of planetary health and lessen how abstract it seems. it is important to use plain language and to combine alarming facts with actionable steps for change. + lessen The negative impact of rampant disinformation and misinformation regarding planetary health by providing accurate information and a clear narrative.

Title:
Houston and Harris County
Residents' Experiences With
Perceived Crimes and Incidents
Motivated by Bias and Prejudice
MainIdea: Nearly half of respondent residents reported being victims of a crime or incident, and a third perceived it was motivated by bias against protected traits (race, gender, religion, etc.).
Problems: High rates of perceived crime and harassment; underreporting due to mistrust in LE and belief “nothing would be done”; lack of mechanisms for non-LEO community-based hate incident response; coordination gap; revalence of unreported incidents that may never meet legal criteria as hate crimes but still cause harm
Solutions: Encourage public discourse and data collection on hate; build cross-sector coalitions like the former Houston Coalition Against Hate
Implement: Launch a CF Community Anti-Bias Task Force modeled after coalitions like Houston’s; work with LE to produce culturally specific victim services and anonymous reporting mechanisms; community liaisons or credible messengers into police departments to build trust and facilitate reporting; -host community listening sessions to uncover hidden incidents and strengthen communal resilience

Title: Arches Reduces Recidivism Among Young Adults on Probation
Authors: NYC Mayor's Office for Economic Opportunity
MainIdea: NYC's implementation of Arches, a group-based mentoring program for young adults on probation, reduces felony reconviction and improves self-perception by pairing participants with “credible messengers” and cognitive-behavioral support
Problems: High recidivism among young adults on probation; lack of culturally relevant or “credible” mentoring and trauma-informed support & inconsistent curriculum delivery; need for stronger service coordination and mentor training; structural barriers to engagement (e.g. rigid schedules, limited referrals
Solutions: Group mentoring with 24/7 access to “credible messengers”; Arches-like cognitive-behavioral journaling and flexible programming structure; support for emotional regulation, future planning, and attitudinal change
Implement: Rolling-enrollment Central Florida Arches-style mentorship program using credible messengers and a healing-centered curriculum

Title: Carbon inequality kills: Why curbing the excessive emissions of an elite few can create a sustainable planet for all.
Authors: Mira Alestig, Nafkote Dabi, Abha Jeurkar and Alex Maitland.
MainIdea: The report reveals that the super-rich, particularly the wealthiest 1%, are emitting disproportionately large amounts of carbon (via jets, yachts, and investments), driving climate breakdown, exacerbating inequity, and causing excess deaths, and argues for urgent policies like progressive wealth and carbon taxes to constrain extreme emissions.
Problems: This report is not region-specific, but its findings resonate in Central Florida: excessive luxury travel, investment-linked emissions, and unchecked consumption by elites contribute to regional air, water, and heat inequities that disproportionately impact low-income and BIPOC communities.
Solutions: Oxfam advocates for targeted policies: carbon wealth taxes, luxury transport levies, and redirecting high-emitting investments into low-carbon assets. These are measures CF could champion at the state level to curb elite emissions and fund equitable climate resilience.
Implement: THRIVE can evaluate local data to quantify elite carbon footprints, build coalitions to advocate for local/state-level carbon taxes or jet/boat levies, create public awareness on carbon inequality’s local harms, and mobilize philanthropic funds to support equitable climate interventions in Central Florida.

Title: Green Space: An Underestimated Tool to Create MoreEqualCities
Authors: Delfina Grinspan, John-Rob Pool, Ayushi Trivedi, James Anderson, and Mathilde Bouyé
MainIdea: There is a direct relationship between urban tree cover and income as a result of historic inequality. A lack of urban tree cover can have negative impacts on the surrounding community.
Problems: Unequal distribution of urban green spaces can lead to green gentrification, urban heat island effect, aue pollution and resulting health impacts, and stormwater flooding.
Solutions: Establish strong political leadership (prioritizes underserved communities), engage communities meaningfully (partner with local organizations), develop innovative funding models (impact bonds, equity criteria, etc.)
Implement: Partner with local organizations to develop and ensure agency in restoration/conservation plans. Advocate for urban green spaces faciliated by innovative funding.

Title: “How Did We Grow? An Analysis of Growth in Central Florida (2007–2017)
Authors: East Central Florida Regional Planning Council
MainIdea: The report evaluates a decade of population, environmental, and infrastructure growth across Central Florida following the How Shall We Grow? regional vision. It examines progress toward sustainable, connected development centered on the “Four Cs”: Conservation, Countryside, Centers, and Corridors.
Problems:
Uneven growth across counties causing environmental strain.
Rising commute times and greenhouse gas emissions.
Water consumption rising faster than conservation efforts.
Solutions:
Expand transit-oriented development (e.g., SunRail corridors).
Implement smart water use targets (≤90 gallons/person/day).
Implement:
Support regional smart-growth partnerships connecting housing, transport, and conservation.
Promote policy advocacy for balanced development and resource protection.
Collaborate on community resilience initiatives tied to water and land use.

Title: Transitions into and out of Poverty in the United States
MainIdea: Transitions into and out of poverty are driven by individual events (e.g. family changes) & macroeconomic forces (e.g. wage levels, unemployment). Improving workforce attachment and stabilizing incomes  key to reducing long-term poverty
Problems: Low wages among bottom earners; high regional unemployment rates; poor job retention among low-income individuals; increased vulnerability to poverty from family structure changes (e.g., divorce); long durations in poverty reduce the odds of escape; high re-entry rates into poverty, especially for single-parent and African American households
Solutions: Boosting real wages at the 20th percentile; increasing weeks of paid employment per year; stabilizing employment during recessions to prevent re-entry into poverty; addressing family-structure vulnerabilities that lead to income shocks; reducing barriers to full-time employment for single heads of household; support programs for recently exited individuals to prevent re-entry
Implement: Support regional employers in creating guaranteed minimum hours contracts or predictable scheduling; launch a community workforce stability initiative to help low-income workers increase annual work weeks; develop emergency income-stabilization grants tied to sudden family transitions (e.g. divorce); partner with childcare networks and transportation providers to reduce obstacles to year-round employment for single parents; advocate wage supplements or employer-side incentives to raise wages at the 20th percentile; data tool to track employment spells and poverty re-entry risk among vulnerable demographics in CF; build “transition navigator” teams that assist families through disruptive life events that could lead to poverty

Title: County Health Rankings & Roadmaps 2019 – Florida
MainIdea: Health outcomes and health factors in Florida counties vary dramatically by geography/race/ethnicity, pointing to inequities in housing, income, education, & healthcare access; systemic and place-based disparities drive long-term health differences
Problems: Significant health disparities across counties; residents of color experiencing health outcomes akin to least healthy counties; children in poverty rates up to 47% in some counties; severe housing cost burdens affect 18% of households; racial disparities in homeownership; toxic housing conditions (lead, mold, smoke)
Solutions: Inclusive zoning, land trusts, tax credits for affordable housing
Implement: Coordinate county governments to expand Housing Choice Voucher access with wraparound supports

Title: Domestic Violence and Mass Shootings: A Review of Current Academic Literature
Authors: Dr. Lin Huff-Corzine, Dr. Thomas Marvell
MainIdea: Literature review of intersection of domestic violence and mass shootings, reviewing research on whether a history of domestic violence predicts mass shootings, particularly domestic mass murders (finds that while domestic abuse is common among mass shooters, the rarity of mass shootings means domestic violence history alone cannot predict them although better enforcement of domestic violence laws and firearm restrictions could reduce mass shootings)
Problems: In FL as elsewhere, high prevalence of domestic violence among mass shooters, especially in domestic mass murders;inconsistent enforcement of domestic violence laws; weak data collection on domestic abuse history and offender risk factors; loopholes in firearm access for individuals with domestic violence histories; lack of clarity in defining domestic violence and mass shootings; over-reliance broad mental health diagnoses instead of focusing on personality traits and behavioral patterns.
Solutions: Strengthen law enforcement accountability models (e.g., High Point model) to prevent escalation of DV; implement & enforce firearm restrictions for domestic abusers, including bans on high-capacity magazines; improve data systems to track domestic violence history in risk assessment; more precise risk assessment tools focused on behavioral indicators beyond criminal records
Implement: Advocate for local adoption of proactive domestic violence interventions (e.g., High Point model); collab with domestic violence prevention organizations to strengthen links between DV prevention and mass violence prevention strategies; law enforcement and court systems in THRIVE communities to improve DV case monitoring and enforcement of protective firearm laws.

Title: Advance Peace & Focused Deterrence: What are the Differences?
MainIdea: The Advance Peace approach, a public health and trauma-informed model for reducing gun violence, differs from focused deterrence by avoiding law enforcement, working 1:1 with the most lethal actors in firearm violence, and offering healing-centered care via long-term fellowships
Problems: Gun violence hyper-concentrated among a small group of high-risk individuals; structural racism, disinvestment, and trauma → cycles of violence; focused deterrence often fails to reach the highest-risk individuals and aggressive policing erodes community trust and worsens trauma; short-term funding and lack of local ownership hinder sustainability
Solutions: Advance Peace enacts daily mentoring through the 18-month Peacemaker Fellowship for highest-risk individuals; LifeMAPs to support mental health, healing, and positive development and programs led by credible messengers, separate from law enforcement; long term, locally embedded public health strategy focused on human development, not just deterrence
Implement: Pilot Advance Peace-style Peacemaker Fellowship model in CF neighborhoods with high firearm violence & fund and embed Neighborhood Change Agents (NCAs) to do street outreach, mediation, and healing support

Title: AgLanta Fresh Food Access Report (2020)
Authors: City of Atlanta Department of City Planning
MainIdea: This report is a progress tracking document for the city of Atlanta's  goal of having 85% of residents within a half-mile of fresh, affordable food by the year 2022. The report assesses disparities in access, documents progress, and issues equity-centered policy recommendations.
Problems: As of 2015, only 52% of ATL's residents were within 0.5 miles of fresh food; many (disproportionately Black, seniors, and children) lived in LILA (Low-Income Low-Access) areas.; so far geographic inequities, with some neighborhoods (especially in South and West Atlanta) seeing minimal improvements;  problems with city policy limited on-site farm food sales; worsened by the fact LILA areas have higher poverty & more limited mobility & are strongly correlated with low economic opportunity.
Solutions: Expanding neighborhood markets and allow food sales on/from farms/gardens; expand MARTA Markets to increase transit-based access to fresh food.; design  a citywide food systems master plan with a focus on equity & community involvement; urban agriculture and local producers as key infrastructure for food justice
Implement: Central Florida has many LILA zones--a group like FCI could map these zones to identify areas with low economic mobility and poor food access for intervention; CFF funding (microgrants?) for local urban farms and food hubs in becoming yearround vendors (esp policy-wise, with permitting and funding support); partnerships with transit authorities to explore mobile markets or transit station food distribution (like MARTA Markets).

Title: How Houston is trying to make philanthropy work smarter in disaster recovery
Authors: John Brannen from Kinder Institute for Urban Research
MainIdea: Greater Houston Disaster Alliance is coordinating philanthropic efforts to support disaster recovery and resilience -->  for vulnerable communities by improving fund distribution, data collection, &  strategic planning
Problems: Philanthropic disaster funding overwhelmingly prioritizes immediate relief over long term recovery and resilience; low-income communities often face delays or barriers to federal assistance; FEMA applications are complex and under-supported; many CF residents  lack financial cushion for even minor disasters; data on community needs often delayed or missing; repeated weather events are exacerbating financial/physical instability for the most vulnerable
Solutions: Develop and fund systems that direct philanthropic resources toward long-term recovery and preparedness; support immediate post-disaster needs assessments through community surveys and data science partnerships; push for better FEMA support via case managers and community navigators; create standing funds or reserve capacity for intermediate recovery assistance; invest in resiliency infrastructure targeting low-income and high-risk areas; improve communication and grantmaking alignment between government and nonprofits to reduce duplication & gaps
Implement: Outreach to in order to model/partner with the Greater Houston Disaster Alliance and Connective to co-develop localized damage assessment tools that feed into recovery grantmaking; establish a fund mechanism at CF that allows for rapid deployment of recovery dollars targeted to ZIP codes with high vulnerability; integrate disaster resilience planning into existing place-based work (e.g., home repairs, energy reliability, and emergency food access); provide grants or technical assistance to nonprofits helping with FEMA applications

Title: Orlando Task Force Final Report (2007)
Authors: City of Orlando Government / Task Force on Violent Crime
MainIdea: A citywide plan addressing a sharp rise in violent crime through coordinated prevention, law enforcement, and reintegration strategies.
Problems: Violent crimes rose significantly between 2005–2006. Youth were both primary victims and offenders. Root causes included poverty, lack of role models, weak family engagement, and limited reentry support.
Solutions:
Prevention: Media and community campaigns, after-school and mentoring programs, stronger family and literacy initiatives.
Law Enforcement: Expanded community policing, gun bounty program, recruitment incentives, and better patrol distribution.
Reintegration: Creation of HELP Centers, transitional housing, and faith-based reentry partnerships.
Implement: Partner with local agencies to expand youth engagement and mentorship programs, strengthen reentry services through community hubs, and assess which 2007 strategies remain effective. Support collaborations that link prevention and reintegration as unified safety efforts.

Title:
ADDRESSING LEGAL ISSUES IN YOUTH DIVERSION
A TOOLKIT
MainIdea: Toolkit for practical legal guidance for jurisdictions establishing or improving pre-arrest and pre-booking youth diversion programs, drawing on lessons from Los Angeles County’s Youth Diversion and Development (YDD) program
Problems: Risk of “net-widening,” where diversion programs unintentionally expand justice system involvement; lack of legal support for youth and families in diversion programs; risk of criminalization of normal youth behaviors (especially impacting girls, youth of color, and LGBT youth); inconsistent referral practices by law enforcement leading to inappropriate diversion referrals; limited accountability mechanisms to prevent misuse of diversion programs; legal ambiguity around information sharing and record sealing.
Solutions: Strong legal infrastructure w early partnerships with legal services to provide training, consultations, and direct services; data monitoring and dashboards to track referrals and flag net-widening; policies to empower CBO providers to reject inappropriate referrals and focus on diverting only cases that would otherwise go deeper into the justice system; decriminalize low level offenses and prevent unnecessary law enforcement involvement
Implement: Establish/pilot pre-arrest diversion programs modeled on YDD; facilitate connections between CBOs and legal aid groups to ensure legal support is embedded in diversion programs; advocate local policies to decriminalize low level offenses and adopt “counsel and release” practices; work with school districts in THRIVE regions to limit police involvement in minor discipline cases?

Title: UNITED ARTS Response to DeVos Institute of Arts Management’s Orange County Arts Ecology Study
MainIdea: Audit of Orange County’s arts and cultural sector to assess capacity, growth needs, community alignment, and opportunities to scale with regional population and tourism growth
Problems: Arts organizations lack capacity to match rapid population growth and tourism economy; inadequate resources and mentorship for BIPOC-led arts organizations; young, underfunded arts sector compared to peer cities; poor national marketing and visibility of local arts
Solutions: Long range 2-5 yr strategic and artistic planning capacity; arts incubators; cultural tourism via partnershps w/ Visit Orlando and Experience Kissimmee; affordable artist studios, performance spaces, and artist co-ops
Implement: Fund and launch a regional arts incubator; advocate for county/city policy that mandates use of vacant public/commercial space for arts usage and creates affordable artist live/work zones; offer multi-year general operating support grants to arts organizations tied to strategic planning milestones

Title: Much Alarm, Less Action: Foundations & Climate Change
Authors: Naomi Orensten, Katarina Malmgren, and Maria Lopez
MainIdea: The report examines philanthropy’s limited response to climate change, despite broad recognition among foundation and nonprofit leaders that it is an urgent, existential crisis. While over 70% of foundations acknowledge climate impacts, less than 2% of global philanthropic giving goes toward climate mitigation.
Problems:
Low philanthropic investment: Most funders allocate <20% of their giving to climate causes.
Inaction among non-climate funders: 79% say climate work is “not part of their mission.”
Inequitable funding: Few resources reach BIPOC-led or frontline climate justice organizations.
Minimal divestment: <25% of foundations avoid fossil fuel investments.
Solutions:
Increase climate-related grantmaking and investments (divest from fossil fuels, fund mitigation/adaptation/justice).
Strengthen policy and advocacy to address root causes.
Promote collaboration and narrative change to shift public discourse.
Encourage non-climate funders to connect climate to their missions (e.g., health, education, housing).
Implement:
Develop cross-sector partnerships linking health, housing, and climate resilience.
Support grassroots and BIPOC-led climate justice organizations.
Launch regional climate impact dashboards connecting community health and environmental data

Title: 2022–2023 Impact Report
Authors: Foundation for a Healthier West Orange
MainIdea: Recap of FHWO's direct programming, health access expansion, and civic partnerships in West Orange County
Problems: Lack of streamlined access to existing health and wellness services; insufficient physical activity among children; fragmented local partnerships across municipalities; low visibility for small community-based health programs; need for infrastructure that supports movement and health literacy; disparities in access to outdoor spaces and transit
Solutions: Expansion on projects -- wellness infrastructure like parks and boardwalks; youth movement programs embedded in schools (Westly’s Mile); use of challenges, events, and social media to drive resident engagement
Implement: Replicate HUBB Direct as a neighborhood-based navigation hub in other underserved CF zip codes (e.g. Pine Hills, Bithlo, Four Corners); fund health-themed infrastructure (walking loops, fitness pavilions, pop-up parks) in historically underinvested areas using the Ocoee mode

Title: 11 Trends in Philanthropy for 2025
Authors: Dorothy A. Johnson Center for Philanthropy
MainIdea: This article takes a look at long-championed trends that have changed or been influenced by the COVID-19 pandemic in the world of philanthropy and non-profits, and whether or not they are currently being sustained. Future projected trends, such as increased lobbying, sunrise giving, aging in-need population, Giving Days (24 hour events), and the future of volunteer management are also discussed.
Problems: Many positive COVID-19 trends that prioritized general operation support for non-profits and philanthropic organizations, such as streamlined reporting, simplified applications, and flexible funding have been reverted to pre-pandemic times. There has also been a decline of household participation in charitable giving, directly traced to federal tax code rewrites (another round expected in 2025). Ever-increasing older population warrants increased focus on non-profit services catering to the needs of that age group.
Solutions: Increased understanding of The system and lobbying- paired with better sector relations with legislators and decision-makers. + Nonprofits should also focus on advancing The wellbeing of their employees and volunteers. + Orange County community Foundation provides a platform for multiple giving days throughout The year to benefit different causes.
Implement: Highlight better/more effective lobbying techniques for non-profits and similar groups. + Encourage other counties in CF should provide a similar platform to Orange County’s GivingDay one. + Updated and potentially virtual volunteer management systems that account for new trend of intermittent volunteering instead of routine.

Title: Local Sustainability Implementation
MainIdea: Survey assessing how US cities with populations over 20K are implementing sustainability work, covering staffing, budgets, policy tools, community collaboration, and the impacts of Covid-19
Problems: Limited staffing (62% cite staff capacity as a barrier) and budgets (only 25% have dedicated sustainability funds); many conflicts with other budget priorities (90%); pandemic disruptions of program implementation (48%); lack of informational resources (43%); disconnect between sustainability rhetoric and practical resource allocation.
Solutions: High external collaboration with environmental groups and the public; increasing use of mixed strategies (policy, education, incentives); data-driven practices like GHG inventories linked to modest emissions reductions; sustainability offices can use small wins (e.g., farmers markets, sidewalks, buy local campaigns) to build momentum.

Title: Health Indicators of Sustainable Cities
MainIdea: Actions to improve urban cities and subsequent resident health are detailed here– from housing standards to pollution reduction, improvement of transportation, and prevention of urban violence.
Problems: Outdoor urban air pollution, physical inacitivity, traffic injuries, and violence are on the rise and worsening with the further urbanization.
Solutions: Improve access to health and sanitization services, food markets, and urban infrastructure. Strengthen urban institutions and provide them with the capacity to manage growth and handle emerging challenges to urban sustainability.
Implement: Advocate for the development of green spaces and sustainable urban policies and collect data/report on all indicators (urban air quality, premature mortality from cardiorespiratory disease, urban housing and health, safe energy-efficient transportation, opportunities for physical activity, and violence and security).

Title: Sustainable Operations and Resilience Action Plan
MainIdea: A framework to cut emissions, enhance resilience, and embed sustainability in county operations.
Problems: High emissions, inefficient infrastructure, water strain, heavy fuel dependence, and natural land loss.
Solutions: Reduce emissions 30% by 2030, shift to renewable energy, electrify fleet, expand green buildings, improve water efficiency, and preserve land.
Implement: Support local renewable and EV initiatives, promote sustainable procurement, expand tree canopy programs, and coordinate regional resilience tracking.

Title: Let's Talk Climate: A How-To Guide
MainIdea: Consistent conversation about climate change is crucial to take action, tackle the problems, and see progress. To start the conversation, one must meet others where theyre at, form a connection, focus on opening a two-way discussion, and remember and honor the humanity and dignity of the person you are talking to.
Problems: Climate change is such a taboo topic of conversation, making it difficult to take action, make or amend policy, or just discuss issues at hand. Meanwhile, communities are suffering and facing repercussions of these changes.
Solutions: Facilitate open, honest discussions that foster questions, open-mindedness, and learning on both sides. Allow climate change education to take a more informal, conversational approach.
Implement: Facilitate open discussions about climate change, welcome questions, encourage everyone to keep an open mind, and promote learning on both sides of the conversation. Provide safe places to have these discussions.

Title: Orange County Green PLACE Program 2023 Annual Report
MainIdea: This article lists the annual progress updates of Orange County’s PLACE program, a conservation initiative with the goal to increase the amount of protected environmentally sensitive lands to 46,000 acres by 2030. Green PLACE (Park Land Acquisition for Conservation and Environmental Protection) aims to preserve national resources, protect wildlife, and provide wildlife corridors.
Problems: -The PLACE program acreage relies heavily on partners to help obtain and maintain properties. Only 5,413 acres/23,778 total acres claimed by the program are directly controlled by PLACE. + Acreage has to be evaluated through specific government guidelines and purchase prices are often in the millions.
Solutions: -Use volunteer efforts for restoration projects, cleanup efforts, and land management activities. + Public Fundraising or seeking private donors for continued conservation efforts? Land is extremely expensive!
Implement: -Only 31 volunteer hours were recorded as assistance provided to Green PLACE. Expansion and advertising of the Green PLACE volunteer program would allow for easier maintenance and greater public attention to be brought to the effort.

Title: A New Power Grid: Building Healthy Communities at Year Five
MainIdea: BHC recaps its strengths, successess, weaknesses and failures so far in their iniative to build up public health outcomes across underserved California communities
Problems: In CF as in California: harsh school discipline practices; poor walkability and access to green spaces; insufficient civic power in underserved communities; poor alignment between local and statewide efforts; over-reliance on short-term grants; underutilization of narrative storytelling in policy work
Implement: Sshift funding structure to include more multi-year general operating grants; build coalitions to pass Health in All Policies resolutions at the city and county level; train and fund youth led organizations to co-design school wellness and discipline reform

Title: The Black Climate Mandate
MainIdea: Black communities are disproportionately polluted, affected by the climate crisis, and preyed on by degrading industries.
Problems: Black communities are disproportionately affected by the climate crisis and often face economic instability after times of forced retreat due to extreme weather events.
Solutions: State-wide assessments of the vulnerability of Black communities to environmental risks, update all policies/mitigation strategies addressing managed retreat, establish an energency water assistance in priority disaster areas, fund rehabilitation and support services
Implement: Assessments of the vulnerability of Black communities to environmental risks, advocate for proper accounting of all marginalized communities in climate decisions

Title: The Ambition Gap: From Intent to Implementation in Local Climate Action
MainIdea: This policy brief aims to address the "ambition gap" by covering the need for federal funding of local government climate action, the need for bespoke technical assistance, sustainability teams in local government, agency of members, and the importance of elected leaders.
Problems: Inequitable adaptaion and accounting of communities in the state. Disporportionate affects of climate change. Improper allocation of resources and funding in climate adaptation, disaser relief, infrastructure planning and development, etc.
Solutions: Develop GHG inventory and identify climate risks, create the climate action plan, design specific activities, integrate climate action and resource allocation, purchase specific tools or infrastructure, and execute a project task or process.
Implement: Advocate for local approaches. Engage the community throughout this process to ensure resources are allocated properly. Streamline assessment (US Community Protocol for accounting and Reporting of GHG Emissions Dash track) and enable next generation insights (USCP Plus track)

Title: Orange County Citizens Safety Task Force Report
MainIdea: The Orange County Citizens Safety Task Force was formed after the occurrence of 7 shootings within a short time span of minors under the age of 17. The group consisted of 37 highly diverse and qualified people dedicated to brainstorming solutions to the issue of gun violence in the Orange County community. After reviewing state & local data on gun violence, risk and protective factors, evidence-based programs and practices, and intervention strategies, they came up with a roadmap of 20 recommendations.
Problems: High levels of violent crime, not unique to Orange County, but still a persistent and dangerous threat. Several resident made talking points regarding community issues were made: proactivity in addressing gun violence, support organizations that provide youth with a positive outlet, and support re-entry programs.
Solutions: -Minimize ACE (Adverse Childhood Experiences) to avoid high-risk behavior. + Invest in “Credible Messengers” located within target zip codes and Positive Youth Development programs. Avoid investing in practices that have proven to be ineffective (i.e. boot camps). + Increase the use of diversion programs. + Reduce Access to illegal firearms + End the Cash Bail System (punishes the poor). + Fund community specific (trusted) programs. + Increase mental health services.
Implement: -Collaborate with and support citizen groups that advocate for Common Sense Gun Legislation at the State level. County level has more limited jurisdiction. + Improve or advocate to improve overall neighborhood conditions (broken windows theory). + Community Safety Forums in partnership with community members and stakeholders. + Encourage and promote community policing, as well as public safety workshops.

Title: Commentary- Florida’s Future Depends on Compassionate Response to Homelessness
MainIdea: This opinion article discusses the ramifications of the US Supreme Court’s eventual decision in Grants Pass v. Johnson- a case covering the legality of criminalizing homelessness. This decision will have a direct influence on Florida’s new anti-camping legislation and smaller governments’ goals in reducing homelessness.
Problems: The explosive growth in The State of Florida, lacking urgent supplies and needs required by overrun homeless shelters, and lack of general public understanding of The homeless situation are all issues of concern identified by The author.
Solutions: support local organizations that are currently working on this issue (Florida Coalition to End Homelessness, Lift Orlando, Habitat for Humanity, Florida Housing Coalition, etc) + Address The growing shortage of affordable Housing and shelter needs.
Implement: help spread public awareness and arouse compassion for The homeless and their situations. + help garner public support for compassionate, yet reasonable responses to The issue.

Title: 2018 Comprehensive Correctional Master Plan
MainIdea: Five-year "master plan" for Florida’s correctional system outlining operational priorities, inmate services, staffing strategies, rehabilitation programming, and infrastructure investment to manage incarceration needs, reentry, and community safety
Problems: High correctional officer turnover rates (low pay, poor retention strategies); overcrowded prisons with need for improved facility maintenance and ADA compliance; underutilization of rehabilitation programs and inconsistent reentry supports; high reliance on incarceration without sufficient alternatives or community integration pathways; systemic gaps in mental health services, workforce reentry preparation, and access to ID/docs upon release
Solutions:
FDOC can implement its five-part “Project Continuum”: 1. Spectrum, 2. Glacier, 3. Statewide Curriculum, 4. Compass 100, 5. Evidence-Based Programming. More assessments, standardized evidence-based programs, vocational training, and reentry pre, expansion of mental health units and substance abuse
Pro ramming. More assessments, standardized evidence-based programs, vocational training, and reentry pre, expansion of mental health units and substance abuse programming;  multi-agency partnerships to provide ID, health, and housing pre-release; expansion of work release and community corrections as alternatives to incarceration; infrastructure investments: $340M FCO plan for maintenance, ADA upgrades, and security improvements; correctional officer pay raises, hiring incentives, and shift pilot programs; oncorporate private sector strategies (e.g., JPay for technology access) and dynamic food sourcing to improve quality.
Implement: Expanded reentry pathways, especially ID acquisition, housing, and employment services--get THRIVE partners to initiate re-integration programs? Collaborative reintegration hubs linking FDC, housing, employment, and local support systems.

Title: Orange County Citizens Safety Task Force Recommendations Final 04_02_21
Authors: Orange County Citizens Safety Task Force
MainIdea: The Orange County Citizens Public Safety Task Force Prevention Committee issues recommendations for addressing those affected by violence, specifically the most at-risk (hereinafter referred to as at-promise) youth primarily between the ages of 13 and 25 residing in the following zip codes where the incidence of violent crime is highest, hereinafter referred to as the “target zip codes:” 32703, 32801, 32805, 32808, 32810, 32811, 32818, 32822 and 32839
Problems: Disproportionate arrests and incarceration of Black youth; high rates of gun violence and trauma in a collection of zip codes; underinvestment/insufficient “messenger credibility” in grassroots efforts and mental health services; gaps in neighborhood infrastructure, housing, youth programming, and communication; lack of trust in law enforcement and court systems
Solutions: Find and fund credible messengers and grassroots youth programs; cash bail reforms + increased civil citations; launch Geographic Stewards prosecution model; enforcmeent and community-police trust building
Implement: Find and create sustained partnerships with BiPOC-led orgs and credible messengers; support neighborhood infrastructure like lighting, tree canopy, clean-up, and community centers; partner w  law enforcement on trustbuilding and public forums

Title: 2020 State Energy Efficiency Scorecard– Florida
Authors: American Council for an Energy-Efficient Economy
MainIdea: This scorecard provides details on the state's efficiencies and efforts regarding utilities, transportation, build energy efficiency policies, state government-led initiatives, and appliance standards.
Problems: Florida appears to have reached a stalemate regarding its energy efficiency. Florida utility fundings for electricity and natural gas efficiency are lower than other states.
Solutions: Treat efficiency as a utility system resource. Set appliance standards. Further promote the integration of transportation and land use planning.  Make free energy evaluations more accessible
Implement: Facilitate energy efficiency trainings, evaluations, and educational programs. Advocate for the establishment of appliance standards.

Title: Orange County LEED Certification
MainIdea: This report details the requirements Orange County, FL achieved to earn LEED Gold certification, demonstrating a commitment to sustainablility and community resilience.
Problems: Some of the areas in which Orange County scored the lowest are integrative process, water efficiency, and materials and resources. Priorities are to further reduce greenhouse gas emissions, align energy management strategies across county departments, update mitigation plans to increase resilience, and protect water quality.
Solutions: Prioritize focuses on integrative processes, natural systems and ecology, transportation and land use, water efficiency, energy and greenhouse gas emissions, materials and resources, quality of life, innovation, and regional priority through community engagement, holistic city planning, policy amendments, and infrastructure improvement.
Implement: Facilitate collaboration of county departments regarding energy strategies, conduct regional surveys to inform mitigation plans, promote the use of EVs, and identify and advocate for the protection of environmentally sensitive lands.

Title:
TALKING ABOUT
YOUTH
PROBATION,
DIVERSION AND
RESTORATIVE
JUSTICE
MainIdea: Messaging toolkit for communication strategies to explain and promote diversion, restorative justice, and probation reform for youth →shifting public narratives from punishment toward rehabilitation and community-based responses, particularly in communities of color where justice system involvement is more frequent
Problems: Public perception that crime and youth crime is increasing; high levels mistrust and perceptions of the juvenile justice system as a “pipeline” to adult incarceration; strong racial disparities in youth justice involvement, exacerbated by “adultification” of youth of color; low public understanding of restorative justice, diversion, and probation reform; persistent belief that accountability equals punishment, especially for violent offenses
Solutions: Communication guidance centered on humanizing language (e.g., “youth justice” instead of “juvenile justice”)with focus on rehabilitation, second chances, and community-based alternatives over punitive systems; use developmental science to explain youth behavior and capacity for change; change messaging strategies to explain restorative justice and diversion in plain language, with a focus on empathy, responsibility, and future success
Implement: Use this language in media advocacy, op-eds, and public testimony when pushing for youth justice reforms; use the “Advance vs Avoid” language guide to audit and revise public statements, grant applications, and communications by THRIVE partners

Title: Why Orlando, Florida is Among America’s Most Dangerous Cities
MainIdea: Violent crime in Orlando is significantly higher than national averages, particularly aggravated assault and murder.
Problems: Higher-than-average rates of violent crime in Orlando, especially during and immediately after Covid-19; Orlando ranks in top 10% of most violent US cities; aggravated assault accounts 65% of incidents; murder rate almost double the national average
Solutions: Data-informed intervention and investment in public safety and prevention?
Implement: Target community safety initiatives (e.g. non-police crisis response, youth programming) with support of neighborhood-level analysis identifying hot spots and inequities

Title: No Room for Rent: Addressing Rising Rent Prices Through Public Investment and Public Power
MainIdea: Rising rents = central driver of econ strain, inequality, inflation, w/ disproportionate imapct on low-income renters, requring large-scale public investment in social hosuing, regulatory reforms, and rent stabilization
Problems: Rent increases outpacing income, rising eviction risk/homelessness; shortage of affordable units, restrictive xoning limiting multifamily development; historic segregation reinfirced
Solutions: Increase in publicly funded affordable social housing units; zoning reform for  allow multifamily housing; implementation of rent control/rent stabilization policies; reinvestment in public housing by repealing federal restrictions like  Faircloth

Title: 2020 Arts Vibrancy Data and Guide
Authors: SMU National Center for Arts Research
MainIdea: Quantitative analysis of arts vibrancy (art providers, arts dollars, government support) specific to Orange County FL, showing high performance w/ strong demand arts dollars) and supply (arts providers) albeit less government support.
Problems: Government support for the arts in Orange County relatively low despite strong demand and supply; arts funding unevenly distrib by ZIP code
Solutions: Target arts investments and programming in ZIP codes w below-average arts vibrancy; improve access to government arts funding and expand local funding tracking; engage communities w low socio-economic indicators through arts initiatives
Implement: ZIP code targeted grant fund/mini grants program targeting areas w below-average arts vibrancy

Title: Leah Thomas harnesses Instagram to promote a vision of anti-racist environmentalism
MainIdea: Explores the role of social media in the promotion of intersectional environmental and the concept itself. BIPOC communities often face the brunt of the climate crisis but also recieve the least support.
Problems: Environmentalism is often closely entwined with racism and must encompass all communities to ensure environmental justice for all.
Solutions: Place a focus on the communities being affected by the environmental issues and the related policies/changes.
Implement: Amplify diverse voices and support environmental organisations belonging to and representing diverse communities/populations.

Title: Orange County Citizens Safety Task Force – A Vision for Our Community
Authors: Orange County Citizens Safety Task Force
MainIdea: OC lays out a ten-year vision for a trauma-informed, structurally equitable OC that shifts investment from punishment/crisis to prevention, early childhood, and whole-community wellness rooted in NEAR science and collective action

Title: Florida Department of Corrections Annual 2017-2018 Report
MainIdea: The report outlines FDC’s operations, budget, staffing, inmate demographics, and major accomplishments. It emphasizes safety, accountability, rehabilitation, innovation, and staff development. The department highlights new programs in health, technology, and inmate reentry as part of its long-term reform efforts.
Problems:
Overcrowding and aging prison population increase healthcare costs.
High recidivism despite rehabilitative efforts.
Staffing shortages and recruitment challenges.
Infrastructure wear and hurricane-related damages.
Rising mental health and substance-use needs among inmates.
Solutions:
Expanded mental health and telehealth services.
Correctional Behavioral Health Certification and resilience training.
Inmate tablets, kiosks, and educational programs to support rehabilitation.
Facility repairs, fleet upgrades, and ADA compliance improvements.
Strategic plan (2018–2022) to modernize operations and improve safety.
Implement:
Partner with FDC to pilot community reentry programs and reduce recidivism.
Support trauma-informed training and mental health capacity building.
Develop cross-agency collaborations for rehabilitation and employment.
Advocate for sustainable infrastructure and health investments in correctional facilities.

Title: Underestimating the Challenges of Avoiding a Ghastly Future
Authors: Corey Bradshaw, Paul Ehrlich, Andrew Beattie, Gerardo Ceballos, Eileen Crist, Joan Diamond, Rodolfo Dirzo, Anne Ehrlich, John Harte, Mary Harte, Graham Pyke, Peter Raven, William J. Ripple, Frederik Saltre, Christine Turnbull, Mathis Wackernagel, Daniel Blumstein
MainIdea: This article explores three issues it believes to recieve too little attention– the large scale of changes to future environmental conditions, whether our current economy or leadership will be able to handle the predicted disasters, and the need for scientists to speak out candidly.
Problems: Loss of biodiversity and species extinction, significant population growth and food insecurity, and lack of environmental prioritization in state planning.
Solutions: Prioritize future environmental consequences in policymaking and city/state planning, strengthen family planning services, invite and communicate more openly with scientists in decision-making, properly price externalities, incentivise non-use of fossil fuels, more closely regulate markets and property acquisition.
Implement: Provide environmental education programs, support female empowerment programs and organizations, provide more of/support a platform to the scientific community, advocate for greater environmental consideration in state/city planning.

Title: America's Zero-Carbon Action Plan
MainIdea: This doc outlines strategies to build an economy built on clean energy, advanced technology, climate safety, and job and economic security.
Problems: Job and economic stability relies heavily on energy use, technology developments, and the state of the environment.
Solutions: Use energy more efficiently, switch from fossil fuel to decarbonized electricity where possible, and implement carbon capture and storage.
Implement: Promote energy efficiency standards and programs through outreach efforts, partner with local orgs like We Are Neutral for carbon offset.

Title: Ending this Place of Torment: A Framework for Transfomring the Criminal Justice Continuum
MainIdea: Reform framework called the “criminal justice continuum,” identifying interventions at the front (prevention), middle (incarceration), and back (reentry) stages to reduce mass incarceration and address systemic racial and economic disparities
Problems: In FL, as elsewhere, mass incarceration disproportionately affects communities of color; 40,000+ laws limiting housing, employment, voting, and education post incarceration; lack of access to education while incarcerated; inadequate reentry supports resulting in high recidivism rates (2/3 rearrested within 3 years); harmful pretrial practices like cash bail and overuse of juvenile detention; community level impacts of spatial concentrations of incarceration
Solutions: Eliminate mandatory minimums, decriminalize drug possession, end cash bail, invest in school discipline reforms, diversion, and holistic defense; expand Pell Grants, ban solitary confinement, incentivize high-quality education in prison; tax credits for families housing returning citizens, Medicaid access pre-release, employer incentives, decreased supervision intensity, housing and employment supports
Implement:
Pretrial reforms and diversion, especially for youth and substance-related offenses; postsecondary education collaborations for people inside Florida DOC facilities; 
connect with reentry housing and employment providers to expand wraparound services; promote participatory defense and holistic legal support models ?

Title: TRENDS IN CREATIVE PLACEMAKING ON PROBLEM PROPERTIES: 2019 National Survey Findings
MainIdea: Examination of creative placemaking case studies and their success/challenges in transforming vacant, underused properties into community assets while centering residents as leaders
Problems: Vacant/blighted properties and abandoned commercial and residential structures; low resident involvement in redevelopment; risk of displacement from speculative development; bureaucratic barriers such as zoning, permitting, and ownership transfer; lack of equitable inclusion in decisionmaking
Solutions: "activate" vacant lots into parks, gardens, or orchards; transform empty storefronts and buildings into galleries, community hubs, or co-creation spaces; organize mural and public art initiatives; support resident-led “quick-win” projects to build momentum
Implement: Conveneartists, nonprofits, city planners, developers to identify and repurpose eligible properties in Central Florida; create “Creative Placemaking Fund” to seed smallscale ($5K–$50K) resident-led projects; partner w schools and universities to engage youth in design and neighborhood transformation

Title: Orange County Arts Ecology Study 2022
MainIdea: Assesssment of of Orange County’s arts ecosystem and recommendations for more sustainable, inclusive, nationally recognized sector thru capacity building, incubation, philanthropy, education, marketing, space solutions, etc
Problems: Arts sector dominated by single flagship (Phillips Center); lack of nationally recognized institutions; scarcity of large BIPOC organizations; limited management/leadership training for arts administrators; short-term artistic planning (often less than 1 year); competition with theme parks and sports for attention; underdeveloped culture of individual giving compared to other metros; overreliance on government grants and a small group of funders; limited affordable space for artists and small groups
Solutions: Support building out of more large “flagship” institutions; incubator programs with subsidized office/rehearsal space, shared back-office services, financial/marketing consulting, and targeted grants; establish an “Arts Jobs” board for gig and full-time work; lobbying for affordable artist live/work housing; provide business and management training through partnerships (e.g., Edyth Bush Institute)' create an Orange County “Culture Pass” (integrated ArtsCard/Local Wanderer) with discounted/free entry
Implement: Support building out of more large “flagship” institutions; incubator programs with subsidized office/rehearsal space, shared back-office services, financial/marketing consulting, and targeted grants; establish an “Arts Jobs” board for gig and full-time work; lobbying for affordable artist live/work housing; provide business and management training through partnerships (e.g., Edyth Bush Institute)' create an Orange County “Culture Pass” (integrated ArtsCard/Local Wanderer) with discounted/free entry

Title: Renewables on the Rise 2020
MainIdea: Significant growth has been seen in technologies supporting clean, renewable energy across the US.
Problems: Pollution and resource depletion from the use of non-renewable energy sources.
Solutions: Incentivize and invest in electric vehicles/building electrification/energy storage/solar and wind energy and promote energy efficiency standards,
Implement: Promote energy efficiency standards and renewable energy resources through education/outreach efforts.

Title: Sustainable Development Report 2024
Authors: ​​Jeffrey D. Sachs, Guillaume Lafortune and Grayson Fuller
MainIdea: The report offers a comprehensive, data-driven evaluation of UN member states' progress on the 17 Sustainable Development Goals, and reveals that most countries are not on course to meet the 2030 targets. It emphasizes the need for urgent reforms in global finance, multilateral cooperation, and sustainable land‑use systems ahead of the upcoming UN Summit of the Future.
Problems: While not designed for regional data, the findings of the report can be relevant to central Florida. There has been limited progress on SDGs tied to equity, sustainable communities, and partnerships, which is similar to problems faced in CF.
Solutions: The report recommends strengthening partnerships and improving governance frameworks through data transparency and inclusive planning, which CF can adopt by aligning public, private, and civic initiatives.
Implement: THRIVE can leverage the SDGi framework to set measurable local targets aligned with global goals, deploy subnational data to benchmark progress, and convene cross-sector coalitions addressing equity, climate resilience, and partnerships. They can also pilot land-use initiatives informed by the FABLE food systems pathways discussed in the report.

Title: 2018 - 2022 STRATEGIC PLAN
MainIdea: FL Dept of Corrections' strategic plan or improving recruitment and retention of staff, expanding inmate/offender programming, improving communications, modernizing facilities, enhancing security, and increasing re-entry success
Problems: In FL DoC, as elsewhere, staffing shortages, high turnover rates, and inadequate employee compensation; outdated facilities and infrastructure needing repairs and modernization; limited inmate access to education, vocational, and re-entry programs; gaps in healthcare services, especially for mental health and special populations; overuse of restrictive housing; poor internal and external communications affecting transparency and accountability
Solutions: Strategic focus on workforce development including leadership training, wellness initiatives, and reduced shift hours; expansion of rehabilitative programs with a focus on evidence-based tools, individualized case management, educational access, and substance use treatment; increased focus on re-entry supports including coordination with community partners and specialty courts; infrastructure upgrades for safety, sustainability, and accessibility (ADA compliance);; invest in technology to improve security, efficiency, and healthcare delivery (e.g., telehealth, electronic records); enhanced internal/external communication strategies for transparency and stakeholder engagement; maintaining national standards (PREA, ACA, CMA)
Implement: Advocate for expanded education, healthcare, and housing programs as re-entry pathways (recruit teachers, etc.?); push for transparency in local facility conditions (especially regarding healthcare, restrictive housing, and infrastructure conditions) through partnerships with state monitoring bodies; collab with local corrections facilities in THRIVE communities to pilot improved employee wellness and retention strategies

Title: Sustainable Development Report 2024
Authors: ​​Jeffrey D. Sachs, Guillaume Lafortune and Grayson Fuller
MainIdea: The report offers a comprehensive, data-driven evaluation of UN member states' progress on the 17 Sustainable Development Goals, and reveals that most countries are not on course to meet the 2030 targets. It emphasizes the need for urgent reforms in global finance, multilateral cooperation, and sustainable land‑use systems ahead of the upcoming UN Summit of the Future.
Problems: While not designed for regional data, the findings of the report can be relevant to central Florida. There has been limited progress on SDGs tied to equity, sustainable communities, and partnerships, which is similar to problems faced in CF.

They can also pilot land-use initiatives informed by the FABLE food systems pathways discussed in the report.

Title: NeuroArts Blueprint
Authors: Aspen Institute, Johns Hopkins, The Center for Applied Neuroaesthetics
MainIdea: The NeuroArts Blueprint presents a strategic roadmap for establishing "neuroarts", which is the rigorous study of how arts-based experiences affect brain, body, and behavior, to create an evidence-based ecosystem that integrates arts, health, and science over the next five years and beyond.
Problems: While focused nationally/globally, this blueprint outlines barriers that can be relevant to Central Florida: lack of standardized protocols for arts-in-health interventions, insufficient local funding or infrastructure for arts-based programs, and gaps in interdisciplinary collaboration between artists, healthcare providers, and researchers in the region.
Solutions: The report’s solutions offer a model for Central Florida to build its own neuroarts ecosystem, encouraging training, grantmaking, and partnerships between Johns Hopkins-affiliated researchers and local cultural institutions. These solutions include standardized methodologies, capacity-building for practitioners, robust data systems, and policy support.
Implement: THRIVE can launch a regional NeuroArts pilot by convening local artists, clinicians, universities, and funders; adopting the blueprint’s standardized evaluation tools; applying for grants like the Renée Fleming Investigator Awards; and embedding arts-based interventions into community health programs with built-in measurement.

Title: Carbon inequality kills: Why curbing the excessive emissions of an elite few can create a sustainable planet for all.
Authors: Mira Alestig, Nafkote Dabi, Abha Jeurkar and Alex Maitland.
MainIdea: The report reveals that the super-rich, particularly the wealthiest 1%, are emitting disproportionately large amounts of carbon (via jets, yachts, and investments), driving climate breakdown, exacerbating inequity, and causing excess deaths, and argues for urgent policies like progressive wealth and carbon taxes to constrain extreme emissions
Problems: This report is not region-specific, but its findings resonate in Central Florida: excessive luxury travel, investment-linked emissions, and unchecked consumption by elites contribute to regional air, water, and heat inequities that disproportionately impact low-income and BIPOC communities.
Solutions: Oxfam advocates for targeted policies: carbon wealth taxes, luxury transport levies, and redirecting high-emitting investments into low-carbon assets. These are measures CF could champion at the state level to curb elite emissions and fund equitable climate resilience.
Implement: THRIVE can evaluate local data to quantify elite carbon footprints, build coalitions to advocate for local/state-level carbon taxes or jet/boat levies, create public awareness on carbon inequality’s local harms, and mobilize philanthropic funds to support equitable climate interventions in Central Florida.

Title:
Key Drivers to Improve Food Security and Health Outcomes
AN EVIDENCE REVIEW OF
FOOD BANK - HEALTH CARE PARTNERSHIPS
AND RELATED INTERVENTIONS
Authors: Institute for Hunger Research & Solutions at Foodshare
MainIdea:
This evidence review uses the Feeding America's Levels of Evidence Framework to identify the core interventions that food banks and health care systems are
engaged with as partners.

Title:
The Overdose Crisis In Central Florida: A Critical Analysis of Recent Trends
and Recommendations for
Sustaining Effective Interventions
MainIdea: This report covers the data of CF's opioid crisis; how medications like naloxone and buprenorphine have played critical roles in addressing it; and persistent gaps in data, access, and system navigation that persist
Problems: Nonfatal overdose data is inconsistent and underreported; buprenorphine prescriptions have NOT increased despite policy changes; MAT access barriers remain; youth and “opioid naive” populations lack education; fragmented mental health services
Solutions: Expansion of harm reduction strategies and naloxone availability; improved data collection for nonfatal overdoses; increased MAT access and reduced barriers to buprenorphine; youth-targeted opioid education campaigns; tech-enabled navigation tools for care coordination
Implement: Regionwide implementation of tech-based care navigation platforms; more youth-focused prevention events in partnership with schools (SM campaigns?); efforts to expand MAT access, including prescriber training and stigma reduction; collab w employers to pilot HR-focused harm reduction and response training; convene stakeholders to form the proposed Regional Opioid Commission to promote cross sector coordination and policy alignment

Title: 2022 Social Threats to Aging Well in America
MainIdea: This report explores the social barriers to senior health are--Top 3 social barriers to senior health are: economic instability, loneliness, and food insecurity
Problems: Economic instability & medical debt among seniors, rising food costs and limited access to healthy food, loneliness and isolation; transportation, tech, and access issues limiting healthcare engagement; mental health strains due to COVID, politics, and lack of support
Solutions: Grocery allowance and food delivery services; technology education and access; nonmedical companion care and friendship programs but also transportation support for medical visits; 24/7 telehealth access
Implement: Partner with local govt/grocers/food banks to offer monthly grocery stipends or pre-filled healthy food boxes to seniors; “senior buddy network” to match seniors with vetted community volunteers for weekly calls or visits or isolation reduction; more telehealth access; discounts from transit agencies and ride-share companies to offer seniors discounted or free rides to appointments

Authors: Aspen Institute, Johns Hopkins, The Center for Applied Neuroaesthetics
MainIdea: The NeuroArts Blueprint presents a strategic roadmap for establishing "neuroarts", which is the rigorous study of how arts-based experiences affect brain, body, and behavior, to create an evidence-based ecosystem that integrates arts, health, and science over the next five years and beyond.
