Title: Healthcare 3.0: Healthcare for the New Normal
MainIdea: Deloitte's "Healthcare 3.0" outlines a future-oriented model focused on building resilient, patient-centered systems for the "new normal" by harnessing digital transformation, virtual care, workforce innovation, and sustainable operations to improve access, efficiency, and health equity.
Problems: Central Florida healthcare mirrors global challenges: unequal access to virtual care and digital tools, persistent workforce shortages, clinician burnout, outdated legacy systems etc.
Solutions:
• Use insights from behavioral studies (psychology
and economics) to increase individual engagement in
health matters
• Integrate behavior change as a core component
within new healthcare delivery models
• Leverage the power of influencers and social
networks to support and drive health behaviour
change
Implement: THRIVE can collaborate with CF health systems to pilot virtual care access in underserved communities, support training programs for digital health roles, advocate for funding of interoperable IT platforms, integrate climate-ready infrastructure in local health facilities, and host forums on AI ethics and workforce well-being.

Title: 2017 Osceola County Health Profile
Authors: Health Council of East Central Florida
MainIdea: Snapshot of Osceola County’s health, demographic, and socioeconomic indicators highlighting disparities and service shortages
Problems: High poverty rates; HPSA in primary, dental, and mental health; low prenatal care access; lower immunization rates; higher infant mortality and birth rates; lower college attainment; economic dependency on small businesses with fewer than 20 employees
Solutions: Expand access to community-based health clinics and mobile care units; increase mental health workforce pipeline; fund prenatal outreach programs and maternal education
Implement: Partner with local FQHCs and mobile health providers to expand services in underserved neighborhoods; fund scholarships or loan forgiveness to attract mental health professionals to shortage areas; pilot prenatal navigator programs in high-risk zip codes??

Title: Community Health Improvement Plan 2021–2025
Authors: Florida Department of Health in Orange County
MainIdea: Strategic plan to address health disparities and improve population health outcomes in Orange County--using MAPP framework
Problems: High rates of being uninsured; child poverty (22%); lack of access to affordable food; insufficient living-wage jobs and job training; limited transportation; behavioral health service gaps; high infant mortality and chronic disease in Black community; racial health inequities in maternal health
Solutions: Improved access to care through preventive screenings and chronic disease management; increased mammography and reduced respiratory hospitalizations
Implement: Support DOH Orange County in implementation of CHIP objectives?

Title: 3-4-50: Chronic Disease in San Diego County
Authors: County of San Diego Health and Human Services Agency
MainIdea: In SD 3 preventable behaviors (poor diet, physical inactivity, tobacco use)cause four chronic diseases (cancer, heart disease/stroke, type 2 diabetes, and pulmonary disease) that account for over 50% of deaths --> San Diego County launched a ten year Health Strategy Agenda to address these through policy, systems, environmental, cultural change
Problems: High prevalence of preventable chronic disease due to modifiable risk behaviors; limited access to healthy food options; car-centric built environments discouraging physical activity; lack of community-wide policy support for smoke-free and wellness-promoting spaces; disparities in health outcomes by race
Solutions: Improved health system integration for preventive care; support smoke-free and substance-free environments; expanded chronic disease screening and early intervention services
Implement: Regional Healthy Food Access Fund to subsidize fresh food in underserved areas; develop a chronic disease prevention media campaign tailored to CF’s demographics

Title:
The Accountable Health Communities
Health-Related Social Needs Screening Tool
Authors: Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services
MainIdea: This is CMS’s complete standardized, evidence-based screening tool to assess and address health-related social needs (HRSNs) of Medicare and Medicaid beneficiaries in clinical settings--with 10 core and 8 supplemental domains related to housing, food, transportation, safety, mental health, and more
Problems: Lack of consistent, systematic screening for social needs in healthcare settings, as with dometic abuse screenings; unmet HRSNs (e.g., food insecurity, housing instability, transportation gaps) contribute to poor health outcomes and higher healthcare costs → many low-income and high-need populations in Central Florida may not have their social needs identified or addressed through traditional care pathways & clinics may lack workflows or validated tools to assess social determinants of health (SDOH); no integrated referral or follow-up mechanism for non-clinical health-related needs (e.g., stress, education, safety, financial strain).
Solutions: Get HCOs implementing the AHC HRSN tool across clinics serving Medicaid and uninsured populations & training staff on how to integrate screening into work; → develop personalized care plans and referrals to local community-based organizations and incorporate supplemental domains to align with THRIVE equity goals (e.g., employment, mental health, substance use); partner with hospitals, FQHCs, and social service agencies for follow-up systems for population health data mgmt?
Implement: Encourage and promote use of the AHC tool at partner clinics and hospitals wimplementation funding or incentives or TA for screening integration; collaborate to establish a  regional referral and follow-up system for unmet social needs; design and host trainings for clinical and CBO staff on interpreting and acting on screening results; campaign + advocate for adoption of HRSN screening as standard practice in Medicaid and safetynet settings

Title: Facts about Mental Health in Florida
MainIdea: Overview of Florida's mental health care access, challenges, and statistics
Problems: Low access to mental health care (Ranked 44th in the US); high percentage of untreated adults with mental illness ; insufficient number of mental health providers relative to population; high suicide rates in certain counties; lack of mental health funding  (ranked LAST in US for MH support?)
Solutions: Expand mental health treatment outreach programs; increase mental health provider availability, esp in underserved areas & implement county-specific suicide prevention programs

Title: Facts About Mental Health in Florida
MainIdea: Highlights Florida’s limited access to mental health care, low state funding, and high suicide rates compared to national averages.
Problems: 61.7% of adults with mental illness go untreated; Florida ranks 44th for access to care and last for per capita mental health spending; provider shortages statewide.
Solutions: Expand funding for mental health services, improve insurance access, increase provider availability, and enhance statewide suicide prevention efforts.
Implement: Advocate for increased state investment in behavioral health infrastructure; partner with local providers to expand affordable treatment and outreach, especially in underserved counties.

Title: Frequently Asked Questions on Medicaid and CHIP Coverage of Peer Support Services
Authors: Center for Medicaid and CHIP Services (CMCS)
MainIdea: This FAQ outlines federal policy on Medicaid and CHIP coverage of peer support services
Problems: Peer support services are underutilized despite proven benefits improving treatment outcomes and reducing crisis system burden; misinterpretation of federal guidelines → limited flexibility in supervision models, restricting program implementation; inadequate training and certification consistency across states → hinder workforce development and peer legitimacy; low Medicaid reimbursement rates → poor retention of peer workers and financial instability
Solutions: FL, like other states, =encouraged to expand peer support into emergency and inpatient settings; states have flexibility to define training, supervision, and provider qualifications & Medicaid and CHIP programs can include peer support services if state plans are amended accordingly

Title: Pivot to Prevention: A CYC Toolbox for School-Based Mental Health
MainIdea: High-poverty communities of color limited access to quality clinical supports; behavioral health services in schools often underfunded; stigma, lack of coordination limit service use; lack of capacity, infrastructure to integrate clinical supports; gaps in Medicaid utilization for schoolbased service
Problems: Embed Medicaid-reimbursed clinicians from community partners into schools; tiered frameworks like MTSS or PBIS for identification and support of student needs; use CYC designed and developed tools to assess readiness, build referral systems, clarify roles, and match with clinical partners?
Solutions: Co-host informational sessions with CYC for school leaders and community stakeholders; advocate for school district participation in monthly coaching calls and referral team development???

Title: Community Health Improvement Plan 2016–2019
Authors: Florida Department of Health in Orange County
MainIdea: Strategic roadmap to improve Orange County’s health outcomes in five priority areas (access to care, chronic disease, behavioral health, maternal and child health, andpartnerships)
Problems: High rates of uninsured and underinsured residents; barriers to accessing primary and preventive care; high incidence of obesity, diabetes, and heart disease; limited behavioral health providers and fragmented mental health system; teen birth rates above state average
Solutions: Expand access points for primary care through partnerships with FQHCs and mobile clinics; increase enrollment in insurance and public benefit programs?
Implement: Develop regional enrollment corps to help residents sign up for Medicaid, SNAP, and ACA coverage; sponsor chronic disease self-management workshops at libraries and community centers in partnership with FQHC

Title: Unleash data to give patients control of their healthcare information and allow that data to follow them throughout their healthcare journey.
MainIdea: A federal initiative to give patients secure, digital control of their health information and promote interoperability across healthcare systems.
Problems: Fragmented health data locked in closed systems limits coordination, efficiency, and patient empowerment.
Solutions: Expand interoperability through the Patient Access Rule, APIs like Blue Button 2.0, and data-sharing pilots that connect patients, providers, and innovators.
Implement: Partner with health systems to promote patient access portals and health data literacy; support community outreach that educates residents on managing their digital health information securely.

Title: Closing the Women’s Health Gap: A $1 Trillion Opportunity to Improve Lives and Economies
MainIdea: Report quantifying and solutionizing economic and societal benefits of closing the women's health gap
Problems: Undiagnosed or misdiagnosed chronic conditions in women (e.g., autoimmune diseases, chronic pain); underinvestment in women-specific health research and care pathways; fragmented care models that do not address life-course health for women; been lower investment in women’s health conditions relative to their prevalence.
Solutions: More funding of research of pilot programsargeting high-burden conditions in women (e.g., endometriosis, fibroids, autoimmune diseases); expand Medicaid and preventive care coverage to include non-reproductive chronic conditions affecting women
Implement: Partner with local employers and health insurers to fund a “Women’s Wellness Benefit” that covers fibroids, pelvic pain, autoimmune screenings, menopause care

Title: Improving the effectiveness of public health efforts
Authors: Matt Kasman and Ross A. Hammond
MainIdea: Brookings developed a computational simulation to find how program continuation decisions (especially removing bias toward older, entrenched programs/inertia based decisionmaking) can significantly improve the effectiveness of public health departments, especially under limited resources
Problems: Public health often continues ineffective programs due to organizational inertia, especially favoring long standing initiatives (sunk cost fallacy); programs are sometimes ended due to lack of short-term funding; internal culture, structure, and leadership practices → implementing evidence-based changes difficult and slow; mis-implementation wastes resources
Solutions: Train leaders to avoid using program longevity as a proxy for effectiveness; use evidence-based decisionmaking tools consistently when choosing which programs to sustain; improve organizational agility and impact; more ongoing evaluation and avoid sunk cost reasoning in public health program mgmt

Title: LLS Children's Initative Overview 20222
MainIdea: LLS is expanding its focus on children's health to help children thrive post-treatment as welll as in treatment
Problems: Pediatric blood cancer research is underfunded due to smaller patient populations; current therapies often→ severe long-term side effects for children; barriers to access for clinical trials and care include financial burden, lack of information, and geographical challenges; need for tailored support in navigating care, treatment, and long term survivorship.
Solutions: Oncology social workers, nurses, travel/insurance assistance, and clinical trial navigation.
Implement: Partner with LLS or similar organizations to refer pediatric patients to these trials and support systems; pilot  local pediatric patient navigator program, modeled from LLS’s model of nurses and social workers

Title: Findings from the Implementation and Outcomes Evaluations of the Kaiser Permanente Health, Housing, and Justice Initiative
MainIdea: Medical-legal partnershops (MLPs) integrated w/i Kaiser healtchare settings imporved housing stability and health outcomes by providing legal aid services to patienst at risk of eviction or experiencing housing instability
Problems: Housing instability among vulnerable pops; barriers to accessing legal aid for eviction prevention; healthcare workers lacking tools to address patients' housing-related legal needs
Solutions: MORE integrated medical-legal partnerships in healthcare settings; referral networks; local legal aid agencies; use ongoing staff consultation models (curbside consults, office hours);
Implement: Partner w/ CF legal aid orgs to embed lawyers/legal navigators within hospitals/clinics; pilot "surbside consult" models for legal aid at hospitals; advocate for state Medicaid waivers to reimburse legal interventions as part of integrated health services

Title: Local Travel Assistance
MainIdea: LLS provides $500 in financial travel assistance for blood cancer patients in treatment who need help covering travel-related costs
Problems: High travel-related costs prevent patients, especially from rural areas, from accessing specialized treatment, clinical trials, or transplants→ delay or prevent continuity of care during active treatment.
Solutions: Patients can receive $500 grants every 6 months during active treatment for approved travel expenses (transportation, lodging, car repairs, ambulance services, etc.).
Implement: Partner with LLS or replicate a Central Florida Travel Assistance Fund for families facing barriers to specialized treatment & collaborate with local transit agencies or rideshare platforms to offer subsidized cancer-care rides.

Title: Public Health 3.0: A Call to Action to Create a 21st Century Public Health Infrastructure
Authors: US Department of Health and Human Services, Office of the Assistant Secretary for Health (OASH)
MainIdea: The focus for public health in the 21st century should be health equity, social determinants of health, and multi-sector partnerships, with local health departments guiding
Problems: Underfunded local public health agencies; fragmented public health infrastructure and workforce shortages; lack of cross-sector partnerships & sustainable and flexible funding models; nadequate timely localized data ; limited accreditation and foundational infrastructure; insufficient focus on social determinants and equity
Solutions: Develop strong leadership and workforce training for PH3.0 roles; establish strategic, sustainable cross sector partnerships w clear governance; increase flexible/blended funding from federal/state/local sources; improved data systems with granular, real-time metrics and integration with clinical data; support PHAB accreditation and shared services; prioritize equity and SDOH in public health policies and programs
Implement: Support development and access to granular public health data & GIS tools, encourage accreditation and infrastructure support, embed equity metrics and social determinant measures into programs and policies(?) - EXTEND THIS ONE LEILA

Title: Community CHIEF Health Strategist Competencies
Authors: National Association of County & City Health Officials
MainIdea: Outlines core competencies and practices of Community Health Chief Strategisst (CHS) in cross-sector leadership and improving population health
Problems: Fragmented healthcare and public health systems; limited use of real-time data for policy and intervention; outdated public health organizational systems; inequities in social determinants of health
Solutions: Update communication using social media and tech tools; empower neighborhood and non-health allies in decision-making
Implement: Embed Community Chief Health Strategists within each county or health district in Central Florida with direct authority to convene cross-sector working groups; develop a real-time community health equity dashboard using open-source tools and mobile access to support neighborhood-level decisionmaking

Title: Florida kicks more kids off Medicaid than expected
MainIdea: FL removed substantially more children from Medicaidj than projected after pandemic-era continuous coverage protections ended (FL alone accounted for 14% of national decline), w/ little use of federal flexibilities and high coverage loss
Problems: Hundreds of thousands CF children may have lost Medicaid or CHIP due to lack of renewal asisstance, complicated paperwork, system errors, NOT necessarily ineligibility; families often unaware their coverage lapsed until they seek care; sharp drop in well-child visits, immunizations, behavioral health access, and school-based medical referrals; disproportionate harm to low-income families, children with chronic conditions, and non-English-speaking households; Florida opted out of all federal flexibilities meant to ease transitions and protect coverage continuity for kids
Solutions: Build up community-level enrollment and retnetion supports and simplify/automate Medicaid renewal processes for family; partner with schools, WIC clinics, and pediatricians to identify and assist eligible-but-unenrolled children; advocate for policy changes that reinstate 12-month continuous eligibility for kids; use trusted messengers (e.g., promotores, school liaisons, pastors) to educate families on coverage rights and timelines; invest in Medicaid navigators/culturally competent assisters in key zip codes
Implement: Launch CF "Don't Lose Your Coverage" campaign in English, Spanish, Creole; mobile enrollment units at food banks, WIC offices, and community events staffed by trained navigators who help families reapply in real-time; create re-enrollment toolkit

Title: Greater Jacksonville Florida Travel Fund
MainIdea: LLS's Jacksonville-area  $500 travel grant program supporting blood cancer patients in Jacksonville, FL, to help with the costs of accessing care supported 38 patients for $19,000
Problems: High cost of travel prevents patients—especially from low ncome or rural backgrounds—from accessing quality cancer care
Solutions: Expand to CF - and engage donors to fund region-specific travel grants tied to pediatric or adult cancer types
Implement: Expand Travel Program inside CF

Title: Getting to Zero Hunger by 2030: Race, Poverty, and Hunger
Authors: Marlysa D. Gamblin and Margot Nitschke
MainIdea: This briefing paper argues that ending hunger in the United States is achievable by 2030 but requires directly addressing racial inequality as the key driver of hunger and poverty. The authors link systemic racism in the workforce, housing, education, and family supports to intergenerational hunger, showing that racial inequities continue to shape economic outcomes across communities of color.
Problems:
Historic exclusion of Black and Latinx workers from fair-wage jobs; tipped workers still legally paid as little as $2.13/hour.
Students of color are 7× more likely to attend underfunded schools due to property tax–based funding systems.
Families of color have less access to benefits, child care, and retirement savings, leading to cyclical disadvantage.
Solutions:
Reinvest in formerly redlined neighborhoods, promote homeownership for families of color, and increase access to community amenities.
Create equitable funding models for schools, expand child nutrition programs, and provide healthier meals in low-income districts.Expand paid leave, retirement savings, and healthcare access for low-wage workers.
Implement:
Advocate for racial equity in wages and benefits across local and regional employers.
Partner with housing and education coalitions to support community reinvestment and fair funding.

Title: Obesity Report for Collier County – 2023 Annual Report
MainIdea: Report on obesity in Collier County and evaluation of  insurance coverage gaps for Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) and Diabetes Self-Management Education (DSME) -- finding nearly all major insurers deny preventive obesity services
Problems: Only 1 out of 106 insurance referrals paid for obesity services; 47% of children in the program have a family history of diabetes; families lack access to covered nutrition therapy; care pathways fragmented across agencies; limited data integration/coordination among nonprofits, schools, and providers; most insurers only cover MNT for diabetes or renal disease, not obesity prevention; provider time is spent on scheduling/admin rather than care; existing community resources under-leveraged
Solutions: Standardize medical referral forms to unlock covered services; build central intake and scheduling protocol to maximize patient throughput; engage physicians directly to write DSME/MNT referrals
Implement: Build/circulate a CF-wide "Pathway to Medical Referral Toolkit" that physicians can use to prescribe DSME/MNT for patients with obesity and family diabetes history

Title: Collective Approach to Social Determinants of Health – An Opportunity for Public-Private Partnerships
Authors: Tracey Grayzer (Impact Alamance), Stacie Saunders (Alamance County Health Department)
MainIdea: This PPT covers HCOs’ organizational shifts from isolated or merely coordinated efforts to true Collective Impact in addressing the Social Determinants of Health (SDOH). It uses Alamance County as a case study for how to improve CHOs with cross-sector collaboration, public-private partnerships, and an educational framework
Problems: In CF as elsewhere health inequities persist due to upstream social and structural conditions (poverty; inequities in education, housing); heretofore siloed efforts from organizations create inefficiencies; public health’s limited funding and cannot act alone; traditional programmatic interventions don’t address root causes; community stakeholders often lack alignment and shared metrics
Solutions: Explicitly identify and implement a “Collective Impact” model with shared outcomes and aligned strategies + backbone organization to manage and drive collaboration; employ the “Strive Together cradle-to-career framework: for systemic education changes
Implement: Establish a THRIVE-aligned backbone organization (or support an existing one) to coordinate multisector efforts as was done in Alamance County; promote use of shared measurement systems (e.g., specific cradle-to-career indicators); incorporate SDOH metrics into all Community Health Assessments

Title: Protecting Maternal Physical and Mental Health
MainIdea: This Psychology Today article highlights  physical and mental health challenges mothers face during and after pregnancy, emphasizing the disproportionate risk among women of color &  the importance of financial, healthcare, paid leave, and social support for maternal well-being
Problems: Rising US maternal mortality and severe maternal morbidity, esp among WOC; physical health risks associated with pregnancy (chronic pain, cardiovascular disease, etc); high prevalence of postpartum depression, anxiety, and “blues”; financial burdens of raising children, inadequate healthcare coverage and high costs of childbirt & lack of paid maternity/paternity leave; social isolation and insufficient social support postpartum.
Implement: Partner with local clinics/HCOs to launch maternal health navigation programs (help w access postpartum care, mental health screenings, and health insurance enrollment); funding or mini-grants to health providers to implement universal screening for postpartum depression and anxiety, w referral pipelines to community-based mental health providers; pilot a Maternal Health Basic Needs Fund(emergency cash assistance for postpartum moms for housing, food, and transportation in the first 6 months after childbirth)

Title: ACCESS Launches the Chicago Area’s First Pilot Program to Improve Care for Patients Struggling with Severe Mental Illness and Trauma
MainIdea: ACCESS launched a virtual, integrated health home pilot with aim of improbing outcomes for residents with serious mental illness by combining primary care, behavioral health, and social services
Problems: High prevalence of severe mental illness and trauma in high-poverty communities; fragmented care systems; poor access to coordinated behavioral health and primary care; compounding SDOH--housing instability, food insecurity, unemployment
Solutions: Creation of integrated health homes that virtually coordinate care among primary care, behavioral health, housing, and employment services & assign care coordinators/bhvrl health specialists to each patient
Implement: Explore partnership models between primary care providers and behavioral health orgs for integrated care homes in Central Florida? Exploration of virtual coordination infra

Title: 2017 Orange County Health Profile
Authors: Health Council of East Central Florida
MainIdea: Orange County review with data demontrating strong population diversity and relatively high educational attainment, but w/ racial health disparities, high uninsured rates in non-elderly, & widespread designation as a Health Professional Shortage Area
Problems: Poverty, high repeat birth teen-birth rates, high non-elderly uninsured rates, disproportionately high diabetes, stroke, and Alzheimer’s mortality in certain populations
Solutions: Expand Medicaid outreach and enrollment efforts to close insurance gap for 18–64 population; addres Health Professional Shortage Areas (HPSAs)

Title: LLS Children’s Initiative Donor Impact Report
Authors: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS), Gwen Nichols, MD, Julie Guillot, Louis J. DeGennaro, PhD
MainIdea: LLS recommends increased research, clinical trials, advocacy, survivorship support, and education to improve outcomes for children with blood cancer
Problems: Universal (not just in CF) lack of child-specific cancer therapies, high toxicity of existing treatments causing long-term side effects (e.g., joint stiffness, hearing loss), limited access to trials and targeted therapies for relapsed pediatric leukemia, nadequate data sharing across institutions, financial &educational barriers for survivors, uneven state Medicaid support for trial-related care, rleatively low national investment in survivorship research
Solutions: Models that could be implemented: launch of PedAL global pediatric master clinical trial (multi-site, multi-country); advocacy secured $80M in NIH federal childhood cancer research funding; developed INTERACT and GEARBOx data tools for clinical trial access and integration; funded $100M+ in pediatric cancer research, including $52M+ in multi-year grants; ushed for state Medicaid trial coverage; LLS scholarships for survivors up to age 25
Implement: Survivorship  scholarship fund in Central Florida aligned with PedAL; partner with regional hospitals to connect children with relapsed leukemia to GEARBOx for trial matching;education and training series for CF pediatricians and oncologists about PedAL and LLS’s tools

Title: National Strategy to Improve Maternal Mental Health Care
Authors: Task Force on Maternal Mental Health
MainIdea: USDHHS steered this task force on the maternal mental health crisis and laid out a vision for a five-pillar framework: 1) build out federal policy infrastrutcure and integrated care models to priorititie perinatal mental health; 2) mprove access and equity (i.e. expand affordable and truama-informed workforce and services); 3) flesh out data systems and evidentiary research; 4) promote prvention strtageis and elevate mental-health and substance-use education with communtiies; and 5) lift up lived experiences
Problems: Problems include lack of integrated mental/bheavioral health and maternal care treatment (siloing, especially for uninsured or Medicaid-using populations); FL has not adopted 12-month postpartum Medicaid coverage; perinatal mental health workforce shortage and in rural counties lack of maternal health options; effectivecare like therapy and peer support may not be reimbursed or may be prohibitively expensive (lack of insurance, transportation, childcare as barriers); high stigma and low awareness; fragmented data and referral pathways; lack of support and funding for nonclinical roles (e.g., doulas and CHWs)
Implement: Launch a regional pilot that embeds licensed maternal mental health clinicians directly in OB/GYN and FQHC settings, prioritizing high-need zip codes, and ensure care coordination with pediatric, doula, and SUD services;  create a Central Florida Perinatal Mental Health Workforce Fellowship, offering tuition, mentorship, and placement support for specifically bilingual doulas, CHWs, peer supporters, and therapists to address disparities; build and maintain a publicly accessible maternal mental health dashboard

Title: SAMHSA’s Concept of Trauma and Guidance for a Trauma-Informed Approach
MainIdea: Outlines SAMHSA’s trauma-informed framework to improve veteran services and avoid re-traumatization.
Problems: Homeless veterans often experience trauma that affects mental, physical, and social well-being; organizations may unintentionally re-traumatize clients.
Solutions: Implement trauma-informed approaches guided by the “four R’s” and six key principles; apply them across all organizational domains including leadership, policy, training, and environment.
Implement: Train service providers in trauma-informed care; integrate SAMHSA’s principles into veteran housing, healthcare, and outreach programs; ensure environments promote safety, trust, and empowerment.

Title: PCAN. A model of success
MainIdea: Primary Care Access Network (PCAN) of Orange County, Florida,  provides a model for serving the underinsured through a network of clinics, FQHCs, and volunteers, PCAN reduces ER overuse, lowers healthcare costs, and improves health outcomes.
Problems: As of 1999, 175,000+ uninsured residents in Orange County overwhelmed ERs due to lack of accessible primary care, causing 2 ERs to shut down → founding of PCAN in 2000; over 80% of uninsured residents were employed but lacked insurance and had no access to affordable care, lack of centralized coordination across safety net providers, high per patient government healthcare cost ($2000/patient in 2000), & disconnected services and overreliance on emergency care -- problems that plague communities today, too
Solutions: PCAN was created FOR Central Florida specifically Orange County as a public-private partnership leveraging FQHCs as medical homes for the uninsured; the model provided access to primary, specialty, dental, pharmacy, behavioral, and urgent care via 11 clinics and volunteer providers; implemented centralized secondary care clinic and shared EMR systems; used County funding to leverage federal and state dollars, plus in-kind donations; developed a sliding fee scale and diverse funding streams (Medicaid/Medicare, grants, volunteer donations)
Implement: Connect with PCAN staff and founders to gain insights (listening sessions) for how to implement and elessons learned → implement PCAN’s model and replicate a coordinated care network in Central Florida’s non-Orange County uninsured care landscape, especially with 8-step PCAN framework to convene partners and create shared governance for local safety-net providers; CFF can fund a pilot to connect FQHCs, volunteer clinics, and hospitals using shared EMRs and referral protocols & advocate for county-level funding to leverage federal/state sources and expand service access

Title: Florida's Child Well-Being Report Card Shows Increase in Mental Distress
MainIdea: FL's child mental health needs are rising, with limited improvement in overall child wellbeing; advocates urge investment in school based mental health resources and mobile response
Problems: FL 35th in child wellbeing nationaly/ 20% increase in youth ental distress between 2016 and 2020; one in five children live in poverty; insufficient access to counselors and school psychologists
Solutions: Invest in accessible, community-connected behavioral health supports for youth
Implement: Assess current reach and responsiveness of Mobile Response Teams and expand coverage where gaps exist; work with 988 call centers to ensure warm handoffs to child-serving providers; partnerships between school districts, behavioral health orgs, and state funding agencies to create scalable interventions

Title: Health Equity Principles for State and Local Leaders in Responding to, Reopening, and Recovering from COVID-19
MainIdea: Outlines five guiding principles for achieving health equity in COVID-19 recovery efforts, emphasizing that health, economic, and racial justice are interconnected. Encourages states and localities to center equity in pandemic response and long-term resilience planning.
Problems:
Structural inequities worsened pandemic outcomes for people of color, low-income workers, and those in congregate settings.
Lack of disaggregated data limited understanding of disparities.
Exclusion of marginalized voices in response planning.
Insufficient funding for local public health infrastructure and workforce
Solutions:
Disaggregate and report data by race, gender, and geography.
Establish racial equity teams to guide policy and accountability.
Address policy gaps (paid leave, eviction protections, Medicaid expansion).
Invest in health and social infrastructure for long-term resilience.
Implement:
Integrate data dashboards tracking race- and income-based outcomes.
Advocate for paid sick/family leave and Medicaid expansion.
Fund digital access and workforce reinvestment for vulnerable groups.

Title: CMS Strategic Plan: Health Equity
MainIdea: Factsheet outline CMS's work embedding health equity into its ethos by implementing systemwide reforms (Medicare, Medicaid, CHIP, and Marketplace programs) to reduce disparities
Problems: Gaps in healthcare access and outcomes for racial/ethnic minorities, lowincome individuals, LGBT+ communities, people with disabilities, and rural residents; lack of culturally and linguistically appropriate care; underutilization of coverage options; limited integration of social needs data
Solutions: Expand culturally and linguistically tailored outreach and enrollment in Medicaid/CHIP/Marketplace; incentivize providers to serve underserved populations through payments and bonuses; expand Graduate Medical Education slots and telehealth in rural areas
Implement: Replicate CMS's equity-driven payment incentives that reward care for underserved communtiies; fund local navigators to enroll residents in Markteplace plans and Medicaid using multilingual materials, especially post-Medicaid unwinding

Title: Healthcare Recruiting Trends
MainIdea: Healthcare recruitment has shifted to emphasize quality of hire and retention, driven by pandemic-era pressures, candidate expectations, new recruiting tech--must modernize processes
Problems: Recruiting stress and burnout among healthcare HR; increased difficulty filling roles postpandemic; misperceptions about AI leading to underutilization; lack of internal mobility systems;; slow time-to-fill for clinical roles; candidate concerns about workplace safety and flexibility; ineffective sourcing strategies; underdeveloped recruitment marketing; weak technological adoption.
Solutions: Shift focus from time to hire to quality of hire and retention; use video and text platforms to streamline communication; increase recruitment marketing spend and presence on platforms like LinkedIn; implement structured internal mobility programs;  train recruiters on AI use to increase efficiency; address recruiter burnout through resource support

Title: 2018 Seminole County Health Profile
Authors: Health Council of East Central Florida
MainIdea: Seminole County data shows strong income and educational indicators compared to Florida overall, but  deep service gaps for low-income populations, high uninsured rates among non-elderly, & key racial/ethnic health disparities in maternal and chronic health outcomes
Problems: High poverty, high teen birth and repeat teen birth rates, HPSA
Solutions: Strengthen maternal health supports via doula access, repeat teen birth prevention, and early prenatal engagement; target small employers for health literacy, enrollment assistance

Title: 2013 Community Health Needs Assessment – Florida Hospital East Orlando
MainIdea: CHNA IDing 14 key health priorities for Orange County and selecting obesity and diabetes as focus areas for Florida Hospital East Orlando's implementation strategy
Problems: High prevalence of obesity and diabetes; elevated rates of heart disease, cancer, chronic conditions; poor chronic disease self-management; limited access to care in low-income neighborhoods; gaps in care coordination; underutilization of public transportation; high rates of single-parent households and crime; disparities in health literacy
Solutions: Targeted chronic disease education programs focusing on nutrition, exercise, and personal accountability; prevention and management of obesity and diabetes; support partnerships with FQHCs, CBOs; INCREASE ACCESS TO AFTER HOURS CLINICS AND HEALTH NAVIGATION

Title: Medicaid Postpartum Tracker
MainIdea: This doc tracks and maps each state's status in adopting the federal option to extend Medicaid postpartum coverage to 12 months--a critical policy to improve maternal health equity and reduce coverage churn after birth.
Problems: FL's eligibility limit for pregnancy is 196%; ACA extension has not been adopted,  and the status of state action is 12-month expansion has been granted. Medicaid historically only covers pregnancy for 60 days postpartum, leaving many low-income mother without care during a vulnerable period; coverage gaps → worsened racial disparities in maternal mortality, especially in non-expansion states like FL → lack of coverage = missed opportunities to treat postpartum depression, chronic conditions, and preventable deaths.
Solutions: Reduce postpartum coverage churn and support better maternal health outcomes
Implement: Partner with THRIVE collaborators to ensure all eligible postpartum indvls are auto-enrolled or retained in coverage for 12 months + community-based outreach campaigns targeting newly postpartum people to inform them about their coverage rights and connect them with care

Title: Central Florida Cares Health System: Behavioral Health Needs Assessment
Authors: Central Florida Cares Health System, Inc.
MainIdea: This regional assessment identifies behavioral health needs, service gaps, and system capacities across Brevard, Orange, Osceola, and Seminole counties. It analyzes demographics, service utilization, client outcomes, and barriers to access, highlighting the urgent need for coordinated, recovery-oriented behavioral health care in Central Florida.
Problems:
Over 75,000 adults with serious mental illness and 26,900 emotionally disturbed youth in Central Florida.
Shortages of behavioral health professionals: counselors, psychiatrists, and peer specialists.
Limited transportation, insurance coverage, and telehealth infrastructure.
Lack of awareness of behavioral health services. Only 15% of stakeholders rated public awareness as good or excellent.
Solutions:
Invest in Peer Support Specialist (PSS) programs and workforce development.
Strengthen Recovery-Oriented Systems of Care (ROSC) with measurable quality improvement goals.
Develop preservation strategies for service continuity in high-need counties (especially Osceola and Orange).
Implement:
Build partnerships with local managing entities to integrate behavioral, physical, and social health data.
Advocate for state-level funding to increase behavioral health provider capacity and peer workforce certification.
Convene public awareness campaigns around early intervention and destigmatization.

Title: COVID Recession: Projected Mental Health and Substance Use Impacts
MainIdea: MMHPI projected major increases in suicides, overdose deaths, and substance use disorders from pandemic-driven economic recession and prolonged stress, esp in Texas
Problems: Increased unemployment tied to spikes in suicide and overdose deaths; stress and trauma from sustained economic hardship; lack of preparedness for post-disaster mental health spikes; economic downturn projected to increase morbidity and mortality
Solutions: Prepare scalanle MHSUD respondses plus plan for a 60–90 day lag between stressor exposure and mental health crises
Implement: N/A

Title: 2023 March of Dimes Report Card – Florida
MainIdea: FL's preterm health statistics are quite poor, and the March of Dimes names several improvements that could save lives and improve outcomes - like medicaid expansion
Problems: Florida received a D+ grade for preterm births (10.6% in 2022); black mothers face 1.6x higher preterm birth rate and 1.8x higher infant mortality than others; 23.8% of birthing people receive inadequate prenatal care; FL has high maternal mortality (26.3 per 100,000) & high rates of low-risk cesarean births (29.1%) & very high Maternity Vulnerability Index (MVI), especially due to lack of general healthcare access. FL also lacks critical policies - it has no paid family leave, no doula reimbursement, limited Medicaid extension and expansion, and is not active in several best-practice programs (e.g., Fetal/Infant Mortality Review).
Solutions: Medicaid expansion and extension, implementing doula reimbursement, establishing or expanding Maternal Mortality Review Committees and Perinatal Quality Collaboratives; improve healthcare access in high-MVI counties; promote paid family leave legislation.
Implement: Fund pilot doula programs and gather data to support reimbursement advocacy & partner with coalitions to advocate for Medicaid postpartum extension and paid leave

Title: 2019 Central Florida Cares Health System Behavioral Health Needs Assessment
MainIdea: Central Florida Cares conducted a data-driven regional assessment of behavioral health needs across Brevard, Orange, Osceola, and Seminole Counties, focused on system gaps, equity issues, &  strategies for a Recovery-Oriented System of Care (ROSC) -- in particular, youth lack access and workforce shortages are a huge issue in FL and 75,000 adults experience serious mental illness
Problems: This document is specifically concerned with Central Florida’s condition: 75,000+ adults with serious mental illness; 26,000+ emotionally disturbed youth; gaps in insurance coverage, provider shortages, and racial disparities in access; wiat times, peer support, underfunding, limited telehealth where telehealth could be especially impactful, lack of public trasnportayion; low public awareness (and stigma against) of how to access bhvrl health services; system fragmentation, uncentralized and siloed systems that complicate patients who need multiple services (housing, substance use, MH)
Solutions: “No wrong door” approach with telehealth, system navigation, and outreach; Recovery-Oriented System of Care (ROSC) model for patient-centered, goal-driven care & surveys of consumers and stakeholders to assess service quality, access, barriers & pilot point-in-time study to identify missed services and systemic roadblocks; peer specialists, tech-enabled coordination, trauma-informed approaches, integration of 2-1-1, enhanced communication across providers
Implement: Peer specialist certification pathways funding (microgrants? background check subsidies, mentorship, placement into community orgs); develop a CF “no wrong door” toolkit for THRIVE partners to use across sectors (schools, libraries, housing agencies, etc.); regional data-sharing initiatives to reduce duplication in data entry & integrate referral systems, and improve client tracking across care levels; advocacy for Medicaid expansion and 1115 waivers that prioritize behavioral health coordination and cover services such as housing navigation and transportation

Title: Fresh Food Access Report
MainIdea: 75% of Atlanta residents were within a half mile of fresh food in 2020 with the goal of reaching 85% by 2022.
Problems: Access to (within half-mile) affordable fresh food, Low-Income Low-Access (LILA) neighborhoods
Solutions: Allow on-site sale of food at farms and gardens, support the expansion of accessible farmers markets programs (MARTA in ATL), create a Food Advisory Council for the city and develop a food systems master plan to increase access to fresh affordable food
Implement: Partner with small farms to promote or organize farmers markets, advocate for on-site sale of food at farms and gardens, create Fresh Food Access Map

Title: 2019 Community Health Assessment
Authors: Florida Department of Health in Orange County
MainIdea: CHNA covering key health outcomes disparities, problems, and SDOH in Orange County in 2019
Problems: High rates of premature death, esp among black residents; leading causes of death cardiovascular disease, cancer; widespread chronic disease and obesity; limited access to affordable healthcare and mental health services; rising substance abuse (especially fentanyl, heroin); high rates homelessness, housing insecurity
Solutions: Provide substance use recovery and harm reduction services; promote sexual health education and HIV testing; transportation to clinical appts - make more accessible?
Implement: Partner with federally qualified health centers (FQHCs) to provide mobile clinics in underserved zip code? and work with transit agencies to improve bus routes and passes for clinic visits and job access

Title: 2022 Behavioral Health Needs Assessment
MainIdea: Scope of regional behavioral health trends and service gaps in Central Florida, with focus on mental health, substance abuse, access to services, and demographic disparities across counties
Problems: Rising opioid-related deaths and fentanyl presence; inconsistent access to behavioral health services; transportation barriers; long wait times; housing assistance gaps; racial and ethnic disparities in service reach; lack of awareness of service navigation resources (211); uneven Naloxone distribution and usage; high child abuse and sexual violence rates; youth substance use and vaping prevalence; persistent poverty and uninsured rates
Solutions: Reduce wait times for behavioral health services; increase community awareness of service navigation tools like 211, expand housing assistance for those in treatment; expand mobile mental health units or community-based pop-up clinics; boost Naloxone training and distribution in underserved areas
Implement: Partner with regional EMS providers to expand Naloxone training and public access boxes in high-risk zip codes; behavioral health literacy workshops for parents and caregivers; provide grants or stipends for transportation support for treatment; distribute Naxolone ??

Title: Community Health Needs Assessment 2019 Progress Report
MainIdea: Report recapping Nemours' efforts to address pediatric health needs in CF, w/ focus on access to health services, prenatal and infant health, injury/safety through community partnerships, education, innovative care delivery models
Problems: Lack of pediatric health insurance coverage; limited appointment availability and inconvenient hours; difficulty finding physicians, esp specialists; cultural and language barriers to care; high emergency room use for non-emergency issues
Solutions: Expanded satellite clinics for specialty care + urgent care centers open daily including holidays; Nemours CareConnect telehealth for pediatric care; interpreter services (and ASL and medical interpreters); prenatal and NICU discharge education on infant CPR, shaken baby syndrome, and safe sleep; lactation consulting and milk bank partnerships; NICU cuddler program for infants including with NAS; NRP and STABLE training for providers; injury prevention programming such as car seat checks, CPR training, and school nurse conferences; sports medicine partnerships with USTA and local schools
Implement: Expanded satellite clinics for specialty care + urgent care centers open daily including holidays; Nemours CareConnect telehealth for pediatric care; interpreter services (and ASL and medical interpreters); prenatal and NICU discharge education on infant CPR, shaken baby syndrome, and safe sleep; lactation consulting and milk bank partnerships; NICU cuddler program for infants including with NAS; NRP and STABLE training for providers; injury prevention programming such as car seat checks, CPR training, and school nurse conferences; sports medicine partnerships with USTA and local schools

Title: Improving Medicare at Home for Beneficiaries and Family Caregivers
Authors: Health, Medicine & Society Working Group, Cascada Consulting
MainIdea: Medicare must evolve to support aging beneficiaries and caregivers through home- and community-based services & integrating SDOH
Problems: Overreliance on unpaid family caregivers; lack of Medicare coverage for non-medical social support services; outdated eligibility criteria (e.g., homebound rule); shortage of nonrelated professional caregivers; caregiver burnout, financial hardship; barriers to accessing benefits like SNAP and Medicaid; disconnected data and service systems; elder abuse risk due to unsupported caregiving; minimal integration with community supports.
Solutions: Expanded home care coverage; local medicare-funded local care navigation systems;
Implement: "Caregiver Navigator Hub" establishment? Shared resource directory for relevant resources, e.g.,  local SNAP, Meals on Wheels, adult day programs, mental health services

Title: The Changing Overdose Crisis in Central Florida: 2021 Data Update
MainIdea: Overdose deaths in Central Florida are at record highs, driven  by fentanyl, despite decreased opioid prescribing; urgent, localized,  equity-focused interventions  needed
Problems: Rising fatal overdose rates;  fentanyl in drug supply; high rates of polysubstance use (fentanyl mixed w cocaine, meth, etc.); disproportionate impact on Black and Hispanic populations; limited access to naloxone in high-risk communities; gack of tailored public education on overdose risk; community misconceptions about current drug risk
Solutions: Widespread naloxone distribution; community education campaigns targeting non-opioid users, families, professionals, and youth; outreach to Black and Hispanic populations with culturally relevant materials; transition support for people leaving incarceration or detox; improved prescribing practices and PDMP support; localized engagement strategies based on community demographics
Implement: Launch a bilingual, multimedia education campaign (social media, radio, school assemblies) to highlight fentanyl risks in non-opioid drugs and fake pills--establish referral protocols from jails and courts to MAT (medication-assisted treatment) providers and case manager

Title: Energy, Water and Broadband: Three Services Crucial To Health Equity
MainIdea: Acess (both affordable and reliable) to energy, water, & broadband is essential to health equity, yet marginalized communities face chronic utility insecurity that undermines health and crisis-resilience
Problems: Gaps in broadband access affecting telehealth, remote learning, and civic participation; disproportionate utility shutoffs during extreme heat/hurricanes; aging water infrastructure with lead pipes and unsafe drinking water in some low-income communities; high utility burden for Black/hispanic/rural households who spend greater share of income on energy; Central Floridians living in mobile homes or older housing often lack proper insulation or AC
Solutions: Prioritizing utility investments in underserved areas; subsidized rates for community broadband networks for low-income housholds; full, not partial, replacements of lead service lines
Implement: USE ARPA and state infrastructure funds to identify and replace lead service lines in older homes, schools, apartment complexes; pilot a community broadband co-op in digitally redlined neighborhoods using the EmpowerCLE model

Title: New study challenges scale of maternal health crisis in the US
MainIdea: CNN reports that recent study suggests that US maternal mortality may be overstated due to reliance on flawed death certificate checkbox data, though overall rates and racial disparities remain high
Problems: Inaccurate data inflates maternal mortality risk perception; postpartum deaths and racial disparities are real but under-addressed; access to high-quality postpartum care and mental health services is inconsistent in CF, especially in “maternity care deserts.”
Solutions: Improved maternal mortality surveillance methods; more accurate and targeted data collection; enhanced postpartum support and linkage to care for vulnerable mothers.
Implement: THRIVE collaborative project for management and tracking of maternal health stats?

Title: FL sees drop in national health rankings among states
MainIdea: Florida has declined in national health rankings due to high uninsured rates, limited access to care, insufficient public health funding, & increasing chronic conditions
Problems: Florida ranks 46th nationally for uninsured residents; 13% of Floridians avoid medical care due to cost; limited Medicaid coverage remains a barrier for low-income adults; mental health care access ranks 43rd in the nation; 26th in adults with multiple chronic conditions; public health funding ranks 44th
Solutions: Increased state investment in children’s health, Alzheimer’s care, cancer research, disability waivers, and senior services; addressing gaps in Medicaid coverage through policy change; expanding access to mental health services and primary care
Implement: Partner with mobilie clincis/telehealth to offer chronic care mgmt to high-need zip codes w severe PCP shortages; ommunity health pop-ups at libraries, schools, and festivals offering screenings, flu shots, insurance sign-up, and chronic disease education

Title: Nation’s Progress on Children’s Health Coverage Reverses Course
Authors: Georgetown University Center for Children and Families
MainIdea: The number and rate of uninsured children in the US increased in 2017--a troubling reversal in children's health coverage trends
Problems: Increase of 276,000 uninsured children between 2016 and 2017; national uninsured rate for children rose from 4.7% to 5%; nine states had statistically significant increases in uninsured children
Solutions: Expansion of CHIP and Medicaid + enrollment assistance programs; counter misinformation and administrative barriers to enrollment

Title: Annual Health Report 2022
MainIdea: UHP's recap of its 2022  initiatives across South FL to advance health equity through place-based strategies & resident engagement
Problems: Systemic inequities in access to healthy food, transportation, parks, and housing; lack of inclusive built environments for LGBTQ+, older adults, and people with disabilities
Solutions: Targeted interventions for lowest-ranked counties in CF (e.g. train and hire community liaisons to lead outreach and policy advocacy; implement community-led audits and action plans for food, parks, and transit; establish cross-sector coalitions focused on age-friendly, LGBT+, and disability inclusion goalsutnam, Osceola, Orange)
Implement: B-JEDI assessmnets to adapt partnered project planning; conduct audits of food environments; map built environment gaps for transit-dependent residents and advocate for micro-mobility and safe streets investments

Title: The Status of Women in Florida by County: Health & Well-Being
MainIdea: Report analyzing women's physical and mental health across Florida by county, identifying disparities by race and region--variations on violence, chronic illness, access to preventative care; FL received C- grade.
Problems: Racial disparities in chronic disease,  AIDS diagnoses; insufficient access to mental health services and culturally competent care; low preventive screening rates in rural  counties; high prevalence of sexual and intimate partner violence; elevated diabetes and heart disease rates in black women; lack of local disaggregated health outcome data to guide interventions.
Solutions: Expansion of access to preventive screenings (mammograms, Pap tests, cholesterol, HIV); increase services for survivors of IPV and stalking
Implement: TO FILL IN

Title: Integrating Primary Care Into Community Mental Health Centers: Impact on Utilization and Costs of Health Care
MainIdea: Study evaluating whether integrating primary care into community mental health centers improves access and reduces costly hospital use for individuals with serious mental illness; found increased outpatient care use at both sites and reduced inpatient admissions and costs at the more experienced integration site
Problems: Indvls w/ serious mental illness (SMI) often lack access to primary care, --> preventable emergency department visits and hospitalizations; primary care systems often unresponsive to SMI needs; integration efforts inconsistent in quality and outcomes
Solutions: Integrating primary care within community mental health centers improves access to outpatient medical services; can reduce hospitalizations and associated costs, especially in clinics with established integration models; PBHCI grants (SAMHSA) help fund this model
Implement: Pursue Medicaid or local health system partnerships to sustain models beyond PBHCI grant cycle

Title: The Status of Women in Florida by County: Poverty & Opportunity
MainIdea: Report outlining disparities and the urgent need for systemic interventions to improve economic and health outcomes for women as FL ranks last nationally in nonelderly's women's health insurance coverage
Problems: FL ranks 50th in share of nonelderly women with health insurance; only 75.3% of nonelderly women insured statewide; lack of access to health insurance increases financial strain and worsens health outcomes for women and families
Solutions: Medicaid expansion to close coverage gap? &  increase culturally competent enrollment assistance for low-coverage racial and ethnic group
Implement: Provide technical support to local governments and nonprofits seeking to improve women’s access to coverage?

Title: Addressing Healtchare Disparities
Authors: The Leukemia & Lymphoma Society (LLS), Gwen Nichols, MD, Julie Guillot, Louis J. DeGennaro, PhD
MainIdea: LLS outlines ways to address HC disparities through expanding diagnostics, education, support, survivorship care, clinical trial access, and policy advocacy
Problems: Rural and isolated communities lack access to experts/care/expert care; high out-of-pocket costs; language barriers, physician bias, and mistrust; underserved racial and ethnic groups have poorer outcomes; survivors lacking educational and financial support; low participation of underserved groups in clinical trials; complex insurance systems hinder access to treatment.
Solutions: LLS → Health Equity & Access Program launched to tackle financial/geographical/systemic disparities; IMPACT grant program expands clinical trials into underserved communities via hub-and-spoke mode; Myeloma Link expanded to 13 cities and broadened to all blood cancers; Spanish language boundary breaking; Susan Lang Pay It Forward Travel Assistance & Urgent Need funds provide direct financial support; reforms at state and federal levels based on research and lived experiences; local mentorship and survivorship programs (e.g., Kelvin Yates, Roberto) drive peer-led access and education
Implement: CF medical institutions to bring the IMPACT hub-and-spoke trial access model to rural areas; Myeloma Link-style-ish peer mentorship programs for high-risk CF populations (e.g., black men, Latinx youth); CF-based health equity fund to provide $500 stipends for urgent and travel-related cancer care (***********);  CF gaming and designing survivor support groups?

Title: Brevard County 2018 Health Profile
Authors: Health Council of East Central Florida
MainIdea: Brevard County’s 2018 health and demographic profile showing disparities in health outcomes, access to care,  socioeconomic conditions (elevated mortality rates, racial health disparities, provider shortages)
Problems: Higher uninsured rates among non-elderly adults; large share of students eligible for free/reduced lunch; HPSA
Solutions: Address workforce shortages in primary, dental, and mental health care by incentivizing providers to serve shortage areas
Implement: Target professional shortage areas (HPSAs) for primary care workforce investment and partnerships with FQHCs; collaborate with schools and community orgs to improve immunization rates;  expanded Medicaid and ACA enrollment supports in underserved areas

Title: Obesity or Overweight Now Affect 1 in 3 Youth: How Experts Are Responding
MainIdea: Childhood obesity and overweight now affect 1 in 3 US youth ages 10–17, with disparities by race, income, geography, and gender--family-centered and non-stigmatizing response for prevention and treatment recommended
Problems: 1/3 youth aged 10-17 are overweight; 55% youth don't exercise regularly; obesity leads to chronic health and mental health issues such as heart disease, diabetes, asthma, anxiety, and depression; highest rates among latino (43%) and black youth (40%)
Solutions: Fmily-centered and non-stigmatizing practices; expand access to nutritious food and exercise opportunities in low-income communities; improve school and neighborhood environments; regulate food and beverage marketing to children

Title: Quick Facts on Telehealth in Schools
MainIdea: Telehealth in school-based settings appears a promising method for expanding access to mental health services for students, especially in marginalized, low-income/underserved communities
Problems: Unequal access to youth mental health services in rural and urban low-income areas; long wait times and transportation barriers to psychiatric care; high prevalence of trauma and behavioral health issues among homeless and incarcerated youth
Solutions: Expand/implement telepsychiatry and mental health counseling in School-Based Health Centers (SBHCs); use telehealth to bring outside specialists into schools lacking on-site mental health professionals; equip SBHCs in rural and high-need urban areas with the tech infrastructure needed to deliver remote therapy, assessments, and follow-up; target telehealth services to marginalized groups (homeless youth, incarcerated youth, youth of color, rural residents); increase Medicaid reimbursement for school-based telehealth services
Implement: Build a certified network of virtual mental health providers reimbursable via Medicaid and private plans, with cultural competency training for treating LGBT+, Black, Latinx, and homeless youth; partner with public libraries and youth shelters to install private telehealth booths with Wi-Fi and tablet access for homeless teens to connect with mental health providers

Title: Radon and Your Health
Authors: CDC (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention)
MainIdea: Exposure to radon, an invisible, odorless gas that can seep into homes, is the second leading cause of lung cancer in the US. Exposure is preventable through testing and mitigation systems.
Problems: Many homes in Central Florida (especially Leon County - although in otehrmay be at risk for radon exposure due to soil composition and older home construction. Public awareness is low; many residents do not test for radon. Low-income households face barriers to remediation.
Solutions: Promote universal radon testing; subsidize mitigation systems for homes above the EPA threshold (4 pCi/L); integrate radon testing into real estate inspections and rental housing standards.
Implement: Offer free/low-cost radon test kits via THRIVE-funded partnerships with local libraries, clinics, and schools &und radon mitigation assistance grants for low-income families or tenants

Title: Live Well San Diego Community Health Improvement Plan – Implementation Overview
Authors: San Diego County Health & Human Services Agency (HHSA)
MainIdea: San Diego health strategy for policy, environmental, and systematic change
Problems: Insufficient preventive services and chronic disease prevention in underserved populations; fragmented care and limited service navigation; social isolation

Solutions: Stronger, less fragmented healthcare career pipeline from middle school to medical school; provide early exposure and mentorship in health professions; fund scholarships, certifications, and experiential learning (e.g., EMT, EKG tech);  sustainable programs supported by aligned missions and funding (e.g., George Snow Fund, FAU, T. Leroy Jefferson)
Implement: Promote sustainable funding partnerships between hospitals, FQHCs,  educational institutions; identify and replicate HCOP-style programs in underrepresented zip codes

Title: Health Care's Role In Ending Homelessness
Authors: Vanessa Davis, Beth Sandor, Meghan Arsenault, Hannah Chimowitz, Lauran Hardin
MainIdea: HC plays (or can play) transformative roles in reducing homelessness by integrating social needs screening, discharge planning, cross-sector data sharing, and case conferencing with local housing systems
Problems: Unhoused patients discharged w/o careplans or shelters; health systems unable to track/support homeless patienst post-care, low SDOH screening implementation due to lakc of staffing/buyin/workflows; high-cost ER care used by unhoused indvsl w chronic needs
Solutions: Dedicated hospital liaisons to prevent discharge into homelessness; use of medical respite and transitional care programs; partnerships between hospitals and CoCs
Implement: hospital discharge liaisons in CF health systems: Fund and embed care coordinators in major hospitals to connect unhoused patients to shelter, respite, or housing services; map existing medical respite capacity in CF and explore Medicaid 1115 waiver options or managed care partnerships to fund expansion

Title: Standards Established To Improve Health Care For Kids With Disabilities
MainIdea: Reporting on Consensus Statement by Developmental Behavioral Pediatric Research Network steering committee on 10 evidence-based standards to make health care more equitable, accessible, and safe for youth with neurodevelopmental disabilities
Problems: Inadequate provider training for autism and IDD populations among non-specialists; structural ableism and lack of accomodations; limited coordination across health and mental health systems; high risk of restraint, seclusion, and injury in clinical care; payment models that don't incentivize time-intensive personalized disability care; families navigating care alone without guidance(!!!)
Solutions: Proactively plan for medical encounters using personalized accomodations; resdsign payment and reimbursement models to support longer visits, multidisciplinary coordination, and behavioral health integration
Implement: Partner with local hospital systems and pediatric offices to implement mandatory NDD care modules as part of continuing education and licensure requirements; launch a "Family-Centered Care Navigator" rogram that provides on-call support for families before, during, and after medical visits to prepare accommodations, reduce trauma, guide coord. of services

Title: Surprise medical bills increase costs for everyone, not just for the people who get them
Authors: Erin Duffy, Erin Trish, Loren Adler
MainIdea: Surprise medical billing inflates healthcare costs for all insured individuals by giving out-of-network providers excessive bargaining power, leading to higher premiums
Problems: CF patients surprise bills from out-of-network emergency/ancillary providers; insurers face inflated payment demands; lack of transparency and choice in emergency and hospital-based care undermines competition; higher premiums for employers and individuals strain household budgets
Solutions: Ban surprise medical billing for all emergency and ancillary services; require standardized payment caps for OON providers based on in-network median rates; shift financial negotiation responsibility to hospitals for all employed or contracted physicians; use Medicare rates as a baseline to benchmark and reduce inflated commercial charges
Implement: Advocate for county/state policy to mandate hospital resp'y fro contracted provideers' billing practices; artner with insurance companies and large employers to develop a shared reimbursement framework based on median in-network rates' PUBLIC EDUCATION CAMPAIGN FOR PATIENTS ON UNDERSTANDING NETWORK STATUS AND EMERGENCY RIGHTS

MainIdea: CHNA IDing health needs and disparities across Orange County and develop a prioritized action plan focusing on increasing access
Problems: Lack of access to mental health services; high healthcare costs; inappropriate emergency department use; undocumented residents avoiding care; insufficient dental care; low health literacy; high rates of chronic conditions (diabetes obesity asthma COPD heart disease cancer high cholesterol)

Title: Mental Health and Substance Use Considerations Among Children During the COVID-19 Pandemic
Authors: Nirmita Panchal, Rabah Kamal, Cynthia Cox, Rachel Garfield, and Priya Chidambaram
MainIdea: The COVID-19 pandemic significantly worsened mental health and substance use outcomes among children and adolescents, especially for marginalized groups, reducing access to care (esp schoolbased access)

Implement: Expand access to telehealth infrastructure for low income families (e.g., broadband access, devices, private space)

Title: Collective Impact and Public Health: An Old/New Approach – Stories of Two Canadian Initiatives
Authors: National Collaborating Centre for Determinants of Health
MainIdea: Collective impact is the idea that organizations are most successful with coordination across sectors.
Problems: Organizations are limited in their potential impacts when working alone in their own sector, lacking a wide range of perspectives, skills, resources, and connections other organizations can provide.
Solutions: Encourage collective impact and the collaboration of various organizations across the state. Support existing CI efforts like those practiced by CFF!
Implement: To continue current CFF/THRIVE CI efforts, identify an influential champion to provide guidance and legitimacy, gather and identify sources for funding, and effectively community urgency and importance of the change.

Title: New Study: Health Insurance Differences Can Cost a Baby’s Life
MainIdea: This article covers a study showing that babies born to Medicaid mothers face nearly 2x infant mortality vs private insurance. Prenatal care delays, rural access, and provider shortages contribute. Medicaid expansions improve outcomes.
Problems: In CF as elsewhere delays in Medicaid prenatal care enrollment create critical gaps in first-trimester care; rural maternity care deserts, insufficient pediatric and obstetric providers; complex Medicaid bureaucracy causes delays, hard to navigate;higher uninsured rates and poverty among mothers in CF; also limited postnatal care access, especially in rural areas.
Solutions: Streamline Medicaid prenatal eligibility/enrollment processes in CF; advocate for Medicaid expansion and presumptive eligibility policies; fund mobile prenatal care and telehealth to rural/maternity care deserts?
Implement: Design & pilot home visitation programs like Michigan’s Maternal Infant Health Program? Recruit/retain rural pediatric and OB providers via partnerships and incentives; collaborate with social servics to address transportation and administrative barriers - transportation vouchers?

Title: A Fully Integrated Care Model: How Tech + a Holistic Strategy Continuously Improve Behavioral Health Patient Outcomes
MainIdea: TeamHealth’s behavioral health model, combining on-site clinicians, psychiatric nurse practitioners, technology (esp telehealth) (and cross-facility coordination) improves care quality, reduces emergency department bounce-backs, and enhances both financial and operational performance in health systems
Problems: Behavioral health services are often accessed too late (e.g., via emergency rooms) due to lack of outpatient and preventive options; poor coordination --> bounce-backs, poor outcomes, and higher costs
Solutions: Strategic use of telehealth across EDs, inpatient units, outpatient care and stramlined medication reconciliation and transitions of care processes; more APCs (nurse practitioners, psychologists)
Implement: More support for psychiatric nurse practitioner leadership in behavioral health delivery; cross-site clinical staffing models in under-resourced areas (e.g., regional networks, shared FTEs); telehealth

Title: 2025 State of Mental Health
MainIdea: This article reports on a survey of 2,000 Americans’ mental health status, the factors that influence them, and barriers to seeking care.
Problems: -Although therapy is becoming more mainstream in 2025 due to increased awareness and research, stigmas still continue to play a noticable barrier to mental health care. + Common casual factors of therapy tend to be more personal, such as anxiety, depression, stress, trauma, relationship issues, etc. + The biggest barrier to mental health care is the cost. As a result of higher costs of living and a recession, many who wish to seek care are balking.
Solutions: There is no one-size-fits-all therapy experience- encouraging people to Find what is right for them can have important effects instead of simply settling.
Implement: -COVID-19 was a big factor in the recent increased awareness and response to mental health care. Although social perception is ever evolving, continued advocacy for its importance is crucial, as well as work to make care more affordable.

Title: Preventable injuries are killing America's children. But some are more at risk than others.
MainIdea: Injury is leading COD for US children/teens, w disproportionately high rates among BIPOC, poor, and rural children; preventable injruies excacerabated by structural inequities in infrastructure, healthcare access, and public safety resources
Problems: Black children/teens in FL at higher risk for fatal and nonfatal injuries; injury disparities persist due to structural racism, poor road design, outdated vehicles, language barriers
Solutions: Improve pedestrian safety infrastructure in high-risk neighborhoods; expand trauma-informed care and violence interruption programs; improve safety infrastructure and EMS access in rural and underserved areas

Title: Women's Mental Health in Northeast Florida: Conditions, Barriers, Resources & Populations at Risk

MainIdea: Women in Northeast Florida experience high rates of trauma-related mental health conditions such as depression, substance use disorders, and PTSD, yet face complex barriers to care, especially among marginalized populations. Coordinated systems, trauma-informed care, and population-specific approaches are essential for equitable, effective treatment.
Problems: High rates untreated depression, substance abuse, trauma' fragmented and condusing MH access; shortage of psychiratrists + medicare/medicaid gaps (FL nonexpansion); over-reliance on jails and emergency rooms for behavioral health treatment
Solutions: Jacksonville System of Care Initiative: wraparound support for at-risk youth and families; LSF Managing Entity System: streamlined regional oversight and funding, data-driven improvements; integrated behavioral health in schools (Full Service Schools Plus) and primary care (collaborative care; PACE, Delores Barr Weaver PolicY Center
Implement: Fund or replicate trauma-informed, gender-specific programs for girls (PACE, Girl Matters) and women veterans; expand telepsychiatry reimbursement and infrastructure

MainIdea: CHNA IDing rising behavioral health demands across Brevard, Orange, Osceola, and Seminole counties and access barriers

Title: Telehealth in an Increasingly Virtual World (2020 Children’s Mental Health Report)
MainIdea: Telehealth's rapid expansion during the pandemic proved an effective, well-received tool for delivering pediatric mental health services--especially in rural, underserved, or marginalized communities
Problems: Limited access to mental health care for youth in rural/low-income/BIPOC communities; digital divide; PCPs often de facto MH providers; stigma
Solutions: Telehelath expansion?

Title: Orlando free clinic Grace Medical Home part of pre-diabetes study
MainIdea: Highlights a pilot program at Grace Medical Home providing education and healthy food to uninsured patients with pre-diabetes.
