Solutions: Combines CDC’s Diabetes Prevention Program with healthy food boxes and nutrition education through partnerships with AmeriCares, Feeding America, and Second Harvest Food Bank.
Implement: Support similar clinic–nonprofit partnerships that merge nutrition access with chronic disease prevention education; scale evidence-based food and health programs across Central Florida.

Title: Study highlights importance of healthy eating for children's heart health
MainIdea: Improved metabolic and cardiovascular markers are associated with early dietary patterns (i.e., healthy diets w plant-based fats, whole grains, and fish at an early age)
Problems: Poor childhood diet quality, especially low intake of whole grains, fruits, vegetables, plant-based fats, and fish, may contribute to early metabolic risks for heart disease; limited access to healthy food, nutrition education, and culturally relevant dietary guidance in Central Florida contributes to childhood health disparities; children may be exposed to diets high in sugary products and low in essential nutrients due to systemic food insecurity or poor school food environments
Solutions: Promote plant-forward diets with high intake of whole grains, plant-based fats, and fish from early childhood; reduce sugary food consumption; use metabolomic tools to track biomarkers of nutritional impact in children; educate families and schools on how early diet influences long-term cardiovascular health
Implement: Culturally tailored nutrition education workshops for parents in underserved areas in partnership with local farms and clinics; expand access to healthy school breakfasts and lunches with plant-based fats and whole grains

Title: The American Birth Industry Is Broken. Midwives Might Be the Answer.
Authors: Avital Norman Nathman, Deborah Hance Wage, MSN
MainIdea: Many aspects of the US bad outcomes for maternal care (especially vis a vis women of color) are prevenatble, and midwifery is a promising solution
Problems: Maternal mortality in FL surged during Covid-19; black women three times as likely to die from causes related to childbirth; FL has high rates of C-sections and medicalized birth; FL has many maternal care deserts
Solutions: Expand access to midwives and incentivize midwife certification and mobile midwifery clinics (e.g., modeled after Southern Birth Justice Network); launch public campaigns to destigmatize midwife-led care; advocate/push Medicaid to fully reimburse midwifery care.
Implement: Partner with midwife training programs to recruit and certify diverse providers from underserved areas (e.g., rural counties in CF); push for integration of midwife care into managed Medicaid plans?

Title: A disturbing health trend is on the rise among younger generations
MainIdea: Despite advances in medicine and awareness, younger generations (Gen X, Gen Y) are experiencing worsening physical and mental health outcomes compared to prior generations: increases in obesity, chronic inflammation, anxiety, depression
Problems: Younger residents are increasingly facing poor metabolic health indicators like high blood pressure, cholesterol, and BMI; rising mental health issues including depression and anxiety; higher substance use including smoking and alcohol; disparities exist across racial groups; health declines begin early and may remain hidden w/o early intervention
Solutions: Early screening and education for metabolic and mental heath risk factors; preventitive care & lifestyle education; efforts community-wide to reduce smoking, obesity, alcohol use

Title: Examples of Successful Community-Based Public Health Interventions by State
Authors: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
MainIdea: This document compiles evidence-based community health interventions across all U.S. states, showcasing programs that improve chronic disease prevention, physical activity, nutrition, and health equity. It highlights how modest, sustained investments in prevention yield large public health and economic returns, demonstrating that community-based approaches can significantly reduce disparities and healthcare costs.
Problems:
Rising rates of obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and tobacco use.
Persistent racial and ethnic health disparities, especially in screening and chronic disease outcomes.
Limited state capacity for sustained prevention funding.
Fragmented local health systems lacking coordination between public and private partners.
Low public awareness about preventive behaviors and environmental health factors
Solutions:
Community-level interventions addressing nutrition, physical activity, and tobacco use (e.g., Alabama diabetes programs, California’s Healthy Eating Active Communities).
Equity-driven initiatives like REACH 2010 to eliminate racial and ethnic disparities in chronic disease care.
Policy and environmental change strategies like tobacco control, walkability programs, school wellness initiatives.
Implement:
Partner with local Florida health departments to replicate proven CDC community health models.
Promote worksite wellness and youth fitness programs in collaboration with employers and schools.
Leverage federal and state grants to fund sustainable prevention programs at the local level.

Title: Grantmakers In Health: 2023 Year in Review
MainIdea: Comprehensive summary of GIH’s 2023 programs/partnerships/convenings/strategic priorities to strengthen health philanthropy across access, equity, workforce, community engagement, policy,  innovation.
Problems: Workforce shortages in healthcare; gaps in access to reproductive, mental, and rural health care; health disparities by race, geography, rurality; underinvestment in hyperlocal community organizations.

Title: The ‘Frequent Flier’ Program That Grounded a Hospital’s Soaring Costs
MainIdea: Parkland Hospital in Dallas successfully partnered with community orgs to build Parkland Center for Clinical Innovation (PCCI)--datasharing network that reduces ER overuse by addressing the social determinants of health (SDOH) & cutting costs & improving care for high-need, high-cost “frequent flier” patients
Problems: Like Dallas CF possesses a high volume of uninsured, chronically ill, and homeless “frequent flier” patients; lack of care coordination between hospitals and social services → assive ER costs driven by unaddressed SDOH (housing, food, meds); unconnected or noncommunicative agencies duplicating or missing care opportunities; fragmented services poorly located relative to geographic or demographic need (e.g., north vs. south Dallas)
Solutions: PCCI created Iris, a data platform connecting hospitals and 98+ community orgs (food, housing, etc); real-time tracking of patients’ needs and service use across sectors.; consent-based data sharing tiers; DASH pilot showed 8% ER use drop among chronic illness patients; over $12M in cost savings and improved patient stability; integrated social supports into discharge planning + triaging system to prioritize housing for most vulnerable -- all seemingly replicable?
Implement: An Iris-modeled Central FL social health data exchange to connect hospitals/shelters/food banks/clinics (something to do w FRM perhaps?); launch a DASH-style pilot in high-need neighborhoods (e.g., asthma, diabetes, homelessness) to track impact of social care interventions on ER use; design a consent-based referral system w THRIVE sector partners; mapping of “hotspots” of service gaps and redirect THRIVE grants to where need is highest (as was done w south Dallas mismatch).

Title: Tracking progress in the primary prevention of violence against women – Report Card
MainIdea: Slow but meaningful progress in preventing violence against women, especially through improved public attitudes and lower overall prevalence rates; serious disparities remain, especially for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women
Problems: Possibly similar skew in violence against women in CF as in Australia, similarly with intimate partner sexual violece
Solutions: Required education + mandatory programming for schools, employers, governments on such violence; expanded localized data collection
Implement: Work with Valencia and/or UCF to develop short-course GBV prevention training for students, staff, etc.; anonymized GBV data  dashboard

Solutions: Develop nonemergency medical transportation programs; deploy mobile clinics to address gaps in pediatric, dental, and mental health care; increase community-wide access to affordable prescriptions via voucher or discount card programs
Implement: Partner with rideshare and paratransit companies to develop low-cost medical ride systems; fund part time benefits navigators in libraries and local nonprofits to assist with insurance, SNAP, and screening enrollment

Title: Transforming the Ecosystem to Focus on Mental Well-Being

MainIdea: Pandemic-induced financial crisis for low- and middle-income households has increased housing precarity and related health risks.
Solutions: Long-term: Legal tenant protections; increased affordable housing investments; improved renter financial support like minimum wage increases and public benefits.

Title: Orlando free clinic Grace Medical Home part of pre-diabetes study
MainIdea: Americares and Feeding America pioneered a pilot program with Grace Medical Home in Orlando to execute on a CDC-backed diabetes prevention curriculum with access to healthy food; the program targets uninsured patients with pre-diabetes and has shown early success in reducing weight and improving health behaviors.
Problems: High rates of pre-diabetes (33% of adults nationally; many unaware) & food insecurity among lowincome and uninsured populations undermines ability to follow diabetes prevention guidance + lack of access to culturally competent and ongoing health education and lack of insurance + potential long-term healthcare costs if pre-diabetes goes untreated, especially among seniors and communities of color
Solutions: Engage more national organizations like Americares and Feeding America with similar sites and HCOs in Central Florida woth partnership building
Implement: Partner with local free/charitable clinics to replicate this model at various other locations, communities, and centers; fund similar clinically approved pilot programs that combine chronic disease prevention and food security and target low-income, uninsured persons; collaborate for outreach on culturally tailored curricula for diabetes prevention in high-risk communities; facilitate collaboration between health systems, food banks, and community clinics via toolkits and partnership building and networking

Title: Moving from Crisis to Hope: Leveraging Behavioral Insights to Market the 988 Helpline
MainIdea: The paper presents five behavioral insights to improve public engagement with the 988 mental health helpline, focusing on stigma reduction, message clarity, and building emotional relevance and trust
Problems: Lw awareness and understanding of 988; stigma around mental health and substance use; fear of exposure or judgment; unclear expectations around calling; lack of emotional relevance in suicide prevention messaging; distrust of national systems
Solutions: Clarify what to expect during a call to reduce fear; localize 988 branding with familiar imagery, community references, and trusted messengers
Implement: Partner with local mental health agencies to co-create a Central Florida-specific 988 outreach campaign - tailored to specific CF communities (lang, culture)

Title: Roadmap for Resilience: The California Surgeon General’s Report on Adverse Childhood Experiences, Toxic Stress, and Health
MainIdea: ACEs and toxic stress cause widespread health and social harms; prevention and intervention require cross-sector, multilevel strategies targeting primary, secondary, and tertiary prevention.
Problems: High prevalence of ACEs and toxic stress in disadvantaged populations; disproportionate impacts on marginalized racial groups, low-income families, and those with poor access to healthcare; resulting in chronic disease, mental health issues, poverty, homelessness
Solutions: Policies for poverty reduction and food security; expand family-friendly work policies including paid family leave; invest in high-quality early childhood education and home visitation programs; public education campaigns about ACEs; widespread trauma-informed training across sectors; scale ACE screening in healthcare, education, and justice systems; develop coordinated referral networks; support mental and behavioral health access; promote community stress-buffering activities like nature access and mindfulness; fund biomedical research on toxic stress
Implement: Partner with healthcare providers and FL-equiv of Medi-Cal to expand ACE screening and trauma-informed care; launch public awareness campaigns on ACEs targeting Central Florida’s vulnerable communities; create community hubs offering mindfulness programs, nature-based interventions, and physical activity opportunities

Title: Easing the stress of poverty can bring down rates of child abuse and neglect
MainIdea: A study from the American Academy of Pediatrics found child abuse and neglect rates declined following the distribution of federal child and earned income tax credits.
Problems: Poverty-related stress contributes to higher rates of child maltreatment; limited financial relief worsens family instability.
Solutions: Expand child tax credits and similar poverty-reduction policies to ease economic stress on families and reduce child abuse risk.
Implement: Advocate for data-driven poverty relief programs, collaborate with family services to identify at-risk households, and promote economic stability as a form of violence prevention.

Title: Final Impact Report – Women’s Giving Alliance Mental Health Strategic Focus
MainIdea: WGA's recap of its six six years of grant-making (2012–2018) to expand trauma-informed, gender-specific mental health services for women and girls across NEFL
Problems: High rates untreated mental illness among women and girls in NEFL; low access to services; stigma; extreme shortage of trauma-informed professionals; underfunded public systems; fragmented care; few co-located or wraparound services; long psychiatric wait times; Florida ranks 50th in per capita mental health funding; Medicaid expansion not adopted
Solutions: Stronger access to mental health services for women and girls
Implement: After-hours and 24/7 MH availability at key sites (DV shelters, homeless shelters); fund internship stipends to increase workforce pipelines through local MSW/psych programs; create regional peer learning collaboratives modeled after WGA’s annual grantee convenings

Title: White House Blueprint for Addressing the Maternal Health Crisis
MainIdea: US maternal mortality rate is the worst of any developed country in the world; the United States is in the midst of a maternal health crisis with preventable pregnancy-related deaths and huge, persistent racial disparaities. Addressing this requires a whole-of-government response plus the coordination of the whole of civil society
Problems: Manifold problems appear here that afflict CF: preventable pregnancy-related deaths, severe maternal morbidity (eclampsia,sepsis, blood tranfusions); racial and ethnic disparities in health outcomes and treatment; gaps in health-insurance coverage (specifically Medicaid ending too soon, at just 60 days postpartum), Florida being a state that hasn't expanded Medicaid; "maternity care deserts," rural obstetric unit closures, ; perinatakl mental health underdiagnossi and lack of maternal mental health crisis response infrastructure; data deficiencies and fragmentation, lack of workforce protections
Solutions: This document is for an expansive, networked-out approach to the crisis--its foremost solutions could be addressed by statewide policy changes (like Florida extending Medicaid coverage to 12 months postpartum). Other solutions include addressing maternity care deserts (fund mobile prenatal care units, midwifery centers, or telehealth hubs in care deserts. & collaborate with FQHCs, Healthy Start, and local hospitals to game-plan). Another key aspect is maternal mental health; the document advises launching a 1 24/7 maternal mental health hotline and training for CHWs for mental health screening. Also, furnishing maternity healthcare worker education to fill workforce gaps and building maternal health data infrastructure for Florida
Implement: CFF could help establish pilot programs for maternal mental health and prenatal support are available in transitional housing, DV shelters, reentry programs & mobile lactation or home-visiting supports to reach women where they are; fund maternal healthcare pipelines (e.g., CHW, midwife, and doula education scholarships and career pathway assistance); data aggregation and data infratructure & GIS map of maternity-care deserts and maternity care in general in FL; launch a Florida or Central FL maternal mental health hotline (bilingual); lend financial (grants?) or staff/partenrship/support to support mobile prenatal clinics or telehealth to get access to healtchare to underserved communtiies

Title: Telehealth and Children’s Mental Health: A Guide for Parents
MainIdea: Overview of telehealth as a treatment option for children's mental health, its benefits, challenges, and how to find providers.
Problems: Post-Covid-19 shift to telehealth; increased access but with some limitations--licensing issues, insurance coverage, technical difficulties, privacy, and difficulty building rapport in telehealth settings.

Title: Telehealth in an Increasingly Virtual World
Authors: Child Mind Institute (summary of Ipsos poll and related data)
MainIdea: Telehealth's expansion during the pandemic appears to show` strong promise for improving access to pediatric mental health services, especially in underserved communities.
Problems: Shortage of child psychiatrists in rural Central Florida;  long delays between symptom onset and care; PCPs are often untrained in mental health care; many families face barriers to digital access as well as stigma, financial obstacles, etc.
Solutions: Expand broadband access and subsidize devices in rural/low-income areas; launch school-based telehealth hubs for mental health.; establish a regional pediatric telepsychiatry network.; support Medicaid/commercial reimbursement parity; fund a regional outcomes dashboard.
Implement: Fund primary care-telepsychiatry consult programs (e.g., ECHO); advocate for statewide telehealth reimbursement parity for youth services; use THRIVE funding (mini grants program?) to incentivize broadband/digital access for low-income families tied to mental health accessl establish a regional working group to track telehealth outcomes by zip code/county/region (FCI?).

Title: COVID-19 and the Experiences of Populations at Greater Risk: Description and Top-Line Summary Data – Wave 1, Summer 2020
Authors: Katherine Grace Carman, Anita Chandra, Delia Bugliari, Christopher Nelson, and Carolyn Miller — RAND Corporation, sponsored by the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation
MainIdea:
This report summarizes Wave 1 (Summer 2020) of a longitudinal survey exploring how COVID-19 affected vulnerable U.S. populations, especially low- to moderate-income households and people of color.
The research extends RWJF’s Culture of Health framework, measuring Americans’ health views, well-being, financial strain, racial inequity perceptions, civic engagement, and trust in institutions during the early pandemic
Problems:
COVID-19, economic recession, and systemic racism disproportionately harmed low-income and minority communities
10 % experienced serious economic stress; a third expected it to last > 1 year.
Majority believed African American and Latino Americans faced greater health and financial impacts than whites.
Solutions:
Strengthen community-based supports (mental-health networks, local aid programs, food and housing security).
Integrate anti-racism frameworks into health policy (e.g., addressing structural determinants like housing and employment).
Build sustainable data tracking systems to monitor inequities and recovery trends in real time.
Implement:
Partner with RWJF and RAND to apply their Culture of Health metrics to THRIVE’s community assessments.
Establish equity listening sessions mirroring RAND’s survey questions to collect local feedback on health trust and systemic barriers.
Frame THRIVE program communications around “a culture of health and justice,” reaffirming that racism is a public-health issue.

Title: Orange County hopes to erase medical debt for thousands
MainIdea: Orange County is allocating $4.5M in pandemic relief funds to elim $424M in medical debt for quallifying residents--targeting low-income households and households w/ medical debt exceeding 5% annual income--over partnership w/ Undue Medical Debt
Problems: Medical debt --> cascading economic troubles --> housing insecurity, poor credit; many residents unaware they carry medical debt; disillusionment, distrust, or lack of info prevents proactive resolution; pandemic left many w/ lingering bills
Solutions: Use of ARPA funds to purchase/forgive debt via trusted third-party nonprofit' proactive forgiveness w/o bureaucratic application burden; negotiation directly with local hospital systems (AdventHealth, Orlando Health, etc.); collaboration with nonprofits to reach financially vulnerable populations in CF
Implement: Host medical bills navigation workshops to teach residents how to read bills/dispute charges/apply for hopsital financial assistance; support and promote CF legal aid orgs helping residents remove forgiven debts from credit reports;

Title: Getting Patient Consent for MLP Legal Services
MainIdea: Factsheet outlining consent protocols for sharing patient information between healthcare providers and Medical-Legal Partnership (MLP) staff (e.g., when verbal vs written consent needed, what is PII vs PHI, how to handle legal-health intersections like HIV status, susbtance use, mental health data, etc.)
Problems: Lack of clear standardized protocol for referring patients to legal services (fear/confusion re: consent/legal sharing rules deters staff from MLP referrals; HIPAA-sensitive issues create barriers to legal interventio
Solutions: Train medical teams on verbal/written consent procedures tailored to legal referral rules; system-wide documentation standards within electronic health records and referral platforms; provide consent training for healthcare staff; distinguish types of consent needed for various legal support scenarios; standardize information-sharing policies to ensure patient privacy while enabling social and legal care coordination
Implement: Integrate verbal and written consent scripts into electronic health record systems across Central Florida health clinics

Title: The Status of Women in Florida by County: Health & Well-Being
Authors: Julie Anderson, M.A. & Gladys McLean — Institute for Women’s Policy Research (IWPR), commissioned by the Florida Women’s Funding Alliance (FWFA)
MainIdea: Comprehensive statewide analysis of Florida women’s health outcomes, disparities, and access to care, with special attention to chronic disease, mental health, reproductive health, and caregiving. The study provides a Health & Well-Being Composite Index (based on 9 indicators) ranking Florida 35th in the U.S. (Grade C-), showing both progress and persistent inequities across counties and racial/ethnic lines
Problems:
Florida ranks 49th nationally for women’s health and well-being.
Severe racial and geographic disparities in heart disease, cancer, diabetes, and maternal health.
Black women experience far higher rates of diabetes (15.4%) and AIDS (33.2 per 100,000) than other groups.
Florida women report 4.5 days/month of poor mental health; suicide rates increasing (6.5 per 100,000)
Solutions:
Expand Medicaid and increase investment in public and preventive health.
Target chronic-disease interventions (heart, lung, breast cancer) by race and county.
Improve caregiver supports: paid family leave, paid sick days, and better funding for long-term services.
Implement:
Support maternal health and behavioral-health access initiatives statewide.
Advocate for policy reform on Medicaid expansion and paid caregiver leave.
Collaborate with hospitals and nonprofits on domestic-violence prevention and data collection.

Title: 2018-2019 Live Well San Diego Annual Impact Report: 9 Years of Healthy, Safe & Thriving Communities
Authors: County of San Diego Health and Human Services Agency
MainIdea: Live Well San Diego uses a collective impact model uniting cross-sector partners to improve health, safety, and quality of life and tracks outcomes
Problems: Gaps in health screenings and services for vulnerable populations like immigrants and refugees; youth disconnection from education and employment; affordable housing shortage; lack of sustainable, walkable infrastructure; food insecurity and inadequate community engagement; low volunteerism rates; environmental quality issues and limited access to parks
Solutions: Establish a collective impact framework modeled on Live Well San Diego engaging nonprofits, healthcare, government, businesses, and faith-based groups; create Regional Community Leadership Teams to ensure local resident voices shape initiatives; implement coordinated health screening programs targeting vulnerable populations; host summits to connect disconnected youth with education and job opportunities
Implement: Coordinate annual health screenings and mobile clinics targeting underserved communities including immigrants and refugees; hold Opportunity Summits to connect youth with education and workforce development resources; partner with local employers and housing developers to increase affordable housing stock and sustainable job creation; organize STEM fairs and family education events collaborating with local schools and universities

Title: 2019 Community Health Needs Assessment
Authors: Central Florida Community Collaborative; Strategy Solutions, Inc.
MainIdea: Regional health assessment across four Central Florida counties identifying top health concerns, disparities, and social determinants of health
Problems: High rates childhood immunization gaps; obesity across all ages; diabetes; cardiovascular disease; infant mortality; low birth weight; hypertension; substance use including alcohol, nicotine, and drugs; HIV/AIDS prevalence; limited access to preventative care
Solutions: Expanded immunizations campaigns

Title: The Status of Women in Florida: Reproductive Rights
Authors:
Robyn Watson Ellerbe, PhD ; Nina Besser Doorley, MPhil; Afet Dundar, PhD
Institute for Women’s Policy Research (IWPR) in partnership with the Florida Women’s Funding Alliance (FWFA).
MainIdea: The report evaluates Florida’s reproductive rights, healthcare access, and policy climate, giving the state a C+ on IWPR’s Reproductive Rights Index (2022). It highlights systemic inequities in access to abortion, contraception, and comprehensive sex education: issues worsened by restrictive laws and limited Medicaid expansion.
Problems:
Florida’s 15-week abortion ban and mandatory parental consent create major access barriers.
One in five counties lack abortion providers; rural and low-income women most affected.
No public funding for abortion or infertility treatment coverage.
Black women face 2× higher infant mortality; immigrant women lack access due to citizenship status.
Solutions:
Repeal restrictive abortion laws and expand Medicaid to cover reproductive care.
Increase Title X funding and ensure equitable distribution statewide.
Support community health initiatives to reduce maternal and infant mortality.
Implement:
Support data collection on racial, gender, and geographic disparities.
Partner with local clinics to improve access in underserved counties.

Title: Kids as Young as 8 Years Old Should Be Screened for Anxiety, Task Force Says
MainIdea: Reports new guidance from the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommending anxiety screening for children ages 8 and up amid rising youth mental health concerns.
Problems: Increasing anxiety and depression among children, worsened by COVID-19; many cases go undiagnosed or untreated.
Solutions: Routine anxiety screenings for ages 8+; continued monitoring, parent and provider collaboration, and more research on early intervention.
Implement: Promote early screening initiatives in schools and pediatric settings; partner with mental health professionals to expand youth assessment programs and family awareness campaigns.

Title: Florida Maternal Mortality Review Committee (FLMMRC) 2019 Update
MainIdea: The FLMMRC analyzed pregnancy-related deaths in Florida to identify preventable factors, reduce racial disparities, and improve maternal outcomes. It found many such deaths are preventable.
Problems: Large maternal mortality disparities: Non-Hispanic black women 2× more likely to die from pregnancy-related causes than white women. 76.8% of deaths occurred postpartum & 67.4% of deaths were deemed preventable. Most of these deaths linked to thrombotic embolism, hypertensive disorders, infection, cardiovascular issues, and hemorrhage. Poor provider training, fragmented communication, and weak care coordination is a serious problem, as are gaps in postpartum monitoring, autopsy data, and vital sign tracking
Solutions: Some solutions suggested herein were improvement of provider training (esp. OBs, ERs, and correctional providers); use of maternal early warning systems (MEWS), standardization of treatment protocols (FPQC toolkits), expansion of Healthy Start interconception services, promotion of preconception health, especially for chronic conditions, funding postpartum depression screening & follow-up, & encouragement  autopsies, care coordination, and shared decision-making
Implement: THRIVE could help by funding and scaling up the FPQC’s quality improvement initiatives, including the MORE (Managing Obstetric Risk Efficiently) and PROVIDE programs. Community grantmaking --> support patients with chronic conditions that increase maternal mortality risk (e.g., hypertension and cardiovascular disease) through wraparound services, care navigation, and home-based follow-up. Address care fragmentation and strengthen partnerships with Healthy Start, supporting their interconception care programming and expanding mental health navigation for postpartum families. Establushment and upkeep for statewide maternal health dashboards, better data-sharing system. Advocacy for policy changes like Medicaid expansion for postpartum coverage

Title: “Building Strong Futures: The Feasibility of Using a Targeted Digital Media Campaign to Improve Knowledge About Pregnancy and Low Birthweight Among Black Women”
Authors: Erika Bonnevie, Sarah D. Rosenberg, Jaclyn Goldbarg, Atalie Ashley‑West, Joe Smyser
MainIdea: This study evaluated the “Strong Beautiful Future” digital media campaign, which used social media to share culturally relevant information with Black women in Orange County, Florida, about healthy pregnancy behaviors to reduce low birthweight risk. It demonstrated the feasibility and promise of a targeted, culturally sensitive digital health campaign for improving knowledge and engagement among Black women.
Problems:
Black women experience double the rate of low birthweight compared to white women.
Limited access to culturally competent prenatal care and frequent distrust in healthcare systems.
Misinformation about safe weight gain, exercise, and nutrition during pregnancy.
Underrepresentation of Black women in positive, empowering maternal health media.
Solutions:
Culturally tailored digital interventions targeting specific communities.
Use of positive representation and local voices (Black prenatal providers, doulas, midwives).
Engagement through social media influencers and community-driven content.
Expand telehealth and digital outreach to supplement traditional health services.
Implement:
Collaborate with Black maternal health organizations and social influencers to co-create content.
Develop THRIVE-aligned frameworks integrating data tracking (RCI-style metrics) with engagement analytics.
Advocate for funding digital health literacy initiatives in communities with high maternal inequities.

Title: Substance Use and Mental Disorders Plan Update (March 2022)
Authors: Palm Beach County Behavioral Health, Substance Use, and Co-Occurring Disorder Steering Committee
MainIdea: Palm Beach County updated its strategy to combat substance use and mental health disorders by shifting toward a person-centered, recovery-oriented system of care (ROSC). The plan integrates prevention, treatment, and recovery services through coordinated networks, neutral care coordination, data-driven evaluation, and collaboration between public and private entities
Problems:
Poor data sharing, inconsistent outcomes, and limited coordination among health providers.
Inadequate housing, employment, and transportation support for people in recovery.
Lack of a single lead entity overseeing behavioral health and substance use systems.
Stigma and insufficient community awareness around prevention and treatment.
Solutions:
Implement Recovery Community Organizations (RCOs) and Recovery Community Centers (RCCs) for peer support.
Create a real-time data dashboard and expand use of the Recovery Capital Index (RCI) to track recovery outcomes.
Develop countywide prevention education programs and advocate for Medicaid expansion.
Expand housing, employment, and transportation resources for individuals in recovery.
Implement:
Integrate RCI-style wellness metrics into THRIVE evaluations.
Facilitate public education and stigma-reduction initiatives in schools and workplaces.

Title: Florida pediatricians concerned and 'exhausted' as kids' vaccination rate drops
MainIdea: FL's childhood vaccination rates have dropped significantly postpandemic, sparking alarm among pediatricians as vaccine hesitancy, state policies, and misinformation lead to increased disease risk and burnout among providers
Problems: Drop in kindergarten vaccination rate to 90.6%, below the 95% threshold for herd immunity; rise in religious exemptions with minimal oversight; increased spread of vaccine misinformation, including from state leadership; pediatrician exhaustion and capacity limitations; resurgence of preventable diseases; diminished trust in health institutions; state refusal to follow CDC guidance during outbreaks; underuse of available free vaccination programs
Solutions: Enforce stronger oversight and education around religious exemptions; rebuild trust in vaccines through public information campaigns; provide support to pediatric practices treating unvaccinated children; reintroduce mandatory consultation for exemptions
Implement: Coordinate with local school districts to develop opt-out counseling protocols where parents must consult a licensed health professional before religious exemption forms are processed; public messaging?

Title: Seminole County 2019 Health Profile
Authors: Health Council of East Central Florida
MainIdea: Data factsheet of health, demographics, and socioeconomic indicators in Seminole County
Problems: High rates of uninsured among non-elderly adults; high age-adjusted death rates for stroke, chronic lower respiratory diseases, and diabetes; teen birth rate and repeat teen birth rate above national averages; healthcare professional shortages
Solutions: Expand FQHC services and coverage; improve mental and dental health workforce distribution through loan forgiveness and local education pipelines
Implement: Partner with medical and dental schools to create local residency and placement programs incentivized to stay in Central Florida; more AC navigators?

Title: Project Opioid Data Update 2025
Authors: Data from CDC and U.S. Department of Commerce
MainIdea: After five years of increases, overdose deaths in the U.S. and Florida began to decline in 2023, with sharper reductions projected for 2024. The report highlights progress in Central Florida but warns that deaths remain far above pre-fentanyl levels. Sustained, coordinated action is still essential.
Problems:
Overdose deaths, though declining, remain 2–3 times higher than a decade ago.
Fentanyl continues to drive most fatalities.
County progress is uneven. Osceola County saw rising deaths while Seminole improved.
Need for sustained attention to prevention, treatment, and innovation.
Solutions:
Expand community-based prevention and harm-reduction efforts.
Target resources to counties with rising deaths (e.g., Osceola).
Maintain momentum through coordinated multi-sector partnerships.
Innovate data-driven strategies to monitor and respond quickly to trends.
Implement:
Facilitate county-level partnerships among healthcare, law enforcement, and nonprofits.
Support harm-reduction initiatives (e.g., naloxone distribution, education).
Use data dashboards to track progress and gaps.
Align THRIVE priorities with ongoing opioid response networks.

Title: Population Health Management: Meeting the Demand for Value-Based Care
Authors: NCQA (National Committee for Quality Assurance) and Health Management Associates
MainIdea: This doc explores how population health management (PHM) can support value-based care by using data/predictive analytics and personcentered interventions to improve outcomes and reduce financial costs especially for high-risk, underserved populations.
Problems: Health systems in FL as elsewhere are unprepared for full transition to value-based payment; fragmented data and siloed systems limit effectiveness of care coordination; disparities persist due to insufficient integration of social determinants of health (SDOH; lack of standardized PHM practices and weak interoperability across sectors as many providers lack training and tools to operationalize PHM in real-life settings.
Solutions: PHM framework that understands risk stratification, care coordination, and data in & integration’/ncorporation of SDOH data to tailor interventions to community needs; AI and predictive analytics to personalize outreach and resource allocation; support for provider teams through shared dashboards and targeted decision support; standardized measurement to drive accountability and continuous improvement; scaling up telehealth, remote care, &digital engagement.
Implement: THRIVE could invest in/fund data integration pilots across THRIVE partners (schools, clinics, housing orgs?) to build up shared population health dashboards for CF + SDOH dat amapping tool; launch funded fellowship careerbuilding opportunity pairing medical providers with community orgs to co-design care plans that address SDOH

Title: Hospital-Based Strategies for Creating a Culture of Health
MainIdea: The report outlines hospital-led approaches to building a “Culture of Health” — a framework emphasizing community partnerships, prevention, and social equity. It details how hospitals can expand beyond clinical care to address the social, environmental, and behavioral determinants of health.
Problems:
Limited access to care and lack of insurance.
High rates of chronic disease, obesity, and behavioral health issues.
Fragmented collaboration between hospitals and community sectors.
Insufficient measurement of outcomes beyond clinical metrics.
Solutions:
Conduct Community Health Needs Assessments (CHNAs) every three years.
Partner across sectors like schools, local government, and nonprofits to build healthy environments.
Develop leadership and financial commitment to community-centered care.
Implement:
Support cross-sector partnerships addressing root causes of poor health (housing, food, education).
Promote replicable community-based case studies  (Bon Secours, Parkview, Spartanburg).

Title:
Mainstreaming Produce Prescriptions:

A Policy Strategy Report
Authors: Center for Health Law and Policy Innovation of Harvard Law School (CHLPI); Rockefeller Foundation
MainIdea:
The document issues recommendations to provide a framework for building the system needed to mainstream produce prescriptions - "“a medical treatment or preventative service for patients who are eligible due to a diet-related health risk or condition, food insecurity or other documented challenges in access to nutritious foods, and are referred by a healthcare
provider or health insurance plan."
Problems: Lack of partnerships with food retailers in central Florida and lack of sustainable funding streams for PPPs (primary), complex privacy and data-sharing regulations in data collection,  inadequate infrastructure and support for healthcare/retailer/nonprofit participation, and absence of unified guidance and technical assistance.
Solutions: Design and implementation of PPPs in Central Florida with technical assistance and funding guidance provided to state of Florida and providers; improved tech infrastructure for PPP redemption
Implement:
Convene local Medicaid Managed Care Organizations (MCOs), hospitals, health clinics to explore pilot PPPs as “value-added services” + fund pilot projects or feasibility studies with health providers; use CFF grantmaking $ to kickstart local PPPs + provide match funding or capacity support to help local orgs apply for and manage GusNIP grants + support organizations in Central Florida to apply for future USDA funding rounds.
Create a Produce Rx Preparation Program by funding local planning grants or technical assistance for CBOs, clinics, and food access orgs to prepare for PPP implementation & support back-end infrastructure building (e.g., referral systems, data-sharing, legal reviews); partner w/ local universities or data hubs to track outcomes in marginalized communities and build tools or fund consultants to collec & analyze user experiences from PPP participant; provide microgrants or infrastructure support to local corner stores, CSAs, farmers markets, or bodegas to accept PPP benefits.

Title: Born to Die: Florida’s Infant Mortality Crisis
MainIdea: An in-depth investigation into Florida’s infant-mortality crisis, revealing racial, economic, and regional inequities that cause preventable deaths among mothers and newborns.
Problems:
Black infants die at 2× the rate of white infants.
Gaps in prenatal access, hospital coverage, and Medicaid expansion.
Rising maternal health risks from chronic conditions and delayed care.
Underfunded state programs (Healthy Start, maternal health initiatives).
Solutions:
Expand Medicaid and prenatal coverage for all mothers.
Invest in community-based maternal-health programs and home visits.
Improve racial-bias training and culturally competent care.
Strengthen data collection on infant-mortality disparities.
Implement:
Partner with Healthy Start coalitions and local health departments.
Advocate for state maternal-care funding and Medicaid expansion.

Title: Autism Report for Collier County
MainIdea: Report on how the Togetherhood Initiative applies its community-based, collaborative care model to improve early autism identification, intervention, and support
Problems: Lack of care coordination in underserved areas like Immokalee and Golden Gate; high autism prevalence and underdiagnosis in Hispanic and low-income communities; siloed healthcare and wellness services; barriers to early intervention and sustained outcomes; limited insurance coverage and navigation for autism services; lack of culturally and linguistically tailored resources; fragmentation between medical and community-based services
Solutions: Togetherhood Initiative (centralized service hub integrating screenings, therapies, education, and care navigation); shared intake system with multilingual support; builds hybrid clinic-community partnerships (e.g., YMCA, Meals of Hope); expands early autism diagnosis (ADOS testing) and therapies through Medicaid and nonprofit scholarships; collects outcome data (KPIs) to optimize strategy
Implement: MODEL/IMPLEMENT TOGETHERHOOD INITIATIVE MODEL IN CF?

Title: Community Health Needs Assessment 2020–2022 Implementation Plan
MainIdea: Assessment and implementation of strategies to improve child and adolescent health in CF
Problems: Limited access to pediatric healthcare; high infant mortality in Polk County; barriers in healthcare access including appointment availability and office hours; elevated child injury risks; rising pediatric obesity; gaps in mental health services; untreated vision, hearing, and speech conditions
Solutions: Expand primary care, urgent care and telehealth services; provide NICU discharge education and infant CPR training
Implement: Partner w Nemours to expand hours and locations for urgent and primary pediatric care in underserved counties; universal depression and postpartum screening programs across pediatric and primary care practices; promote use of bilingual care navigators and interpreter

Title: 2024 State Scorecard on Women’s Health and Reproductive Care
MainIdea: Data visualizations and rankings highlighting state-level stark disparities in women’s health outcomes across the US, especially in maternal mortality, insurance coverage, reproductive health access, and postpartum support
Problems: Women in southeastern and non-Medicaid-expansion states face higher maternal mortality, syphilis rates, breast/cervical cancer deaths, and low postpartum depression screening; high uninsured rates pre- and during pregnancy; provider shortages; abortion bans; postpartum depression underdiagnosed and untreated; Medicaid expansion improves outcomes but is not universal.
Solutions: funding for early pregnancy insurance enrollment navigation; integrate culturally competent doulas and midwives in maternity care;
Implement: Pushing medicaid expansion; fund OB/GYN and midwifery scholarships tied to service in maternal care deserts

Title: Orange County Mental and Behavioral Health System Gaps Analysis
Authors: Orange County Mental Health and Homelessness Division
MainIdea: Gap analysis, outlining shortages in  mental health and behavioral health services in Orange County including insufficient psychiatric capacity, crisis response, housing, care coordination, and workforce and funding needs totaling approximately $49 million for system improvements/ expansion
Problems: Long wait times for psychiatric services (1-3 months); lack of psychiatric emergency department; insufficient youth and adult residential beds; minimal peer support post-incarceration; transportation barriers and workforce shortages (e.g., Nurse Family Partnership needing 4.5 staff)
Solutions: LAID OUT RECOMMENDATIONS FOR STATE: Expand Psychiatric Emergency Department ($330k); increase youth/adult residential beds ($2.6mil); add peer support programs ($455k); grow Nurse Family Partnership (+4.5 staff, $441k); broaden crisis intervention teams and mobile crisis services ($1M); invest $27mil in early childhood mental health consultation; fund housing initiatives per Housing for All 10-year $160mil plan; expand training in evidence-based practices ($1M); SAMHSA System of Care grant expansion ($1M); establish drop-in centers with substance abuse overlay ($900k); expand medication-assisted treatment and outpatient services
Implement: Partner with Orange County Mental Health Division and Heart of Florida United Way to align funding and services; advocate for Medicaid expansion; infrastructure for crisis services including 24/7 drop-in centers and mobile crisis teams and indvls transitioning from jail and residential settings

Title: Sequential Intercept Model Mapping Report for Orange County
Authors: Patricia A. Griffin, PhD; Brian Case, MA
MainIdea: Report on reduction of justice involvement and improving care continuity for individuals with behavioral health needs in Orange County
Problems: People with mental illness or substance use disorders cycle through the justice system; lack of reentry support; poor data integration; insufficient crisis response; gaps in CIT training; fragmented services
Solutions: Develop more fleshed-out continuous reentry/reintegration center; create alternative low-demand diversion center; expand mobile crisis services; integrate cross-system data; enhance CIT program
Implement: Partner with OC to support development of reentry center with service mapping and funding coordination; assist in design and evaluation of low-barrier diversion center model; help identify sustainable funding for expanded adult mobile crisis teams; advocate for data-sharing MOUs across agencies?

Title: Born to Die: Florida's Infant Mortality Crisis
MainIdea: The report reveals that Florida’s infant mortality crisis is a preventable public health failure.  Black babies are dying at twice the rate of white babies, and many deaths occur in communities with limited access to obstetric services, overwhelmed hospitals, and under-resourced support systems.
Problems: Central Florida, like much of the state, faces critical gaps in maternal and infant health: OB/GYN shortages, transportation barriers, and inconsistent Medicaid acceptance limits access to care. This is especially true for Black and low-income families. Many hospitals and clinics in the region lack the staffing, NICU capacity, and culturally responsive care practices needed to prevent preterm births and infant deaths.
Solutions: The report suggests expanding access to midwives and doulas, investing in prenatal outreach in underserved counties, and ensuring Medicaid expansion reaches vulnerable populations. Central Florida stakeholders can improve maternal health by creating mobile or community-based prenatal services and building cross-agency collaboration to support expectant mothers before emergencies arive.
Implement: The Thrive working group can use Map the Meal Gap data to prioritize high-need counties in Central Florida, advocate for equitable food systems, and collaborate with food banks and schools to expand access.

Title: Healthcare 3.0: Healthcare for the New Normal
MainIdea: Deloitte's "Healthcare 3.0" outlines a future-oriented model focused on building resilient, patient-centered systems for the "new normal" by harnessing digital transformation, virtual care, workforce innovation, and sustainable operations to improve access, efficiency, and health equity.
Problems: Central Florida healthcare mirrors global challenges: unequal access to virtual care and digital tools, persistent workforce shortages, clinician burnout, outdated legacy systems etc.
Solutions:
Use insights from behavioral studies to increase individual engagement in
health matters
• Integrate behavior change as a core component
within new healthcare delivery models
• Leverage the power of influencers and social
networks to support and drive health behaviour
change
• Adopt remote monitoring and self-care technologies
to support and empower individuals and establish
linkages to clinicians and influencers
• Encourage multi-stakeholder involvement which
includes public and private sector partnerships across
the healthcare ecosystem
Implement: THRIVE can collaborate with CF health systems to pilot virtual care access in underserved communities, support training programs for digital health roles, advocate for funding of interoperable IT platforms, integrate climate-ready infrastructure in local health facilities, and host forums on AI ethics and workforce well-being.

Title: Working to make Florida even more competitive
MainIdea: ChamComm head says FL’s child care crisis undermines both current workforce participation and long-term economic competitiveness, and therefore must be addressed through legislative reform, business engagement, increased investment in early learning
Problems: $5.4 billion in lost economic value annually from parents missing work to care for children under 6; $911 million in lost tax revenue due to child care access issues --> structural : families earning slightly above poverty level are excluded from School Readiness Program; providers opt out of the program due to low reimbursement rates
Solutions: Raise income eligibility threshold for School Readiness Program toat least 55% of State Median Income; increase reimbursement rates (incentivize provider participation), tax incentives for employers to support employee child care needs; reduce unnecessary regulations on child care providers; establish business-led advocacy and investment thru the Florida Business Alliance for Early Learning Project
Implement: Raise income eligibility threshold for School Readiness Program toat least 55% of State Median Income; increase reimbursement rates (incentivize provider participation), tax incentives for employers to support employee child care needs; reduce unnecessary regulations on child care providers; establish business-led advocacy and investment thru the Florida Business Alliance for Early Learning Project; acilitate roundtables with child care providers to identify specific regulatory barriers in Central Florida and report findings to policymakers;f supporting SB 916, HB 929, SB 820,  HB 635;

Title: Causes of and Solutions to the Student Debt Crisis
MainIdea: Analysis of the origins, inequities, and impacts of the student debt crisis in the US w solutions from federal, state, institutional, employer levels to reduce burdens on borrowers and hrpw economic stability
Problems: Rising tuition and fees outpacing wages; declining state funding for higher education; easy access to loans without sufficient repayment safeguards; college degrees diminishing economic returns; predatory/fraudulent for-profit institutions; high default rates among borrowers, esp borrowers of color, women, paents, low-income studensts; knockdown effects of debt on consumer spending and wealth accum
Solutions: Reduce out-of-pocket college costs through free college/community college programs and expanded grants; reinstate, expand debt forgiveness/enrollment programs for dropouts; enforce regs against predatory lending and fraudulent institutions; targeted loan repayment programs, employer tax credits, housing assistance tied to debt reduction
Implement: Partner w local colleges to pilot tuition waiver or debt-forgiveness reenrollment programs for dropouts in key workforce areas (e.g., nursing, teaching, skilled trades); employer consortium to collectively offer student loan repayment benefits (pooling funds to increase reach)

Title: Home-Based Child Care: A Surprising Key to Keeping the American Workforce Strong
MainIdea: Homebased child care (HBCC) is majority for children, yet remains underfunded and unsupported; robust, targeted investments in HBCC infrastructure, provider support, policy integration needed
Problems: Despite being 97% of all child care settings and serving nearly two-thirds of children birth to five, HBCC providers undervalued & excluded from quality initiatives, subsidy access, and data systems - esp in rural and low-income areas
Solutions: Caching, peer networks,  individualized support; models like All Our Kin; inclusion in data collection
Implement: Listening sessions to identify their specific local needs, then partner with child care resource & referral agencies to codesign wraparound business and quality supports tailored to this population; All Our Kin model for homebased probider hub; data collection??

Title: Supporting Teachers in State-Funded Preschool – 2017-2018 State Preschool Workforce Supplement
Authors: National Institute for Early Education Research
MainIdea: Inconsistent pay for similar qualifications for teachers of state-funded preschool programs; significant disparities in access to paid planning time, mentoring, and scholarships
Problems: FL less generous than other states in pay and access to paid planning time, mentoring, and scholarships
Solutions: Promote salary and benefit parity policies between Pre-K and K–3 teachers; expand professional development supports (coaching, mentoring, paid PD time, scholarships, loan forgiveness)

Title: Child Care – An EssentialWork Support for WomenA 2018 Analysis of Access to Child Care inCentral Texas
Authors: The Women’s Fund at Austin Community Foundation.
MainIdea: The report examines access to affordable, quality child care in Central Texas, showing how a lack of affordable care restricts women’s workforce participation and economic mobility. It frames child care as both an economic and equity issue, essential to a two-generation strategy that supports today’s working parents and builds the future workforce.
Problems:
Low- and middle-income families spend up to 36% of income on child care.
27 zip codes in Central Texas have no high-quality (Texas Rising Star) child care seats.
Low pay and high turnover among early educators; 92% are women and 45% are people of color.
Solutions:
Increase subsidy funding to serve all eligible children.
Require state-funded providers to meet Texas Rising Star (TRS) quality benchmarks.
Invest in teacher education, wages, and benefits to stabilize the workforce.
Implement:
Promote public–private partnerships to expand affordable child care options.
Advocate for state-level policy alignment between Pre-K, Head Start, and subsidy programs.

Title: The Lifecycle Benefits of an Influential Early Childhood Program: Advocate Briefing
MainIdea: This briefing summarizes Heckman’s landmark analysis of the Abecedarian/CARE early childhood programs, showing that comprehensive, high-quality care from birth to age five yields a 13% annual return on investment (ROI), which is significantly higher than programs beginning at preschool age. The benefits come from improved health, education, employment, and intergenerational income outcomes, proving that early care is both an economic and social policy priority.
Problems:
Low-quality child care can have negative outcomes, particularly for boys.
Health and nutrition are often excluded from early learning programs.
Policymakers undervalue early childhood as an economic driver.
Solutions:
Invest from birth to age five in integrated, high-quality programs that combine care, learning, and health.
Treat child care as both early learning and family economic support.
Embed health, nutrition, and developmental screenings within child care centers.
Implement:
Advocate for birth-to-five comprehensive programs that include health and nutrition components.
Build partnerships between public health, education, and workforce agencies to model Heckman’s findings locally.

Title: Perry: The Program That Inspired Modern Early Childhood Education
MainIdea: The Perry Preschool Project shows   quality early childhood education significantly improves life outcomes not only for at-risk children but also for their children thus validating early education as a powerful multigenerational tool
Problems: Poverty and inequality in early childhood experiences and home environments; simplistic education metrics (e.g., IQ) that ignore long-term life outcomes; persistent criminal activity and instability in underserved communities; generational transmission of disadvantage
Solutions: Implement high-quality, child-centered early learning that includes parental engagement, home visiting; invest early education starting at birth to maximize returns; use evidence from Perry and Abecedarian to expand access to effective early childhood programs for disadvantaged families
Implement: See CF solutions/

Title: District Scorecard
MainIdea: This scorecard provides numerous measurements regarding schools performance to indicate how they have reached/not reached their 6 objectives for 2025 (high expectations for student learning, support of student resiliency, dedicated and high-quality team, positive climate and safe environment, efficient operations, engaged and invested community).
Problems: Achievement gaps in writing and mathematics between student demographics (between ethnicities, abilities, languages, etc.). Room for improvement in secondary students' sense of belonging, student resiliency knowledge and skills, diversifying personnel, students' perceptions of classroom and school-wide culture, and more.
Solutions: Continue fundraising efforts, gather stakeholder feedback, develop metrics for surveys and self-reported observations, provide/strengthen teacher supports, etc.
Implement: Engage in and support fundraising efforts with Foundation for Orange County Public Schools. Advocate for increased volunteering efforts. Partner with student mental health organizations and school counselors to further impacts and reach.

Title: Orlando Technical College Catalog
Authors: Orange County Public Schools, Career and Technical Education Division.
MainIdea: Comprehensive overview of career and technical education (CTE) programs in Orange County, offering workforce pathways, adult education, and industry certifications aimed at economic mobility and lifelong learning.
Problems:
Barriers to access for adult learners and low-income students.
Skills gaps between education and workforce demands.
Need for inclusive support for students with disabilities and veterans.
Solutions:
Affordable, accredited training linked to high-demand careers.
Partnerships with industry, veterans’ programs, and CareerSource.
Academic remediation and ADA accommodations for equitable access.
Implement: Partner with OTC and CareerSource to align CTE programs with THRIVE job.

Title: Business Case for Early Childhood Investments
MainIdea: Strategic early childhood investments have  economic, educational, health, and workforce returns and should be a priority for business and policy leaders to support longer-term prosperity.
Problems: Early developmental gaps among disadvantaged children leading to long-term educational underperformance; significant word gap between children of different socio-economic backgrounds; economic and workforce vulnerabilities due to underinvestment in early development
Solutions: Nurse-Family Partnership? Michrigan's Great Start Readiness, Cicago Child-{arent Centers?

Title: High School Alternatives Could Meet the Evolving Needs of Students
MainIdea: A new report is discussed in which alternative high school models and practices are analyzed. Proven practices to support student success in this field are further identified and recommendations are made to strengthen alternative high school options.
Problems: Counter The narrative that alternative high schools are for students with poor academic abilities and poor job prospects. + Age Limits tied to funding for alternative high school options.
Solutions: -Create a supportive environment and focus on the existing strengths of students; LISTEN to the students + Create a special educator certification for alternative high school teachers to better set them up for success. + Provide staff with the support that they need. Makes for stronger mentors.
Implement: Create greater awareness, funding opportunities from private and public entities, and providing operational support to apply for grants. + advocate for The removal of policy barriers that hinder success. + help build program networks so best practices can become widespread and refined.

Title: Pre-K suspensions and expulsions can have dire effects — but we don’t know how common they are
MainIdea: Suspensions and expulsions in pre-K can have long-term negative effects, but  insufficient data to track or address the issue effectively
Problems: Disproportionate suspension of black boys & children with disabilities; lack of standardized discipline policies; limited support for educators; absence of comprehensive data especially in private and home-based early childhood settings
Solutions: Suspension and expulsion alternatives; state-level data collection efforts; mental health support for educators; updated discipline policies specific to early childhood?
Implement: Fund on-site behavioral specialists and SEL coaches in high-need early childhood programs?

Title: Kindergarten Readiness In Baltimore City
Authors: The Institute for Innovation & Implementation, University of Maryland School of Social Work for Baltimore’s Promise (Sarah Nadiv, Margo Candelaria, Tiffany Martoccio, Deborah S. Harburger)
MainIdea: This report details the roles of early care and education (ECE) in academic, social, emotional, and financial impacts, barriers to student success and accessibility, and methods for change.
Problems: Low access to high-quality affordable ECE programs (not many, limited funding), high costs of care, low pay for child care providers and rise of illegal care providers,
Solutions: Lower the cost of high-quality education by 10%, delegate funding to public-funded ECE programs,  support child care providers
Implement: Advocate for increased funding delegated to ECE programs, promote trainings and supports for child care providers,

Title: Colleges & Universities in Orlando MSA
MainIdea: Overview of higher education institutions in Orlando MSA and surrounding 100-mile radius, including enrollment and graduation data across public, private, and technical institutions, wimplications for workforce development and community planning.
Problems: High concentration of graduates in certain fields not always aligned with regional labor market needs; large numbers of students carry debt without clear workforce pathways; fragmented ecosystem of technical, state, private, and for-profit institutions with uneven quality
Solutions: Expanded apprenticeship and work-study models with major employers (Disney, AdventHealth, Lockheed Martin, etc.); more shared student support infrastructure across smaller colleges (e.g., mental health, financial aid navigation, childcare)
Implement: Convene presidents/chancellors of UCF, Valencia, Seminole State, technical colleges w major employers to co-design curricula; pilot “earn-and-learn” apprenticeship programs in high-need fields (healthcare, tech, trades) tied to participating colleges; pooled scholarship/debt-reduction fund from philanthropy/corporations that forgives loans or covers tuition in exchange for local employment commitments

Title: The racist and sexist roots of child care in America explain why the system is in shambles
MainIdea: The article analyzes the root causes of America’s broken child care system, arguing that inequities embedded in race, gender, and class have shaped a structure that undervalues both caregivers and families. Child care in the U.S. is not simply underfunded, it is built upon systemic inequities that disproportionately affect women (especially women of color), who make up the majority of the early childhood workforce, and low-income families who cannot afford rising costs.
Problems:
U.S. child care evolved from racist and sexist policies. Care work has long been devalued as “women’s unpaid duty” or “Black women’s labor.”
Families spend a large share of income on child care while educators earn poverty-level wages.
Child care workers, especially Black and Latina women, face stark wage inequities and limited benefits.
Solutions:
Treat child care as essential infrastructure, with universal, sustained government funding. Ensure affordable, high-quality care for all families, especially low-income and rural households.
Design reforms that correct historic injustices and lift up undervalued labor sectors.
Implement:
Advocate for universal child care funding in state and federal budgets.
Partner with women’s economic justice coalitions to elevate the workforce equity agenda.

Title: Pinellas schools to offer day care for district employees starting in July
MainIdea: Pinellas County Schools launched afforadbale childcare program for employees as a recruitment and retention incentive for teachers and staff.
Problems: High cost and inaccessibility of quality childcare; difficulty in retaining and attracting education professionals due to lack of family support benefits; limited childcare options for working parents within the school system
Solutions: Launching employer-sponsored childcare for district employees; subsidizing weekly childcare costs based on child’s age; locating the facility within district-owned property; integrating childcare as part of a broader staff benefits package
Implement: Offer guidance to local governments and large employers (health systems, municipalities, nonprofits) on how to use underutilized properties for affordable on-site childcare; ORANGE COUNTY????

Title: ‘The pay is absolute crap’: Child-care workers arequitting rapidly, a red flag for the economy
MainIdea: The article reports on the national child-care staffing crisis, revealing how poverty-level wages and burnout are driving mass resignations among early childhood educators. The workforce collapse (down more than 126,000 workers from pre-pandemic levels) has become a major economic bottleneck, preventing parents (especially mothers) from re-entering the workforce and threatening overall recovery.
Problems:
Severe workforce shortage: Child-care employment remains down 10% since the pandemic.
Low wages: Median pay around $12/hour, among the lowest of any profession.
Federal stabilization funds ($39 billion) are delayed and insufficient for long-term pay increases.
Solutions:
Massive federal funding (Biden’s proposed $450 billion) to stabilize the industry and expand access.
Increase educator wages and benefits to professional levels.
Treat child care as critical economic infrastructure, not a private market good.
Link childcare investment with pre-K expansion, paid leave, and family support systems.
Implement:
Partner with early education networks to pilot wage supplements and workforce stabilization programs.
Push for cross-sector advocacy linking business recovery with child-care investment.

Title: How to Fix Crumbling Child Care Infrastructure
MainIdea: Child care infrastructure is underfunded and poorly planned, leading to shortages, unsafe facilities, and high barriers to creating/maintaining new programs
Problems: Lack of affordable, quality child care facilities; aging and unsafe infrastructure; zoning and permitting barriers for new and home-based providers; shortage of child care slots
Solutions: Local tax abatements/credits for property owners or employers who build child care; use public buildings and land for free/low rent child care; update zoning, safety codes for child care in more spaces; charge developer impact fees to fund facilities; streamline permitting/licensing barriers for home-based providers
Implement: Advocate for county-wide zoning reform to allow child care in all residential/commercial zones; establish a public-private child care facilities fund for renovations and expansions; create a fast-track permitting program for providers

Title: Thrive by Five Pinellas Annual Report 2020
MainIdea: Accounting of Thrive by Five Pinellas's work in early learning & initiatives addressing barriers to school readiness, family support, racial equity,  community engagement.
Problems: Inefficient use of existing early childhood resources; difficulty for caregivers in navigating services; bias vs. children of color; limited affordable/hhealthy food options; inadequate access to transportation; lack of culturally competent services/language; workforce and systemicbarriers in early learning and family support
Solutions: Create a centralized caregiver-facing resource tool to match families with tailored services
Implement: Family Friendly Business recognition program with tax incentives; partner with local cultural and educational institutions to produce early literacy and racial equity programming; collaborate with food banks and farms to supply early learning centers with nutrition packs and family engagement material

Title: State of Financial Aid in 2022: Lots of Change, Little Details
MainIdea: Overview of sweeping changes to federal student loan programs, repayment rules, FAFSA simplification, and Pell Grant recalculations that will significantly alter how students access, repay, and benefit from financial aid.
Problems: Complexity of financial aid programs creates barriers for first-gen/low-income/minority students; loan forgiveness and repayment restart timelines create confusion for borrowers; students in default unaware of “Fresh Start” opportunity; FAFSA changes may disadvantage families with multiple children in college and complicate filing for divorced/separated households
Solutions: Awareness campaigns in Central Florida about loan forgiveness (Public Service Loan Forgiveness, Fresh Start, closed school forgiveness, disability discharge); FAFSA completion support hubs across colleges and community-based organizations to help families adjust to SAI (Student Aid Index) changes; partner w IRS and community VITA sites to connect tax filing with FAFSA completion
Implement: Launch a CF-wide FAFSA completion drive tied to high school graduation season; partner with large local nonprofits and hospital systems to ensure employees maximize PSLF benefits

Title: How Other Nations Pay for Child Care. The U.S. Is an Outlier.
MainIdea: The US dramatically underfunds child care compared to other wealthy nations -->  limited access, high costs, and inequity
Problems: High out of pocket child care costs; limited quality public preschool access; inequitable distribution of care; underpaid early childhood educators; low avail of subsidized options for children under 3; low-income and Hispanic families less likely to access formal care; policy fragmentation; economic strain on working families; gender inequities in workforce
Solutions: Public funding to cap family child care costs at 7% of income; universal public preschool for ages 3-4; wage parity for early childhood educators with K-12 teachers!!!!!!!; expanded subsidies for families under state median income; direct government support for SAH caregivers
Implement: Early Care Fund to provide tiered subsidies for child care based on income, reducing costs to no more than 7% of income; collaborate with local school districts and Head Start providers to launch free, mixed-delivery universal preschool programs for 3- and 4-year-olds

Title: Florida Senate Takes Aim at 3rd-Grade Reading and High School Graduation Tests
MainIdea: The article reports on Florida Senate proposals to eliminate key standardized tests, including the 3rd-grade reading exam and high school graduation tests, shifting toward a “progress monitoring” model. The move is framed as reducing student testing stress but raises questions about accountability and long-term educational equity.
Problems:
Removing standardized tests may reduce transparency in student performance.
Critics warn it could widen achievement gaps, particularly for low-income and minority students.
Teachers express concern over unclear metrics for literacy and graduation readiness.
Solutions:
Replace single high-stakes exams with continuous performance tracking.
Emphasize teacher-driven assessment and literacy support.
Ensure equity safeguards for students at risk of falling behind.
Implement:
Monitor impacts of testing reforms on student outcomes by race and income.
Support literacy initiatives and teacher training in low-performing districts.

Title: Tracking Transfer: Four-Year Institutional Effectiveness in Broadening Bachelor’s Degree Attainment
MainIdea: Four-year institutions greatly influence bachelor’s completion rates for community college transfer students, yet many fail to adequately support these diverse, often low-income populations. Stronger institutional accountability, partnerships, policies needed
Problems: Low transfer completion rates (only ~50% finish w/i4 years of transfer); disparities for low-income, Black, NA, hispanic, & older students; many transfer students do not complete an associate degree before transferring, lowers success rates; for-profit and predominantly online institutions underperform badly while still enrolling large numbers of vulnerable students; transfer students are underrepresented in high-demand STEM fields
Solutions: Increase associate degree completion before transfer; stronger community college–four-year partnerships with aligned transfer pathways and clear articulation agreements; enrollment of transfer students at selective universities to boost equity and diversity; discourage partnerships with low-performing for-profits and online providers
Implement: Transfer Success Task Force; regional articulation maps for credit transfer and timely pathways from CF community colleges to four-year institutions; expand transfer admissions slots and financial aid; STEM transfer bridge program that provides advising, summer coursework, lab placements for CC students entering STEM; train community college advisors to steer students away from predatory for-profit/online institutions and toward local universities with stronger completion record

Title: Heckman’s Research on the Economic Returns of Early Childhood Education & Nebraska Childcare Gaps
MainIdea: Evidence suggests investments in quality birth-to-5 early childhood education deliver strong economic returns, strengthen families, increase maternal workforce participation, & reduce long-term public costs
Problems: Inadequate access to quality early childhood education; barriers to maternal employment and workforce reentry; high economic costs from lost income, productivity, and state tax revenue; long-term poverty perpetuated
Solutions: Promote childcare investment as an economic growth strategy; elevate ECE in workforce development, economic recovery,  maternal health initiatives
Implement: More high-quality, comprehensive early learning for disadvantaged children beginning at birth; build greater access to childcare across income levels; fund family-centered ECE programs that enable maternal employment -- no specific programmatic suggestions given

Title: Education Insights Presentation: Valuing Quality Credentials for Student Success
Authors: Florida Philanthropic Network (FPN) & Consortium of Florida Education Foundations (CFEF)
MainIdea: Outlines Florida’s 2022 legislative priorities around education-to-workforce pipelines, emphasizing Credentials of Value, the REACH Act, and the FATES project to mitigate the “benefits cliff” for working families.
Problems:
Low postsecondary attainment (below 60% goal).
“Benefits cliff” deters career advancement for low-income parents.
Gaps between workforce needs and credential alignment.
Limited collaboration between employers, educators, and philanthropy.
Solutions:
Develop Master Credentials List defining high-value, equitable credentials.
Expand FATES pilot (childcare subsidy phase-out + job training).
Promote cross-sector data sharing and career mobility tools.
Strengthen partnerships with regional workforce boards.
Implement:
Partner with CareerSource and local education foundations on FATES replication.
Advocate for policy addressing benefits cliffs and funding continuity.

Title:
Dual Enrollment in Arizona High Schools: Participation Rates and Effects on
College-Going Patterns
MainIdea: Fual enrollment significantly increases college-going rates + persistence, but access inequitable across student groups and regions; equitable policies and supports can expand its impact statewide
Problems: Inequitable access to dual enrollment (racial/ethnic gaps, low-income and rural students underserved); financial barriers to participation (tuition/fees)
Solutions: Expand equitable access by subsidizing tuition/fees or forming district–college partnerships; create alternative eligibility criteria beyond standardized tests (e.g., GPA, coursework, teacher recommendation); scale collaborative models (e.g., Arizona Student Opportunity Collaborative) to expand rural access
Implement: Convene Central Florida school districts and local colleges to co-design dual enrollment partnerships with cost-sharing agreements to minimize student/family fees
