Title: The Packard Foundation and Seven National Philanthropic Organizations Announce Investing in America Child Care Partnership
Authors: The David and Lucile Packard Foundation
MainIdea: Announcement that a coalition of major philanthropic organizations' partnership to leverage federal infrastructure investments to expand affordable child care in communities nearby
Problems: Shortage of high-quality child care slots in areas experiencing infrastructure related job growth; difficulty blending federal, state, and private funding for facilities; lack of employer engagement in child care solutions
Solutions: Engage employers, especially in infrastructure related industries, to develop child care plans and provide implementation tools?
Implement: Convene CF employers in construction, clean energy, and manufacturing to create customized child care support plans? with whatcarrot???

Title: Parents Will Do More Good with an Effective Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit
MainIdea: Updating/expanding the Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit by making it refundable would support working families, improve access to quality early childhood education, increase labor force participation, & generate long-term economic and social benefits
Problems: The current CDCTC disproportionately benefits high-income families and excludes low- and middle-income families who need help most--nonrefundable tax credits do not reach families with little or no tax liability; negative effects of high chilcare (affects single mothers most); perpetuates inequality
Solutions: Increase the maximum amount of the CDCTC to reflect real childcare cost; make the CDCTC refundable to ensure low- and middle-income families benefis; recalibrate narrative and frame support for childcare not as a subsidy, but as an investment in both child development and workforce participation
Implement: Advocate for a refundable CDCTC as part of state and federal tax reform agen

Title: New Hampshire Tomorrow 2020 Progress Report
MainIdea: The New Hampshire Tomorrow initiative recap of its significant investments in 2020 to support early childhood, education, recovery, and family resilience through scholarships, emergency programs, and partnerships
Problems: Education inequity tied to poverty; school closures and learning gaps worsened by Covid; lack of behavioral supports in schools; financial instability for community college students; loss of early childhood care capacity; children of parents with substance use disorders at increased risk; limited internet and digital access for remote learning; mental health and recovery services interrupted by the pandemic
Solutions: Flexible funding for Family Resource Centers and emergency scholarships; virtual telehealth expansion for recovery programs; creation of Council for Thriving Children to unify early childhood strategy; emergency child care system for essential workers; summer counselor continuity programs for at-risk students; therapeutic programming like Camp Mariposa for children of addicted parents
Implement: Programmatic modeling after New Hampshire--fund year-round scholarship and emergency aid programs for low-income college students, including wraparound supports like tech access, childcare; partner w  regional nonprofits to create trauma-informed therapeutic youth programs like Camp Mariposa for children impacted by addiction; coordinate w school districts to retain summer counselor engagement w high-need student

Title: Breaking the Silence: 10 Powerful Strategies to Raise Awareness about ACEs
MainIdea: Combatting stigma around adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) requires communities to implement trauma-informed multi-sector solutions
Problems: Widespread stigma and silence around ACEs; lack of trauma-informed public discourse; underrepresentation of survivor stories; insufficient healthcare training on ACEs; lack of inclusive, accessible community programming; financial barriers to healing (e.g., therapy, education)
Solutions: Public awareness campaigns (e.g., Number Story); integrate ACEs training into healthcare systems and schools

Title: Working Paper:The Effects of Full-day Pre-kindergarten:Experimental Evidence of Impacts onChildren’s School Readiness
Authors: Allison Atteberry (University of Colorado Boulder), Daphna Bassok, & Vivian C. Wong (University of Virginia).
MainIdea: This randomized control trial (RCT) provides the first experimental evidence on the impact of full-day vs. half-day prekindergarten on children’s school readiness. Conducted in Westminster Public Schools (Colorado), it finds that full-day pre-K significantly improves receptive vocabulary, literacy, math, and physical development compared to half-day programs, especially for low-income and Hispanic children.
Problems:
Unequal access to full-day programs for low-income and working families.
Limited rigorous evidence on how program intensity (hours per week) affects outcomes.
Funding challenges: full-day programs are costly and may divert resources from other ECE needs.
Benefits of early education often fade by later grades without sustained support.
Solutions:
Expand full-day pre-K availability, especially in underserved communities.
Use evidence-based evaluation to justify funding increases.
Integrate high-quality teaching, family engagement, and developmental supports.
Continue longitudinal tracking to ensure benefits persist into elementary years.
Implement:
Support state and local advocacy for full-day pre-K expansion.
Collaborate with school districts to pilot evidence-based early learning models.
Fund longitudinal evaluation of early education outcomes.
Promote integration of early education with health, nutrition, and family services.

Title: A Blueprint For Reimagining The Federal Role In Education Research And Development
MainIdea: Schapiro writes that the federal government must update, and not dismantle, its role in education R&D to support student success, with a renewed focus on evidence-based practices, robust data systems, and innovation in design
Problems: Growing danger of “shutdown”/abolition of DOE, with cuts to IES and DOE funding reducing the availability of research, data, and guidance for states, limiting CF's ability to leverage national support or insights to improve outcomes
Solutions: Not necessarily for CF - but broadlY:  modernize federal education research capacity; create accessible tools like A–F ratings; invest in data systems and innovation
Implement: Advocate for reinstating evidence-based practices; pilot more accessible tools like DOE What Works Clearinghouse (WWC) ratings in CF; connect CF efforts with national data sources.

Title: 2024 Update- Are Floridians Ready to Go Back to School? Not Without More Teachers…
MainIdea: Current effective and certified teacher shortages within the Florida education system can have devastating effects on students’ academic performances if not monitored and rectified.
Problems: Statewide, 1/10 courses during the 22-23 School Year lacked a teacher with appropriate certifications [K-12 Student Population]. Reasons for the shortage include but are not limited to: the teaching profession’s decline in popularity (many exiting & few entering), stressful nature of the position, low pay & degree requirement, high general cost of living, and the slow conversion of education into a politically charged environment.
Solutions: Things Currently Being Worked on: increasing teacher starting salary, Florida Hometown Heroes Housing Program to alleviate costs in critical professions, & Alternative Pathways to becoming a licensed teacher.
Implement: Support and educate about the advantages of increasing teacher pay- must be made competitive with other states at the minimum and brought to a level where it is competitive against other jobs that require a bachelor’s degree to be attractive. Analyze Texas as a similar model for how to achieve better results.

Title: The Business of Childcare and Early Learning in Orange County
Authors: Early Learning Coalition of Orange County
MainIdea: Orange County's ECE system is underfunded, short-staffed, and financially unstable; changes needed
Problems: teacher shortages, high unmet demand, financial instability of providers after expiration od relief funds, underinvestment, affordability crisis
Implement: Launch shared services hubs in pilot neighborhoods (business supports, enrollment software, payroll, ACA compliance, revenue diversification); build a teacher recruitment pipeline via community school partnerships, internships, and credentialing pathways; fund/pilot paid internships, job placement, and certification support for new ECE professionals; offer business coaching to small providers to help them remain viable

Title: How Some States Are Fixing Problems with Early Childhood Education
MainIdea: Article on how some states are reforming ed systems using data infrastructure, measurement tools, and long-term student tracking in response to declines in enrollment, effectiveness during the pandemic
Problems: FL lacking integrated early childhood education systems, resulting in fragmented data, inadequate quality measurement, poor tracking of long term student outcomes
Implement: Unify early childhood education data systems under a single agency (like Virginia); implement universal  quality measures (e.g., VQB5 in Virginia)

Title: K-Ready Playbook September 2020
MainIdea: Playbook for goals and metrics of effort to rebuild a stronger, more equitable early childhood education and health system in Orange County
Problems: High rates of kindergarten unpreparedness (45–75%); lack of access to affordable quality ECE; systemic racial inequities in care and education; low maternal and child healthcare access; fragmented ECE system; underpaid teachers; insufficient public investment; VPK quality issues?
Solutions: Expand access to affordable, quality early education via scholarship and rating systems (e.g. Parent Aware model); incentivize forhigher standards; develop stackable credential pathways for early educators; fund home visitation programs to support early health and development; promote universal screenings;  local coordinated hub of wraparound services for families
Implement: Fund and pilot stackable credential programs with local colleges (e.g., Valencia, UCF); launch a scholarship and rating navigator for parents to choose high-quality care (modeled on Minnesota’s Parent Aware)

Title: Florida’s High School Cohort 2021-22 Graduation Rate
MainIdea: This article describes Florida’s graduation rates, the manner with which they are calculated, and how they compare against other states. New federal regulations have been implemented by the US Department of Education to make graduation rates across states more uniform and comparable.
Problems: Florida’s high school graduation rate decreased by 2.8% points during the 2021-2022 school year, but still remains higher than the pre-pandemic times and has seen an overall increase in the last 2 decades. Different states have different criteria for a standard diploma, making comparison between them extremely difficult to do accurately and on the same scale.
Solutions: N/A State comparison rates are made possible only at the federal level.
Implement: N/A Mostly descriptive, any change needs to be done at the overall state or federal level.

Title: Maximizing CTE as a Tool for Workforce
MainIdea: CTE prioritized by Tallahassee but students lack access/credits/opportunities, therefore building student-centered transfer systems can increase degree attainment, earnings, and employer retention
Problems: Complex transfer processes, confusing articulation agreements, lack of guidance; nontraditional students (and low-income, first-gen, veterans, minorities, adult learners) risk being tracked into low-mobility jobs
Solutions: Expand statewide articulation to include CTE pathways, modeled after Florida’s 2+2 transfer; align advising and create clear articulation agreements between technical colleges and state colleges; est student-centered partnerships like Tech Express between Orange Technical College and Valencia College; embed certificates WITHIN AS degrees (as at Pensacola State College) to provide incremental credentials
Implement: Convene a CF CTE-to-college pathway task force with technical colleges, Valencia College, UCF, and local industries to map good credit transfers; fund/scale embedded certificate programs that give students short-term credentials en route to degrees; expansion of Tech Express–style advising models across all CF technical colleges, embedding state college advisors onsite

Title: Tracking Transfer: Community College Effectiveness in Broadening Bachelor’s Degree Attainment
Authors:
Tatiana Velasco, John Fink, Mariel Bedoya, Davis Jenkins (Community College Research Center);
Tania LaViolet (Aspen Institute College Excellence Program).
MainIdea: Analyzes how effectively U.S. community colleges support student transfer to four-year institutions and bachelor’s degree completion, especially for low-income, Black, Hispanic, and older learners. Only 16% of students who start at a community college earn a bachelor’s degree within six years.
Problems:
Only 33% of community college entrants transfer to a four-year college.
Less than half of transfers (48%) earn a bachelor’s degree within six years.
Equity gaps: 11% of low-income, 9% of Black, and 6% of older students earn bachelor’s degrees.
Older students and men face consistently lower transfer and completion outcomes.
Solutions:
Strengthen guided transfer pathways and articulation agreements.
Award associate degrees before transfer, which boosts bachelor’s completion.
Expand dual enrollment programs, which nearly double transfer success.
Implement:
Support transfer equity dashboards tracking student outcomes by race and income.
Advocate for statewide articulation reforms (2+2 programs).
Convene college–university partnerships to streamline transfer pipelines.

Title:
Leveraging Peer-to-Peer Influence for College-Going Success:
Broward County Public Schools Introduces BRACE Cadets
MainIdea: Recap on peer to peer advising program, BRACE cadets, which trains high school students to support their peers with college applications, FAFSA, scholarships, and career readiness, expanding counseling capacity and building a college-going culture
Problems: Student-to-counselor ratio in Florida (485:1) far above recommended 250:1; postsec attainment in Broward at 44.2% vs proj. 60% workforce need by 2025; inequities faced by first-generation, low-income, and students of color in accessing college counseling and resources
Solutions: Expansion of programs like BRACE to expand peer to peer avising capacity and college-going culture; social media outreach to demystify applications and financial aid
Implement: ID CF schools w/ highest counselor-to-studen t rations and and pilot peer advising programs modeled on BRACE Cadets; partner w local LCANs, universities, nonprofits, corporate sponsors to fund stipends ($500/student) and training costs; dvlp a standardized two-day training on FAFSA, scholarships, and applications

Title: The Real Cost of College in Florida
MainIdea: FL has among the lowest tuition in the nation, but housing, food, childcare, and other living costs make college unaffordable for many students, esp low-income, part-time, adult, and caregiving students
Problems: Rising student debt despite low tuition; living expenses (housing, food, transportation, childcare, healthcare) exceed scholarships; lower-income students disproportionately affected (net price up to 32x higher burden than wealthier peers); FAFSA noncompletion --> $300M in Pell aid unclaimed; adult learners and student parents face steep childcare costs; food insecurity (23% undergrad); housing insecurity and 8% homelessness; lack of emergency financial supports; declining four-year enrollment tied to cost concerns
Solutions: Adopt universal FAFSA completion policy (with opt-out) to increase Pell access; expand Florida Student Assistance Grant (FSAG) to reduce unmet need; scale up emergency aid programs statewide (modeled on FSU/Seminole State); integrate student supports with SNAP, WIC, TANF access; increase on-campus childcare and subsidies for student parents; improve public awareness of FAFSA and aid simplification
Implement: Partner with local school districts to integrate FAFSA completion into graduation checklists; pilot regional emergency aid fund managed jointly by colleges and nonprofits with rapid disbursement capacity (<72 hours); launch a Central Florida Student Basic Needs Network linking campuses to SNAP/WIC/TANF enrollment support; partner with childcare providers and HCOs to develop subsidized childcare slots for student parents; establish corporate partnerships (employers offering tuition support + flex scheduling for student workers)

Title: Social and Emotional Learning in School Health: Addressing SEL through the Whole School, Whole Community, Whole Child Framework
MainIdea: Provides a guide for schools to integrate SEL into health and education systems, promoting emotional well-being and whole-child development.
Problems: Fragmented SEL implementation; lack of trauma-informed practices and coordinated policies between health and education sectors.
Solutions: Use WSCC framework to align SEL with health education, counseling, family engagement, and community partnerships; establish wellness and trauma-informed policies.
Implement: Partner with schools and local agencies to embed SEL into existing health programs; promote trauma-informed practices; coordinate family and community-based SEL initiatives.

Title: Early Wins in Childhood
MainIdea: Key lessons for driving early childhood systems change: relationship building, systems alignment, backbone infrastructure, facilitation, and communications strategy
Problems: Parent participation in early childhood education/preK is often low due to logistical barriers (transportation, childcare, comfort in settings); stakeholders are often stuck in direct service thinking vs systems-level change; tension between organizational agendas vs shared goals; lack of strong backbone staff or sustainable infrastructure for coordination
Solutions: Invest in deep, ongoing relationship building with parents; carefully select and support backbone organizations with skilled, complementary staff; use flexible facilitation to manage meetings and stakeholder  (parent) engagement
Implement: Fund/establish in some manner parent leadership pipelines by removing participation barriers (stipends, childcare, transportation)

Title: Jacob Williams, Libbie Brey, and Ming Yin
Authors: Jacob Williams, Libbie Brey, and Ming Yin for the Annie E. Casey Foundation
MainIdea: What causes successful reengagement programs for disconnected youth are strong, consistent relationships and individualized support--many youthsdisengage due to negative school experiences, lack of support, and mental health challenges
Problems: Oftentimes youth disengage out of feeling school is irrelevant, hostile, or unsafe; schools’ poor identification and outreach; many existing reengagement programs underresourced & overburdened, with an especial ack of mental health support, inadequate ESE implementation
Solutions: Programs that build strong relationships and provide daily encouragement and coaching; holistic, individualized both academic and nonacademic support (e.g., tech, SEL, logistics); improved data systems to proactively ID youth needing support
Implement: THRIVE could fund/pilot a CF Reengagement Network linking programs and school districts/convene a THRIVE learning community on schoolbased prevention (SEL, IEP/504 compliance, mental health); invest in youth-led design labs at school to re-calibrate setting and belonging & funding for non-academic wraparound support (e.g., transportation, tech, therapy); push for data-sharing and cross-agency student tracking of goalmeeting

Title: The Real Shame of the Nation: The Causes and Consequences of Racial Segregation in America’s Schools
Authors: Richard D. Kahlenberg and Halley Potter
MainIdea: The report explores how racial and economic segregation in U.S. schools continues to widen achievement gaps, limit social mobility, and perpetuate systemic inequities. It argues that school integration is essential for equal opportunity and democracy.
Problems:
Re-segregation trend: U.S. schools are more racially and economically segregated than in the 1980s.
Resource disparities: Majority-Black and Latino schools receive $2,000–$3,000 less per student annually.
Achievement gaps: Segregated schools correlate with lower test scores and graduation rates.
Solutions:
Promote income- and race-conscious school integration.
Reform school assignment zones and encourage interdistrict collaboration.
Fund magnet and charter schools that prioritize diversity.
Link housing and education policy for equitable development.
Implement:
Partner with school boards and housing authorities to pilot integration models.
Advocate for equitable funding and school choice that promotes diversity.

Title: Orange County Head Start Annual Report 2020–2021: Teach, Learn, Lead
Authors: Orange County Government – Head Start Program
MainIdea: Highlights Head Start’s efforts to provide early childhood education and family support during COVID-19, focusing on school readiness and family self-reliance.
Problems: Pandemic disruptions, health and oral care barriers, low family engagement, and financial strain on families.
Solutions: Expanded virtual learning, family engagement strategies, health initiatives, father involvement, and financial wellness programs.
Implement: Collaborate with local early education and health networks to reduce learning and health gaps; scale family engagement and financial wellness models across Central Florida.

Title: Sustainable Development Goals in the Classroom: A UF Faculty Guide for Using the United Nations SDGs
Authors: University of Florida Office of Sustainability & collaborators
MainIdea: Resource and strategy guide for administrators and professors to integrate the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) into university courses, research, and campus engagement
Problems: Opportunity for more spotlighting and engagement with SDGs, disconnection between academic content and real-world global challenges, interdisciplinary approaches to teaching sustainability
Solutions: Use experiential learning and service-learning opportunities tied to SDGs, frame campus sustainability efforts around SDG targets, build faculty learning communities around SDG teaching strategies
Implement: Incorporate SDG literacy into THRIVE leadership programming and donor education/faculty toolkit similar to this, focused on SDGs in community settings/-romote service-learning projects through CF-funded programs with explicit SDG alignment.

Title: College is a Net Positive for Black Students in Florida
MainIdea: College enrollment, esp at in-state four-year institutions, produces significant lifetime economic + social benefits for Black students in Florida and generates measurable returns for the state in higher earnings, tax revenues, civic outcomes
Problems: Lower college enrollment rates among Black high school graduates; disparities in enrollment at selective institutions and in high-paying majors; lower degree completion rates; higher likelihood of accruing burdensome student loan debt
Solutions: Increase in-state college enrollment and degree completion among Black students; strengthen pathways to four-year institutions; support HBCUs; expand state-sponsored need-based financial aid

Title: School System Improvements- Data-Driven Strategies for Teacher Shortages
MainIdea: This article examines Washington State’s persistent teacher shortage - similar to the one Florida is facing - and pertinent data-driven solutions.
Problems: As The study examines Washington State, There are no specific problems mentioned from Central Florida, but some of The issues uncovered in this article could be similar. + limited Certification teachers face large barriers to becoming fully certified- preventing an increase in The teacher workforce. + teacher testing, specifically The financial costs for them, is a large barrier for minority candidates.
Solutions: -Removal of barriers for full teaching certificates could be considered; however it is important to remember that while this might cause an increase in quantity, quality might suffer.
Implement: advocate for lowering costs for teacher Certification tests and lowering Certification barriers.

Title:
BILLIONS TO GAIN:
The Economic Benefits of Investing
in a More Educated Arizona
Authors: Dr. Clive R. Belfield, Paul Perrault, Kimberly Lent Morales, and Rich Nickel
MainIdea: The report quantifies the individual, social, and fiscal benefits of increasing postsecondary enrollment and completion in Arizona. Modeling a single cohort of high school graduates, the study shows that a 20% rise in college enrollment could yield over $5 billion in social gains and $1.82 billion in fiscal gains per cohort. Investing in education, especially for underrepresented students, would generate long-term economic prosperity and equity across the state.
Problems:
Postsecondary attainment in Arizona remains low (46% vs. 60% target).
Large racial and socioeconomic disparities in college enrollment and completion.
Declining K–12 readiness, particularly after COVID-19 disruptions.
Cost barriers and insufficient state investment in higher education.
Solutions:
Strengthen K–12 to college pathways, including dual enrollment and academic advising.
Expand need-based financial aid and state college subsidies.
Prioritize equity: raising enrollment and completion rates among Latino and low-income students.
Implement:
Advocate for state investment in need-based aid and college access initiatives.
Develop mentorship and workforce bridge programs for underrepresented youth.
(Partner with higher education institutions)

Title: Recovery & Renewal- Principles for Advancing Public Education Post-Crisis
Authors: The Aspen Institute- Education & Society Program
MainIdea: This article was written mid-pandemic and discusses how the pandemic has highlighted inequities within our system. Furthermore, it breaks down crucial elements in the ideal response to the pandemic from the education system. Five guiding principles are identified and discussed: ensure equity & engagement, take a holistic view to set a coherent strategy, ground the work in the science of learning, take a long-term view of student success, and embed an Innovation and Learning Agenda.
Problems: During the pandemic, several problems were identified: food insecurity (free or subsidized meals), the digital divide, and undetected neglect and abuse. Different levels of government do not communicate effectively, and have at times given difficult to follow directives that leave educators and leaders struggling to try to meet them at various levels.
Solutions: -When making decisions, make sure that those most affected by the decisions have a voice at the table. + Decisions made at different levels of government need to work towards the same overall goal (holistic approach). + Conduct studies on the differences between the official decisions and their actual implementation.
Implement: Although the prime time for post-pandemic reform has passed, many of the guiding principles identified can still be operationalized at any point in time.

Title: The Economic Impacts of Insufficient Child Care on Working Families
MainIdea: Inadequate child care access for families with children under age 3 leads to significant + measurable economic losses for parents, businesses, and taxpayers, both in the short term and long term.
Problems: Lack of accessible, reliable child care for children under 3; major disruptions to parents’ work time, productivity,  career progression; missing work, reduction in hours, turn down promotions or training,  job instability; businesses experience lost revenue, high turnover recruitment costs --> taxpayers lose income and sales tax revenue; child care problems disproportionately affect women and primary caregivers.
Solutions: More reliable, accessible child care for infants
Implement: Invest in home- and center-based child care supply through targeted incentives and workforce development; incorporate child care access into workforce development and economic mobility strategies; collab w/ orgs in CF to do roundtable on topic?

Title: Early Childhood Education Position Paper
MainIdea: To improve kindergarten readiness, FL must increase funding, raise quality standards, support teachers, build better data systems
Problems: Only 57% of Florida’s kindergarteners are considered “ready” despite 72% VPK enrollment; FL’s VPK program ranks 41st in per-child funding, w real dollar support declining since 2008

Title: Strong Families Programme
Authors: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC)
MainIdea: Evidence-informed family skills program designed for challenging settings to strengthen family relationships, reduce violence, and improve mental health for children and caregivers.
Problems: High family stress, coercive parenting, weak communication, risk of violence, and early substance use initiation in resource-limited environments.
Solutions: Three-week program combining caregiver, child, and family sessions that promote positive parenting, stress management, communication, and pro-social family interaction.
Implement: Partner with local family service organizations to pilot brief, low-cost family skill sessions modeled after Strong Families; integrate caregiver training into existing child welfare and community mental health programs.

Title: US Adult Literacy Facts
MainIdea: More than 36 million adults in the US have low literacy (or illiteracy), and this affects unemployment, family literacy, poverty, education, health literacy/healthcare costs, and crime rates.
Problems: High number of low literacy in adults with little access to services (less than 10% of adults in need are receiving service in the US)
Solutions: Increase accessibility to literacy programs (reading, writing, math, health literacy)
Implement: Advocate for or provide more adult literacy programs or greater access to them.

Title: What we can learn from red states’ approach to child care challenges
MainIdea: This article discusses the factors that make investment in early childhood education critical- majority of brain development occurs in the first 3 years of life, young parents can’t afford high-quality childcare early in their careers, and parents can return earlier to the workforce if childcare is provided.
Problems: Further investment in early childhood education/care required.
Solutions: Solutions offered: childcare vouchers, covering child care costs for teachers, matching local investments, incentives for quality, improving subsidy, tax credits, and strengthening public pre-K.
Implement: Encouragement of local government and decision-makers to adopt proposed solutions or lobby for them?

Title: Why Now Is the Time for Businesses to Improve Child Care
MainIdea: Pandemic era federal funding for child care now over, access gaps have widened. The article urges businesses to step in
Problems: Loss of federal child care stabilization funds leading to reduced access; higher rates of parents, especially women, leaving the workforce due to lack of child care; small businesses facing growth constraints or closure because employees cannot access affordable child care
Solutions: Employers providing child care stipends, on site centers, negotiated rate partnerships
Implement: Partner with chamcomms to host employer workshops on child care benefit design, ROI; develop CF employer toolkit outlining models for stipends, on site care, local provider partnerships; create pooled purchasing program to negotiate lower child care rates for employees across multiple small businesses

Title: The Business of Childcare and Early Learning in Orange County
Authors: Helios Education Foundation, Early Learning Coalition of Orange County
MainIdea: Research innovations and proposals and recommednations for addressing childcare teacher and seat shortages in Orange County by developing pilot innovations and systemic solutions to improve provider stability, teacher recruitment, early childhood education infrastructure
Problems: Inadequate funding for childcare; high cost and lack of affordability; teacher shortages and retention issues; provider financial instability; insufficient childcare capacity and seats; lack of coordinated data and advocacy
Solutions: Provide shared back-office services to childcare providers including centralized bookkeeping, payroll, HR, ACA compliance, and enrollment systems; paid internship and apprenticeship programs in partnership with CareerSource and local ECE centers to fast-track credentialing and classroom readiness
Implement: Partner with UCF and Valencia to co-develop ECE teacher training program embedded in high schools in underserved zip codes;fund + pilot a shared-services pilot for 10 small providers in a single zip code, providing them with outsourced payroll, marketing templates, enrollment software, coaching; Childcare Sponsorship Match Fund where philanthropic dollars match employer contributions to fund employee childcare scholarships

Title: There’s a child care crisis in Florida. Here’s why that matters.
MainIdea: Florida’s child care crisis is deepening, marked by high costs, low worker pay, staff shortages, and insufficient access to early childhood education, although child care remains a keystone for development and economic stability
Problems: Nearly half of Tampa Bay children enter kindergarten unprepared, affecting long-term learning outcomes; child care costs are higher than college tuition; early childhood educators are paid below a living wage, severe worker shortages with 38% of Floridians living in child care deserts; lack of affordable child care limits parental workforce participation; disparate access
Solutions: Expanded public and private sector investment in early education, increased wages, hiring/retention bonuses for child care workers, training pathways for early educators, programs like the Early Learning Coalition initiatives in Pinellas County
Implement: Local pilots of family engagement programs tied to early learning; expanded public and private sector investment in early education, increased wages, hiring/retention bonuses for child care workers, training pathways for early educators, programs like the Early Learning Coalition initiatives in Pinellas County

Title: Florida's 2021 Child Care Survey
MainIdea: The survey highlights Florida parents’ challenges in accessing affordable, high-quality child care. Parents emphasize safe, nurturing student-teacher relationships, but affordability, availability, and infant/toddler care remain major barriers.
Problems:
Affordability is the biggest barrier for many families.
Infant and toddler care is especially hard to access.
Quality concerns: many parents rate their care only “average.”
Limited hours, locations, and long waitlists restrict access.
Underrepresentation of certain communities (e.g., Haitian Creole speakers).
Solutions:
Expand access to affordable child care through subsidies and public investment.
Increase support for infant/toddler programs and reduce waitlists.
Improve teacher training, education, and compensation.
Address disparities by ensuring inclusion of underrepresented groups.
Implement:
Advocate for expanded subsidies and financial supports.
Partner with providers to pilot infant/toddler-focused programs.
Develop training initiatives for early childhood staff.
Gather more inclusive survey data (Creole, rural families, low-income parents).

Title: Staffing and compensation are at the heart of building a better early childhood system
Authors: Daphna Bassok, Anna J. Markowitz, Kate Miller-Bains, Isabelle Fares
MainIdea: Chronic staffing shortages and low wages undermine early childhood education; public investments and permanent compensation reform will be essential to stabilize the workforce and ensure high-quality early care.
Problems: High teacher turnover in childcare; chronic staffing shortages leading to closed classrooms and longer waitlists; low wages ($9–$10/hr) and minimal benefits make retention unsustainable; teachers forced to work extra hours; low morale
Solutions: Wage supplements or guaranteed stipends (monthly?) for early childhood teachers funded through mixed public-private models

Title: This early-education approach has cities everywhere talking
MainIdea: Providence Talks, a language development program, has been implemented elsewhere with great gains for children who started in low-talk environments
Problems: Low-talk environments, reading scores in CF?
Solutions: Implement pilot of Providence Talks or Birmingham Talks!
Implement: Implement pilot of Providence Talks or Birmingham Talks!

Title:
The Economic and
Fiscal Impacts of
Education and
Training Beyond High
School in Florida
MainIdea: FL must boost postsec educations and credential attainment, esp among populations of color, to reach its “SAIL to 60” workforce goal by 2030, close gaps, and strengthen economic and social outcomes
Problems: Ed'l attainment gaps between racial and ethnic groups; current attainment is 7% below the 60% goal (almost800,000 people); declining enrollment since 2010, especially among white and black populations; financial and logistical barriers for learners; need to replace 1.2M retiring specialized workers in next decade

Title: Silicon Valley Discovers an Age-Old Child Care Hack: The Neighbors
MainIdea: Otter (a new platform connecting working parnets w/stay-at-home caregivers nearby) is attracting major venture capital support, but raising concerns about regulation, worker protections, systemic equity
Problems: Lack of affordable and flexible childcare; caregivers earn low wages and have few protections; shortage of licensed childcare options; parents forced out of workforce due to lack of care; rigid center schedules
Solutions: Otter = tech solution that matches parents with local stay-at-home caregivers using algorithms, human review, and background checks; caregivers can earn income on their own terms; village-style childcare?
Implement: Explore launching or piloting a CF-specific version of the Otter or Otter-like model that meets local regulations; work w partners to facilitate informal caregiver networks and ensure training, vetting, pay protections

Title: Sarasota employers and workers both hurt by childcare crisis, survey shows
MainIdea: Reporting on new survey by the Early Learning Coalition of sarasota county reveals widespread childcare challenges among local employees that significantly affect business operations; employer interest in on-site care and tax credits
Problems: Hgh childcare costs for employees; lack of available childcare slots; increased absenteeism. turnover for employers; decreased workplace productivity; recruitment and retention challenges; reduced revenue and profits; limited awareness or use of employer childcare tax credits
Solutions: Employers expressing willingness to help solve childcare access issues; growing interest in providing on-site childcare; partnership opportunities between employers and existing providers like YMCA; task force as connector between businesses and providers(?); employer info sessions on state and federal tax credits for childcare benefits
Implement: Launch a childcare-business matchmaking program to connect interested employers with providers able to expand on-site or nearby care; organize tax credit info sessions in partnership with local chambers and economic development councils

Title: Racial Disparities in Basic Needs Insecurity During the Pandemic
MainIdea: Students who are parents (the article focuses especially on students of color, single parents, and Black fathers) face disproportionately high levels of food and housing insecurity during college
Problems: Extreme rates of basic needs insecurity among parenting students of color; lack of outreach, stigma around public benefits; underutilization of support services, esp among Black fathers; structural racism limiting access to aid and housing; exclusion from financial aid due to full-time or recent graduate restrictions
Solutions: Designate college coursework as work for TANF compliance; increase peer navigator and targeted outreach efforts; fund and design culturally-responsive support programs for Black fathers; collect and disaggregate data on parenting students
Implement: Partner with local colleges - UCF, Valencia - to support peer navigator models tailored to parenting students, especially single parents and Black fathers; advocate for statewide and campus-specific policy reviews of aid programs that exclude or hinder parenting students

Title: Degrees Awarded by Field of Study – Orlando MSA
MainIdea: Breakdown of 61,745 degrees and certificates awarded in the Orlando metro area (2021–22), showing educational and workforce trends by sector, highlighting strengths in health, business, and STEM-related programs
Problems:
Gaps in STEM and skilled trades relative to regional job demand.
Underrepresentation of women and minorities in engineering and technology.
Low graduation volume in education and public service fields.
Solutions:
Expand career pathway partnerships between colleges and employers.
Invest in apprenticeships and upskilling programs for high-demand sectors.
Promote diversity in STEM, trades, and leadership programs.
Implement:
Collaborate with Valencia, UCF, and Orange Technical College to fill skills gaps.
Create scholarships and mentorships targeting underrepresented fields.

Title: AAUP Special Committee Report on Florida
MainIdea: Examines the erosion of academic freedom, shared governance, and higher-education autonomy in Florida under recent state policies. The report concludes that legislative and administrative interference threatens democratic values, diversity, and intellectual inquiry across the state’s public universities.
Problems:
Laws restricting DEI, gender studies, and classroom speech.
Loss of faculty self-governance.
Negative effect on teaching about race, sexuality, and politics.
Censorship and intimidation in faculty hiring and curriculum design.
Solutions:
Reinforce academic freedom protections in university policies.
Build faculty-led oversight bodies to preserve shared governance.
Pursue legal and policy advocacy against unconstitutional restrictions.
Implement:
Support academic freedom coalitions and faculty advocacy groups.
Partner with legal networks to defend First Amendment rights in education.
Integrate education freedom indicators into THRIVE’s democracy dashboard.

Title: Opinion: What Do Early Child Care Workers Need? Better Pay, More Respect and a Few Good Men
MainIdea: Op-ed: ECE workforce needs better pay, improved working conditions, and greater gender diversity, esp  more men, to elevate status and compensation of profession
Problems: Low pay and poor benefits for ECE workers; workforce shortages; gender segregation in caregiving roles; lack of male representation in the early education workforce; undervaluing of “pink collar” jobs, stigma
Solutions: Improve compensation and benefits for early childhood workers; recruit more men to increase status and wages in the field; support male educators through targeted retention efforts and recognition; encourage credentialing through programs like the Child Development Associat
Implement: Partner with training providers to launch male-targeted recruitment campaigns into early childhood roles

Title: StriveTogether Theory of Action Overview
MainIdea: Theory of action statement by Strive Together - which seeks to build civic infrastructure for cradle to career positive outcomes for children in poverty
Problems: Disjointed efforts among community orgs; insufficient tracking and acting infrastructure
Implement: Proof Point model; youth & family voices central in data collection, planning, outcomes

Title: Want to Grow the Economy? Fix the Child Care Crisis
Authors: Sandra Bishop-Josef, Tom Garrett, Sara Watson, Chris Beakey
MainIdea: Lack of affordable quality and accessible child care for infants and toddlers is causing major economic losses for parents, employers, and taxpayers
Problems: Child care access is limited, esp for infants/toddlers, off hours work, and rural areas; child care costs exceed college tuition many areas
Solutions: Expand and improve affordability, quality, and availability of child care through public funding, tax credits, employer partnerships, and quality accreditation
Implement: Pooling funding for subsidies and facility expansion; establish employer-sponsored child care programs or on-site care for shift workers; provide tax incentives for businesses that partner with accredited child care providers

Title: Invisible Students: The Information Crisis in Early Education
MainIdea: Data is lacking on early learning and development of young learners, and this has only been exacerbated by the pandemic. It has been observed, though, that enrollment is declining and there are fewer learning gains. Outside of the classroom, there has ben an observed rise in stress levels and decline in quality in early childhood experiences.
Problems: Information gaps in data on childhood development and classroom quality are due to the diversity in funding sources, formats, and venues (state funded pre-kindergarden, federally funded Head Start, private or publicly subsidized child care).
Solutions: Unify the state's early childhood system under the state department of education. This will allow for easier service coordination, sharing of data, and parent communication.
Implement: Conduct assessments and surveys of early childhood experiences and education to help bridge the information gap. Advocate for unification of school policies under the state department of education.

Title: How Accelerated Coursework Contributes to Increased Postsecondary Attainment in Florida
MainIdea: Participation in accelerated coursework (DE, IB, AP) significnatly increases postsec enrollment and degree attainment in FL; expanding equitable access to these programs can close achievement gaps and improve regional educational outcomes.
Problems: Lower participation rates in accelerated coursework among Black, low-income, ELL, and special education students; rural districts have less access to AP courses; lack of awareness among students and families about accelerated opportunities; insufficient advising and guidance for students navigating options; geographic inequity; in program availability; disparities in educator incentive use and access
Solutions: Continue state funding and incentive programs for districts and teachers offering AP/DE; improve equity in access by targeting underserved populations for AP/DE enrollment; increase quality advising for students, especially those from priority groups; build awareness campaigns to educate families on options and benefits; expand DE offerings in smaller and rural districts; ensure instructional quality through bonuses and supports for teachers in lower-performing schools
Implement: Fund/develop microgrants for schools to offer AP training or DE partnerships with local colleges + launch an annual regional teacher incentive program that layers add'l bonuses for teachers in higher need areas who support students in AP/DE; advocate for increased state investment in the Dual Enrollment Scholarship Program to ensure long-term sustainability

Title: States with the Best & Worst School Systems
MainIdea: US state public school ranking (on metrics like but not limited to performance, funding, safety, class size, and teacher credentials) - FL ranking 26
Problems: FL ranked 26, w/middling academic outcomes; significant disparities in funding and performance exist between states
Solutions: Identify & replicate low-spending/high-performance models (like Colorado and Iowa); prioritize factors beyond funding--e.g., teacher quality, student safety, class size

Title: 2025 Kids Count Report– Florida
MainIdea: This report covers the prevalence of child poverty in Florida while looking at four main aspects/contributors: economic well-being, education, health, and family and community.
Problems: While some measurements have improved, 4 of the 16 have seen no progress and 6 (children living in households with a high housing cost burden, young children not in school, fourth graders not proficient in reading, eighth graders not proficient in math, low birth-weight babies, and child and teen death per 100,000) of the 16 have worsened. 3 of the 6 measurements that have worsened fall in the education category.
Solutions: Utilize these findings to focus efforts on these stagnant or worsening factors. Specifically, support education by encouraging young childrent to attend school, supporting/funding tutoring organizations, providing support services for teachers, etc.
Implement: Help fundraise for tutoring organizations, advocate for and increase awareness of services and resources for teachers. Partner with family-focused organizations to assist with funding and promoting their resources to families and children.

Title: Community Pulse Report for Children & Families (October 2020)
MainIdea: PPT summarizing and tracking key health, housing, education, financial, and child wellbeing markers during the pandemic across a five-county region—racial inequities, service gaps, and systemic stress points that have worsened under Covid
Problems: Drastic increases in food insecurity, housing instability, utility shutoffs, child care gaps; reduced access to mental, physical health care; higher eviction filings in black-majority neighborhoods; disparate impacts of virtual schooling on non-white and low-income students; rising unemployment, wage gaps; declining maternal employment; increasing enrollment in SNAP and Medicaid but insufficient benefits; post-secondary enrollment declines especially for Black students.
Solutions: Expanded moratoriums on utility shutoffs, evictions, foreclosures; utility and housing assistance programs (plus internet as a utility); expanded broadband access; child care stabilization and supports for working mothers of color; direct cash assistance and flexible support services for families.
Implement: Mobilize around stabilization funds for child care and education recovery program; community-led mutual aid and direct cash programs to fill immediate gaps

Title: Feeding the Line or Ending the Line? Innovations Among Food Banks
MainIdea: This doc narrates food banks' shift toward addressing the root causes of hunger
Problems: Chronic food insecurity linked to poverty, unemployment, and structural inequities; imited infrastructure for fresh food (refrigeration, logistics) at food banks but processed food reliance contributes to poor health outcomes; prvsly, metrics focus on pounds of food vs. long-term impact & donor expectations and institutional inertia block innovation → ack of alignment across food, health, and economic systems
Solutions: Partnerships with farmers and gleaning programs for fresh food; mobile markets, nutrition education, and SNAP matching to improve access and affordability; urban gardens & community farms empowering residents and building self-reliance;ealthcare collaborations: food insecurity screening, produce prescriptions, co-located pantries
Implement: PRODUCE PRESCRIPTIONS - but also funding pilot projects for a mobile produce market or food truck serving high-need neighborhoods and/or community gardens/farms; expand SNAP-doubling and support community-led pantry design.

Title: CareerSTAT A Guide To Making The Case For Investing In The Frontline Hospital Workfore
Authors: Randall Wilson And Robert Hold, Jobs For The Future/National Fund For Workforce Solutions
MainIdea: The U.S. health care system is under pressure to deliver higher quality care at lower costs, with frontline hospital workers playing a critical role. Investing in the education, training, and advancement of these workers strengthens patient care, workforce morale, and long-term institutional performance.
Problems: Despite evidence of the benefits, hospitals often face skepticism from decision makers about investing in frontline workers. Barriers include uncertain revenue streams, resistance to long-term workforce investment, and a lack of clear, shared models and metrics to demonstrate impact.
Solutions: Provide hospitals with adaptable templates and benchmarks for workforce investment, establish recognition programs, and create learning networks to share effective practices.
Implement: Partner with hospitals to pilot training programs, document outcomes, facilitate regional learning networks, and advocate for policies linking workforce development to quality care.

Title: Promoting Racial Equity Helps End Hunger: A Child Nutrition Programs (CNP) Equity Brief
MainIdea: To reduce racial inequlities and hunger--expand summer and winter out-of-school EBT, increase funding for funding for schools and meal sites in low-income sites (to offset kitchen-equipment and staffing and initial summer feeding startup costs), more schools eligible for FFVP, ensure tailoring to nutritional defiiciencies, establish process for community participation, and strengthen diaggregation and collection of data
Problems: Disproportionate hunger and nutritional deficiencies in children of color, excacerbated by inadequate summer and holiday food access and barriers (both financial and infratructural) to expanding food access
Solutions: Opportunities to make the most of existing funding available for schools to close those gaps. CFF can't do much on the paper's policy solutions (legislative power) outside of lobbying--but certainly can help set up microgrant programs to establish infrastuctrure and staff in high-poverty areas and to inform schools of the FFVP
Implement:
Design and implement an awareness and pressure campaign to get schools to apply for FFVP--the Fresh Fruit and Vegetables Program that provides free fresh fruit and vegetables to children at elementary schools where at least 50 percent of students are certified to receive free or reduced-price meals. Many are eligible, but they don't all apply
Set up system to establish transportation systems for children to get to free-meal site during breaks, Establish grant program to facilitate school feeding sites’ kitchen equipment costs, kitchen staffing costs, and initial summer feeding start-up costs.

Title: The Path Out of Poverty
MainIdea: The Inter-American Development Bank's approach to reducing poverty focuses on utilizing data-driven, locally tailored strategies that promote multi-sector collaboration, community engagement, and accountable governance.
Problems: The report does not specifically focus on community foundations (CFs), but it identifies broader problems in poverty reduction efforts such as lack of locally tailored solutions, limited community participation, fragmented sector collaboration, insufficient use of data for decision-making, and weak governance and accountability.
Solutions: While the report doesn’t specifically address community foundations, it provides solutions applicable to them such as adopting data-driven approaches, fostering genuine community engagement, strengthening cross-sector partnerships, and enhancing transparent, accountable governance to effectively reduce poverty.
Implement: By using local data to tailor interventions, actively involving community members in decision-making, building strong partnerships across sectors, and promoting transparent accountability mechanisms to ensure effective poverty reduction.

Title: Lessons Learned from Implementation of the Food Insecurity Screening and Referral Program at Kaiser Permanente Colorado
Authors:
Sandra Hoyt Stenmark, MD; John F Steiner, MD, MPH; Sanjana Marpadga, MSc;
Marydale DeBor, JD; Kathleen Underhill; Hilary Seligman, MD, MAS
MainIdea: Kaiser Permanente Colorado integrated routine food insecurity screening into clinical care using validated questions (Hunger Vital Sign), embedded referrals via EHR to community partners like Hunger Free Colorado, and co‑located nutrition services and a food pantry to systematically identify and address patients’ food needs, improving health outcomes and care engagement.
Problems: Central Florida healthcare systems similarly lack systematic screening for food insecurity, have limited EHR infrastructure for referral workflows, and face barriers like insufficient clinic–community partnerships, staff time constraints, and lack of nutrition services integration.
Solutions: Based on Kaiser’s model, Central Florida could standardize food insecurity screening using tools like the Hunger Vital Sign, build EHR-triggered referral pathways to partners (e.g., Feeding America networks, local pantries), co-locate nutrition services, and use team-based care roles to share referral workloads.
Implement: THRIVE can pilot a clinical screening initiative in select Central Florida clinics: train providers on the Hunger Vital Sign, configure EHR prompts linked to community resource lists, establish formal referral agreements with local food partners, and build a workflow that includes follow-up and monitoring of food assistance uptake.

Title: Alice in Osceola County
MainIdea: ALICE is an acronym for Asset Limited, Income Constrained, Employed. Many households are struggling due to wages not keeping up with the cost of living.
Problems: Household costs are above the federal poverty levels outlined income. 52% of households in Osceola County are ALICE households, higher than the state average (33%).
Solutions: Osceola County can address its high ALICE household rate by expanding workforce training, advocating for living wages, increasing affordable housing, and improving access to childcare, transportation, and financial literacy resources
Implement: Osceola County can address its high ALICE household rate by expanding workforce training, advocating for living wages, increasing affordable housing, and improving access to childcare, transportation, and financial literacy resources. Public–private partnerships, led by groups like the THRIVE working group and the Central Florida Foundation, can coordinate these efforts to lift working families above the ALICE threshold.

Title: Re-Imagining Orlando’s Talent Supply: A Business Perspective on In-Demand Skills
MainIdea: This report discusses how COVID-19 reshaped the local labor market and emphasizes the need for a future-ready workforce. It introduces skills-based hiring as a pathway for upward mobility and identifies both persistent and emerging skills essential for long-term competitiveness.
Problems: The pandemic disproportionately impacted low-wage workers in Orlando, especially women and workers of color, many of whom remain disconnected from the workforce. Employers also face difficulty filling positions due to childcare barriers, health concerns, and job quality issues that limit worker return.
Solutions: CFF can promote skills-based hiring by helping job seekers highlight transferable skills like communication, cultural awareness, and teamwork. Additionally, it can connect workers to training in high-demand areas such as healthcare, cloud computing, cybersecurity, and software development.
Implement: Thrive can operationalize these solutions by partnering with employers and educators to “skillify” curriculum and align job postings with in-demand competencies. It can also facilitate direct pipelines between local industries and workers through credentialing programs and targeted workforce development initiatives.

Title: Challenges of Florida Nonprofits
MainIdea: Florida nonprofits are facing significant financial and operational challenges, worsened by natural disasters and a large population of low-income households. Many organizations lack the resources to sustain operations or respond to emergencies.
Problems: One-third of nonprofits were affected by hurricanes in 2024, and nearly half of those were hit twice in the same year, with 89% receiving no recovery funding. Additionally, nonprofits in Florida have low revenue per household, one-third have no financial reserves, and 30% of CEOs plan to leave within three years.
Solutions: Improving financial resilience through emergency funding, disaster preparedness, and revenue growth strategies could stabilize nonprofits. Strengthening leadership pipelines and retention plans would help mitigate the loss of experienced CEOs.
Implement: Thrive could provide funding assistance, training, and resources for disaster recovery planning. It can also offer leadership development programs and succession planning support to ensure organizational stability in Florida’s nonprofit sector.

Title: The Gap: A Shortage of Affordable Homes
Authors: Raquel Harati, Dan Emmanuel, Katie Renzi, Andrew Aurand
MainIdea: The U.S. faces a severe shortage of affordable rental housing for extremely low-income renters, which threatens financial stability, health, and economic mobility. This shortage disproportionately impacts Black, Latino, and Indigenous households, as well as seniors, people with disabilities, and single-adult caregivers.
Problems: There are only 35 affordable and available homes for every 100 extremely low-income renter households nationwide. No state or major metropolitan area has an adequate supply, and federal housing assistance programs are underfunded, leaving millions at risk of housing instability.
Solutions: Expanding subsidies for deeply affordable housing, preserving existing affordable units, and providing short-term rental assistance could address the housing gap. Sustained and robust federal investment is needed to ensure safe and stable housing for the lowest-income renters.
Implement: Thrive could partner with local housing agencies to support affordable housing development and offer rental assistance programs. It can also advocate for federal and state policy changes to increase funding and access to deeply affordable housing for the most vulnerable communities.

Title: High Frequency Indicators of Economic Recovery
MainIdea: The Orlando Market Overview tracks high-frequency labor and economic indicators to assess recovery and detect contractions in the region. It highlights consumer spending, job postings, labor force participation, and industry hiring trends across Lake, Orange, Osceola, and Seminole counties.
Problems: Job postings fell below 26,000 in September 2021, down 14.7% month-over-month and 7.5% from 2019, signaling hiring struggles despite workforce growth. Labor force participation still lags 1.7% behind pre-pandemic levels, with skill mismatches and “The Great Resignation” contributing to shortages.
Solutions: Consumer spending remains strong, standing 25.2% above pre-pandemic levels statewide and up to 39.1% higher locally, driven by retail, entertainment, and pent-up demand for travel. Orlando also regained over 15,000 jobs, with major hiring in Education and Health Services, Professional and Business Services, Construction, and Leisure and Hospitality.
Implement: Thrive can leverage strong consumer and sector-specific growth to guide workforce development, prioritizing training in expanding industries like healthcare, business services, and construction. At the same time, it can partner with employers to address labor frictions by connecting ALICE workers to opportunities in sectors with high demand but recruitment challenges.

Title: Beyond the Baseline: Orlando Prosperity Scorecard 2021
MainIdea: The Orlando Prosperity Scorecard emphasizes Broad-based Prosperity, which is an economy where opportunities are widely accessible, barriers to participation are removed, and life outcomes are not determined by race or neighborhood. It highlights both pre-pandemic growth and the ways COVID-19 accelerated disparities, setting a baseline for a more inclusive recovery.
Problems: Nearly half of Central Florida households live paycheck to paycheck, even after years of record economic expansion. Median wages remain among the lowest of the 50 largest U.S. metros, with racial and neighborhood gaps in outcomes compounded by pandemic-driven disruptions
Solutions: The Scorecard shows that reducing structural barriers to workforce participation, expanding access to digital opportunities, and improving wages are key to creating upward mobility. Collaborative, cross-sector strategies around housing, transportation, and education will be essential to sustaining a resilient regional economy.
Implement: Thrive can convene business, government, and nonprofit partners to address systemic barriers, aligning workforce development programs with digital economy growth. It can also push for policy and investment in equitable infrastructure—like internet access, skills training, and affordable housing—to make broad-based prosperity attainable for all communities.

Title: The State of Senior Hunger in 2020
Authors: Dr. James P. Ziliak, Dr. Craig Gundersen
MainIdea: Food insecurity remains a persistent challenge for seniors in the U.S., affecting 6.8% of the 60+ population and leaving 2.0 million with very low food security in 2020. Although rates did not spike during the COVID-19 pandemic, they remain significantly higher than pre-Great Recession levels.
Problems: Senior food insecurity has grown dramatically over the past two decades, with a 222% increase in very low food security since 2001. Disparities are especially severe among Black and Hispanic seniors, renters, and those with lower incomes, creating ongoing health and equity challenges.
Solutions: CFF can strengthen senior-focused programming by supporting outreach that addresses racial, income, and housing disparities. It can also invest in tracking food insecurity trends and partnering with healthcare systems to mitigate the health consequences tied to poor nutrition.
Implement: Thrive can foster collaborations with senior centers, healthcare providers, and housing organizations to expand access to nutritious food. It can also advocate for policy changes that sustain SNAP expansions, improve benefit adequacy, and ensure targeted aid for vulnerable seniors.

Title: Expanding the Toolbox: The Whole-of-Government Response to Homelessness
Authors: United States Interagency Council on Homelessness
MainIdea: The federal government’s 2013 shift toward a “housing first” one-size-fits-all model has not reduced homelessness across populations or communities. A new, more flexible strategy called Expanding the Toolbox emphasizes root causes, dignity of work, trauma-informed care, prevention, racial disparities, and alternatives to criminalization.
Problems: Policies that ignore underlying causes of homelessness, paired with high housing costs in over-regulated markets, have worsened the crisis. Artificial redefinitions and narrow approaches provide false progress while overall homelessness has increased despite funding growth.
Solutions: CFF can support multifaceted strategies that prioritize prevention, workforce participation, mental health, and tailored programming for different populations. Partnerships with public and private agencies can strengthen trauma-informed care, racial equity, and affordable housing construction.
Implement: Thrive can promote flexible, outcome-driven funding models that allow local agencies to choose the mix of shelters, transitional housing, and services best suited to their communities. It can also amplify collaboration across government, nonprofits, and faith-based partners to reduce reliance on public assistance while optimizing self-sufficiency.

Title: Affordable Housing in Pittsburgh: A Collaborative Approach to Neighborhood Development in Southwest Atlanta
MainIdea: Pittsburgh, one of Atlanta’s oldest African American working-class neighborhoods, has faced decades of decline due to disinvestment, discriminatory policies, and the foreclosure crisis. The Annie E. Casey Foundation launched a revitalization effort in 2009, using affordable housing development and community partnerships to stabilize and rebuild the area.
Problems: Despite progress, rising redevelopment pressures from nearby projects like the BeltLine and airport expansion threaten to displace low-income residents. Without sustained affordable housing initiatives, Pittsburgh risks losing its long-term residents to rising rents, property taxes, and housing costs.
Solutions: CFF can learn from Casey’s multipronged strategy that combines affordable housing construction, land trusts, community engagement, and legal aid for vulnerable tenants. Partnerships with nonprofits, local government, and neighborhood coalitions provide holistic support, from home repairs to eviction prevention.
Implement: Thrive can adopt collaborative housing initiatives that prioritize both preservation and creation of affordable units while investing in resident-led community development. By leveraging legal support, home repair programs, and land banking, Thrive can ensure long-term stability and prevent displacement in at-risk neighborhoods.

Title: Benefits Cliff Coaching Program Beta Program Learning and Insights
MainIdea: Higher wages can sometimes reduce eligibility for public benefits, creating a “benefits cliff” where total income drops despite increased pay. COVID-19 has worsened financial instability for working families, increasing reliance on benefits.
Problems: 80% of Leap Fund’s clients had never heard of benefits cliffs before. No one knows if they will hit one until it has already happened.
Solutions: Leap Funds Benefits Cliff Coaching Program was proven to help clients in their decision-making. The tools offered allowed clients to identify if they were at risk, and using this information were able to properly plan for their future. CFF can partner with Leap Funds or create their own coaching program.
Implement: An important part of this program was its ability to spread awareness about benefits cliffs. Many people are simply unaware of the concept, and are blindsided when they are stripped of benefits after a raise in income. THRIVE can create outreach events, and partner with Leap Funds for access to their digital tools and calculators.

Title: Map the Meal Gap & Hunger-Health Report
MainIdea: Feeding America issued this report as a data-driven analysis of food insecurity at the county level--linking hunger with poverty, health outcomes, housing instability, and racial disparities and calls for integrated public health and nutrition strategies.
Problems: Food insecurity in CF, with disproportionate effects on communities of color & rural counties;  SNAP/WIC benefits may fall short, especially where food costs are high; insecurity linked to chronic illness (e.g., diabetes, hypertension, obesity), housing instability, lack of insurance; many affected households are above federal poverty line but cannot make ends meet
Solutions: Legislative/policy advocacy for expansion of  SNAP, WIC, and medically tailored food programs (e.g. produce prescriptions); community food banks as partners in diabetes mgmt/education and referrals; "nudge" interventions in pantries to improve nutritional choices; public-private collaboration between food banks & healthcare systems; data tools to guide investment and track impact (e.g. HungerandHealth.org)
Implement: THRIVE can absolutely tackle the food-health connection with its partners--perhaps partnering with local/regional food banks to launch “Food is Health” Clinics offering produce prescriptions, SNAP enrollment, diabetes-friendly groceries on-site at schools, clinics, shelters, etc; “Nudge Pantry” pilots in CF (behavioral design upgrades to pantries that promote healthy food choices); conduct food insecurity screening at ERs, clinics, and schools, using tools like Hunger Vital Sign to ID

Title:
2019 KIDS COUNT DATA BOOK
STATE TRENDS IN CHILD WELL-BEING
MainIdea: Data book examining  national and state-level child well-being data across four domains (economic, education, health, family/community), w/ a focus on demographic changes, disparities, and opportunities to improve outcomes
Problems: Child poverty stagnant since 1990; rising cost burdens on families; persistent racial and ethnic disparities; insufficient early childhood education access; high teen birth and dropout rates; large opportunity gaps for children of color
Solutions: Expand state-level Earned Income Tax Credits (EITC); provide universal preschool and pre-K access; expand Medicaid regardless of immigration status; fully fund Complete Count Committees for accurate census data; expand CHIP and ACA coverage to include all children
Implement: Est a "Complete Count Committee for future census efforts; advocate for adoption and expansion of a state-level EITC; partner with school districts to pilot more free universal preK in high-poverty ZIP codes; push for legislative action to expand Medicaid to cover all children, regardless of immigration status

Title: America’s Women and the Wage Gap
MainIdea: Fact sheet outlining the gender wage gap in the US, especially among women of color, driven by systemic factors like discrimination, occupational segregation, lack of caregiving support, and weak labor protections
Problems: In CF as elsewhere women of color experience some of the steepest wage gaps, especially in service, hospitality, retail, and healthcare sectors; high concentration of low-wage industries in CF worsens economic insecurity for women; lack of paid leave, affordable child care, and workplace protections amplify wage inequality in CF; CF’s tipped workforce (hospitality, tourism) suffers from sub-minimum wages that disproportionately affect women.
Solutions: Local coalitions to advocate for paid family and medical leave policies, affordable childcare, and fair scheduling ordinances+  community education on pay transparency rights and wage discrimination protections
Implement: Advocate for CF municipalities and employers to adopt pay equity audits and salary transparency commitments.

Title: Orlando, FL ranks among least affordable cities for renters
MainIdea: 45% of Orlando renters pay at least 35% of their income on housing, ranking the metro area one of only 16 among 50 revuiewed where more than 40% are cost-burdened by housing
Problems: Housing cost burden; wage-rent gap, with rent increasing by more than 50% over the last decade as wages stagnate and COL pressure intensifies; financial instability for renters, who have less capacity to build emergency savings
Implement: Advocate for local rental subsidies and increased affordable housing supply, especially for those under 60% AMI; track and publicize Orlando's rent-buden data' expand mixed-income/workforce housing stratgies?

Title: The Wage Gap: The Who, How, Why, and What to Do
MainIdea: Factsheet showing data that suggests women, especially women of color, mothers, older women, LGBTQ+ individuals, women with disabilities, and women in low-paid jobs, experience persistent wage inequities, with lifetime impacts on earnings, retirement, and family welleing
Problems: Persistent wage gap across all categories of women; largest gaps for Black women, Latinas, Native women (as low as 55-63 cents per dollar); mothers in CF, as elsewhere, experience compounded wage penalties; high prevalence of women in low-paid essential roles in CF; pay secrecy and lack of pay transparency policies in local workplaces; wage inequity→  reduces CF women’s lifetime earnings and retirement security.
Solutions: Advocate pay transparency policies & pay equity audits in Central Florida’s large employers; increased access to childcare and paid leave to address motherhood penalties
Implement: Work with local chambers to implement pay audits and salary transparency commitments.

Title: The Status of Women in Florida by County: Employment & Earnings
Authors: Julie Anderson & Emma Williams-Baron, Institute for Women’s Policy Research (IWPR) for the Florida Women’s Funding Alliance.
MainIdea: Comprehensive statewide report assessing women’s employment, wages, and labor-force participation in Florida, revealing persistent gender and racial wage gaps, low labor-force participation, and occupational segregation.
Problems:
Women earn 87.5¢ per dollar men earn.
Florida ranks 36th nationally (grade D+) for women’s employment and earnings.
Racial disparities, like, Hispanic women earn only 59% of white men’s wages.
Lack of paid leave, low minimum wage, and gender-segregated industries.
Solutions:
Enforce fair-pay and transparency laws; ban salary-history requests.
Raise minimum wage and expand paid family/sick leave.
Support career access for women in nontraditional and technical fields.
Encourage employer accountability in pay-equity reporting.
Implement:
Launch equity-in-workforce initiatives in partnership with women’s foundations.
Support career-pathway programs for underrepresented women in STEM/trades.
Collect and publish gender-disaggregated economic data for counties.

Title: Navigating Disruption Orlando Prosperity Scorecard 2023
MainIdea: The Scorecard evaluates prosperity in the Orlando region on three quantifiable bases, opportunities, capabilities, and access.
Problems: Income and child poverty gaps between races/genders have worsened; Orlando has lowest median wage among peer metros due to high concentration in low-wage service jobs; large and growing education attainment gaps by race and gender; food insecurity, cost-burdened renters, transit inaccessibility, and digital divides continue to disproportionately affect communities of color; Covid fallout
Solutions: UpSkill Osceola & UpSkill Orlando (skills-based hiring and job pathway creation aligned with local industry needs (e.g., semiconductors); All Women Empowered (AWE) (strategic coalition to elevate women entrepreneurs and close gender income gaps); Black Boardroom Leadership Institute (expanding leadership equity in nonprofits); transit & digital equity Improvements (more internet access, tracking job commutability by public transit); data-driven equity monitoring
Implement: Fund skills-based hiring campaigns and community outreach in partnership with UpSkill Osceola

Title: The Path Forward: Rethinking Solutions for Homelessness in Florida
Authors: Barbara Poppe and Associates, in collaboration with the Central Florida Commission on Homelessness and JPMorgan Chase.
MainIdea: This statewide report examines the root causes of homelessness in Florida and calls for a Housing First approach alongside policy reform and cross-sector collaboration to make homelessness “rare, brief, and one-time.” It highlights economic, legislative, and structural barriers while urging the integration of business, philanthropic, and public systems.
Problems:
45% of Florida households (3.2 million) struggle to afford housing, childcare, healthcare, food, and transportation.
Rising chronic homelessness (+7%) despite national declines.
Insufficient affordable housing: only 1 affordable unit per 5 extremely low-income households.
Solutions:
Adopt Housing First statewide — provide housing first, then services.
Scale permanent supportive housing and Rapid Re-Housing programs for families and individuals.
Incentivize affordable housing for extremely low-income households through tax credits and development mandates.
Integrate state policy and data systems: create a unified HMIS (Homeless Management Information System).
Implement:
Support statewide housing equity advocacy, including functional zero goals.
Launch public education campaigns to reduce stigma and promote Housing First.
