Facilitate grants and impact investments from philanthropic and corporate allies (e.g., JPMorgan Chase, Florida Blue).

Title: Opportunity Knocks: Lessons Learned from Leveraging Opportunity Talent
MainIdea: Employers facing workforce shortages can strengthen talent pipelines by using the "Talent Pipeline Management"framework to intentionally recruit, train, and retain "opportunity population" talent (individuals who face barriers to employment due to factors such as incarceration, immigration status, disability, or economic hardship)
Problems: "opportunity talent in CF" (formerly incarcerated people, opportunity youth, underemployed residents) face barriers to employment (e.g., lack of transportation, child care, legal ID, and access to credentialed training)'
Solutions: Adopt and scale the TPM framework across Central Florida workforce collaboratives, particularly within industries facing persistent vacancies (healthcare, construction, clean energy, advanced manufacturing)
Implement: Partner with organizations like Second Harvest, CareerSource, Valencia, and Reentry Centers to deliver wrap-around services (ID, housing, transit, digital access, child care; create Wraparound Services Fund; incentivize second-chance hiring

Title: Exploring America’s Food Deserts
MainIdea: The report examines U.S. food deserts: areas with limited access to affordable, nutritious food, linking them to poverty, racial segregation, and poor health outcomes.
Problems:
Low-income neighborhoods lack grocery stores and fresh produce.
Transportation barriers restrict access to healthy food.
Racial and geographic inequities intensify hunger and chronic disease.
Solutions:
Support community grocery cooperatives and farmers markets.
Offer tax incentives for supermarkets in underserved areas.
Integrate food access with housing, transit, and health initiatives.
Implement:
Map regional food-access gaps.
Partner with city planners and nonprofits on mobile markets and local gardens.

Title: Four reasons why more public housing isn’t the solution to affordability concerns
MainIdea: Building more public housing is not an effective or scalable solution to America’s housing affordability crisis. She identifies four main reasons and suggests alternative strategies
Problems: Housing affordability crisis
Solutions: Zoning/land-use bottlenecks; public agencies inequipped to build housing; public housing requires long-term committment and long term financial obligations which USG has historiclaly failed to meet; housing vouchers, acquisition/rehab of existing  units/rental assstance

Title: Florida's September Employment Figures
MainIdea: This report covered unemployment statistics for the state of Florida from the month of August 2025, comparing them against the July 2025, August 2024, the year, other states, etc.
Problems: Despite having a lower unemployment rate than the US seasonaly adjusted rate (3.8% v 4.2%), Florida's unemployment rates for August were up 0.1% from the previous month and up 0.4% from last year. There was a slight decrease (less than -0.1 percent) in jobs over the month but the number of jobs rose 0.9% over the year.
Solutions: Allow the unemployment rates to reflect the increase in jobs over the year by providing stronger unemployment resources to and minimizing financial support income gaps to allow and incentivize people to engage in the job hunt.
Implement: Provide access to and promote awareness of unemployment resources, technology to complete applications, etc. Advocate for more supportive and inclusive unemployment benefits and resources.

Title: Cost of Turnover Tool
MainIdea: This Aspen Institute doc puts forth a turnover cost calculation tool and argues that turnover has quantifiable financial, operational, and cultural consequences to make the case for investing in frontline workers, reducing churn through better hiring/training/onboarding, and improving productivity by creating more supportive and stable workplace
Problems: In CF as elsewhere high turnover disrupts operations, inflates labor costs, reduces productivity; indirect costs, customer dissatisfaction, brand harm, shrink, morale; employers lack simple tools to assess the ROI of workforce investments while workers lack upskilling and advancement pathways, leading to churn
Solutions: Implementation of tool: Turnover calculator for estimating/quantifying actual business cost of losing and replacing workers → highlights value of investing in retention strategies (upskilling, stable scheduling, supportive supervision) → intended roadmap for conversations across HR, ops, and finance teams to align around people-centered business practices
Implement: NA - maybe encouraging partner orgs to run/implement tool in HR calculations?

Title: Citi Enviornmental, Social, and Governance Report 2020
MainIdea: Recap for Citi’s 2020 ESG strategy, with key initiatives focused on racial equity, affordable housing, economic inclusion, climate action, responsible business practices, and governance reforms integrated its business model through lending, philanthropy, sustainable finance, community investment, and governance practices
Problems: Persistent racial wealth gaps and systemic inequities in financial access; underserved communities lacking affordable housing and economic opportunity; youth unemployment; climate risks and underfinancing of low carbon transitions; disconnect between ESG statements and corporate accountability; risk of greenwashing or inadequate integration of ESG into actual operations
Solutions: Across all of Citi’s operations: $1 billion Action for Racial Equity initiative targeting banking access, black-owned businesses, and homeownership; $7 billion in affordable housing lending and a $2.5 billion social bond; Community Investing & Development (CID) to align Citi business functions with community needs; net-zero GHG emissions by 2050 and a $250 billion environmental finance target; youth employment programs (Pathways to Progress); ESG factors tied to executive compensation and risk governance.
Implement: Engage local financial institutions on creating “community investing” portfolios aligned with THRIVE goals

Title: Foundational Digital Skills
MainIdea: Highlights the growing need for digital literacy across all occupations and the training gap among many U.S. adults.
Problems: 16% of adults lack basic digital skills; rates are higher among older, less educated, immigrant, and minority populations.
Solutions: Integrate foundational digital skills training into existing workforce programs and assess learner skill levels to tailor support.
Implement: Partner with libraries, workforce centers, and employers to embed digital literacy modules into job training and adult education programs.

Title: Dreaming Out Loud works to create economic opportunities, equitable food system
MainIdea: Dreaming Out Loud (DOL) is a D.C.-based nonprofit that addresses food insecurity and racial inequity by supporting food entrepreneurs of color and expanding access to healthy food in underserved communities.
Problems: Food insecurity and unequal access to healthy food in marginalized neighborhoods; limited business opportunities for minority farmers and food entrepreneurs.
Solutions: Support community-anchored development that promotes local food systems, training for minority entrepreneurs, and partnerships between nonprofits and food enterprises.
Implement: Collaborate with local farms, nonprofits, and training programs to empower underrepresented entrepreneurs; expand access to affordable, nutritious food in low-income areas through community-based food initiatives.

Title: Unpacking Inequities in Unemployment Insurance
Authors: Mońee Fields-White, VIvian Graubard, Alberto Rodríguez Álvarez, Nikki Zeichner, & Cassandra Robertson
MainIdea: Inequitable pandemic effects and recovery efforts have resulted in disporportionate COVID-related death rates, unemployment, small business closures, etc., primarily impacting Black and Latinx individuals and workers. Unemployment insurance is not accessible and Black and Latinx workers recieved UI benefits at lower rates than white workers. This doc aims to unpack the makeup and interactions with UI that may contribute to inherent racism. Communities of color are profiled and criminalized based on racial biases as there is no automated fraud detection system but is rather done by humans.
Problems: Current unemployment systems exclude gig and undocumented workers, doesn't protect essential workers, and reduces low-wage workers' accessibility to benefits.
Solutions: Expand unemployment benefits to cover more workers, ensure paid leave and other benefits, standardize benefit duration to 26 weeks, ensure a living wage, ensure benefits cover a greater portion of workers' wages, and protect the right to safe working conditions. Properly fund the state UI and raise tax obligations on businesses to prevent further cuts in benefits.
Implement: Accurately account for and represent undocumented workers. Organize and/or promote automated fraud detection systems for UI. Point workers towards and make accessible to them resources that can assist them during periods of unemployment.  Provide or advocate for access to internet services and technology to conduct applications.

Title: 2022 County Health Rankings: Florida State Report
MainIdea: 2022 data and rankings for health outcomes and health factors in all 67 Florida counties - interconnection between economic security, health equity, and social determinants of health across the state
Problems: Widespread racial and economic disparities in health outcomes; poor rankings for health factors in several Central Florida counties including Putnam (#67), Osceola (#49), Orange (#33); childcare cost burden far exceeds federal affordability benchmark (27% in Florida vs. 7% benchmark); worse outcomes for rural areas
Solutions: Targeted` interventions for lowest-ranked counties in CF (e.g. Putnam, Osceola, Orange)

Title: Orange County Community Indicators 2021-2022
Authors: Orange County Business Council, First 5 Orange County, Orange County United Way, CalOptima, Orange County Community Foundation
MainIdea: This report explored the long-term impacts of COVID on Orange County (economy, housing, income, education, health, infrastructure, and governance and civic engagement) to guide response recovery efforts.
Problems: Many of the effects felt by Orange County have been reflected in CF as well– primarily decreases in tourism and disporportionate impacts of COVID on Latino or Hispanic communities, lower-income communities, women, and others with few tailored protections or response efforts in place.
Solutions: Idenfify and record indicators critical to CF's economy and community well-being to track responses to crises like COVID. Utilize these reports to better inform and tailor response efforts.
Implement: Advocate for the equitable allocation of resources and recovery efforts to communities often disproportionately affectes by disturbances.

Title: Job Growth in Orlando, Kissimmee, Sanford MSA + The Villages (Lightcast Q1 2023)
MainIdea: Orland-Kissimmee-Sanford metro area and the Villages, cumulatively, is projected to grow by over 90,000 jobs (6%) from 2022 to 2025, with more than 210,000 annual openings, primarily driven by turnover-heavy service roles
Problems: Job growth is concentrated in low-wage, high-turnover sectors like food service, cleaning,  personal care; median annual earninsg in mnay dominant occupations sub 30,000
Solutions: Upskilling and credential pathways for frontline workers in food service, hospitality, and caregiving; investments in career coaching, employer incentives, and retention supports
Implement: Convene employers and training providers in top-growth industries to co-design career pathways, apprenticeship models, wage floor agreements

Title: Adults Who Are Not Digitally Literate: Key Data from the National Skills Coalition
MainIdea: The report analyzes the scope of digital illiteracy among U.S. adults, showing that 32 million adults (16%) lack basic digital skills needed for modern employment, education, and civic life. It underscores the need for large-scale public and private investment in digital upskilling.
Problems:
16% of adults can’t use a computer, email, or simple online task.
Black and Latino adults twice as likely to lack digital skills.
Nearly 60% of adults 65+ lack basic proficiency.
Solutions:
Integrate digital literacy into all workforce and adult-ed programs.
Fund digital skills training at community colleges and libraries.
Require employers to support digital upskilling and mentorship.
Implement:
Partner with CareerSource, libraries, and tech nonprofits to deliver digital-skills programs.
Track digital literacy as a workforce equity metric.

Title: 2017 Florida Statutes - Employer Presumption Against Negligent Hiring (Section 768.096)
MainIdea: Defines employer protections against negligent hiring claims if a thorough background check and due diligence steps were taken before hiring; outlines specific investigation requirements including criminal checks, reference verification, job application disclosures, driver’s record check, and interviews.
Problems: Employers may face liability for employee intentional torts without clear guidelines or understanding of due diligence; inconsistent background screening practices across businesses; lack of awareness about legal protections and required checks; risk of negligent hires leading to community harm; potential gaps in employer knowledge about statute specifics and best practices.

Title: Status of Women in the States
MainIdea: Fact sheet summarizing the economic realities of women in Florida: persistent gender wage gaps, high poverty rates, barriers to opportunity, highlighting how pay inequity, low labor force participation, & poor access to health insurance impact women’s economic wellbeing
Problems: Women in FL earn 87.5 cents per dollar compared to men; hispanic women earn just 60 cents per dollar; FL ranks low nationally in women’s Employment & Earnings and Poverty & Opportunity, CF mirroring larger state trends: high poverty among single mothers, wage gaps especially for hispanic and black women, low health insurance coverage; local data is sparse—CF is lumped into state averages.
Solutions: Expanded pathways to higher-paying, managerial roles, especially for women of color and health insurance access via policy advocacy and enrollment support;  data collection for CF to get more local data

Title: The Pandemic Has Created Two Very Different Kinds of Workplaces. That Especially Matters for Women.
MainIdea: Pandemic labor shifts has caused white-collar employers increasingly tooffer robust paid leave and child care benefits, while low‑wage and part‑time workers, often women and people of color, are left out, exacerbating inequality without universal policies
Problems: Low-incomeworking women in CF may lack access to paid family leave and child care benefits; without these supports, caregivers scale back work, lose promotions,  exit workforce; racial and gender disparities are likely to deepen unless addressed; absence of statewide/federal policy means employer-determined benefits create inequity.
Implement: Establish regional incentive programs to encourage Central Florida employers, especially those in retail, hospitality, healthcare, to offer on-site child care, paid leave packages, and flexible scheduling, backed by local tax credits or county grants

Title: Ending Working Poverty: How to Get It Done
MainIdea: Working poverty persists b/c of low wages, weak labor protections, and systemic discrimination
Problems: Low-wage workers (often FT) remain in poverty, esp in CF retail/service/caregiving; gig economy and contract labor creates job precarity and unstable income; race, gender, disability as strong predictors of poverty
Solutions: CostCo-style models promotion for job redesign (higher prod'y, lower turnover, better service)

Title: Where Are the Jobs? Career Exploration Guide for Central Florida
Authors: UpliftED; CareerEdge Funders Collaborative; designed by students from oTECH
MainIdea: Guide outlining pathways into high-demand, well-paying careers in CF that don’t require a traditional four-year degree--career certificates/stackable credentials instead
Problems: Workforce skills gap, underemployment; educational inequity
Solutions: Promote career certificates; forcus on six key growth industries (Advanced Manufacturing, Construction, Healthcare, Hospitality, Information Technology & Finance, and Trade/Transportation/Logistics)
Implement: Distribute this and similar guides and partner to offer workshops on FAFSA, workfroce funding; peer mentorship programs that connect jobseekers with current students or recent grads from career certificate programs; microgrants to cover gap expenses (childcare, transportation, uniforms) during short-term training

Title: 2023 KIDS COUNT Data Book: State Trends in Child Well-Being
MainIdea: Report sketching out state trends in child well-being
Problems: High, high, high cost of center-based child care in Florida consumes 26% of a single mother’s income; inadequate access to affordable, flexible child care for shift workers and low-income parents; shortage of child care workers and providers due to low pay and burnout' limited subsidy access for eligible families; adverse economic effects of insufficient child care options on workforce participation/wages
Solutions: Expand access to subsidies and simplify qualification processes; co-locate care centers at job sites and colleges; expand Child Care Access Means Parents in School (CCAMPIS) funding; enact long-term federal/state investments akin to ARPA’s impact; expand access to high-quality early childhood education programs like Head Start and pre-K
Implement: Co-fund flexible, nontraditional-hour child care pilot programs; partner with employers to pilot on-site child care at hospitals, logistics hubs, and universities - example: Publix?

Title: Cost of Turnover Tool
MainIdea: This document details the use and role of the cost of turnover tool which helps identify how much it costs businesses to replace staff.
Problems: Small businesses are more susceptible to changes in the economy, political climate, trends, etc.
Solutions: Small businesses can be made more resilient by equipping them with tools like the cost of turnover tool that can help save money with proper budgeting and fund allocation.
Implement: In small business groups/workshops, promote understanding and use of this tool.

Title: Florida Workforce 2030 & Florida Jobs 2030 – Talent Development Blueprint
MainIdea: Report outlining Florida’s strategic plan to lead the nation in workforce education by 2030 - calls for 60% postsec attainment
Problems: FL significant equity gaps in postsecondary attainment; lack of critical skills; disruption from automation, AI, tech; unclear career pathways
Solutions: SAIL to 60 Initiative: 60% of working-age adults to earn a high-value credential by 2030; FTDC: coordinates a statewide, data-driven strategy to align talent supply with demand
Implement: Work with local partners to embed industry certifications and work-based learning across all levels of education (K12-adult learners); advocate for tuition reimbursement, re-skilling programs, and incumbent worker upskilling as part of local employer benefit

Title: Connecting People with Charitable Food through New Home Delivery Partnerships
MainIdea: This doc evaluates Project DASH’s partnership model with anti-hunger orgs for home-delivering charitable food to people facing barriers such as age, disability, lack of transportation, and explores lessons for scaling sustainable food access solutions post-COVID--it certainly increased reach to vulnerable populations but also had shortfalls (tech barriers/access, delivery quality issues)
Problems: Seniors, people with disabilities, and transit limited indvls have outsized barriers to food access; in-person pantry lines may not be accessible
Solutions: Home delivery of groceries improved food access, reduced stigma, and helped clients save money/time/transit costs; enabled nonprofits to expand capacity and reduced barriers for disabled, elderly, and isolated clients; increased flexibility and convenience, particularly for caregivers and people w chronic health conditions

Title: Orlando MSA Market Update
MainIdea: Despite job growth, Orlando faces emerging challenges in consumer spending, hiring, and housing affordability
Problems: Consumer spending softened despite outpacing inflation; early signs of labor market softening; local job loss recorded in December; tight labor market creating recruitment challenges; hiring slower across all industries; housing market cooled; afforability concernstransportation ridership below pre-pandemic levels; job growth skewed toward service industries
Solutions: Strengthened workforce pipeilines; workforce retention and upskilling; affordable housing development and down payment support; expanded public transportation access
Implement: Partner with SunRail and Brightline (?) to embed transit stops into job matching tools; recruitment relief rapid response team task force to help small and medium-sized businesses facing recruitment barriers;  map "invisible commuters" and missing transportation links (GIS + anonymizied phonbe data to show where commyting from) --> data to advocate for SunRail stops, shuttle routes, flexible micro-transit services;

Title: Engaging Apprentice Voice: A Strategy for Promoting More Equitable Programs for Youth
Authors: National Alliance for Partnerships in Equity
MainIdea: Youth apprenticeship programs can promote equity in workforce development
Problems: Many youth workforce initiatives lack  youth engagement in program design and oversight --> culturally disconnected training models, poor retention,  exclusion of young people from marginalized communities; current systems may rarely provide stipends, tech access, or leadership roles
Implement: Create an Apprentice Voice Council in Central Florida modeled after CareerWise Colorado; recruitment startegiesand messaging in high schools; funds by CFF to compensate apprenctices for leadership, advisory, and facilitation

Title: Occupational Talent Pools
MainIdea: Report showing employment and projected 5-year growth data for key occupational talent pools in the Orlando MSA
Problems: Skills gaps and shortages in rapidly growing technical and professional roles; insufficient alignment between workforce skills and employer demand; regional competition for high-demand talent, especially in tech and management; potential underinvestment in moderate- and low-skilled occupations critical to operational functions
Solutions: Targeted training and certification programs to upskill workers for highgrowth roles in tech, management, and professional services; enhance partnerships between employers, educational institutions, and workforce boards to align curricula with labor market demand; invest in talent attraction and retention strategies
Implement: dentify priority occupations for workforce initiatives and funding; Collaborate with regional colleges and training providers to tailor programs around high-growth roles

Title: How to talk about homelessness: getting started
MainIdea: Provides messaging guidance to frame homelessness as a collective, solvable issue through inclusive, human-centered language.
Problems: Public often views homelessness as inevitable or caused by individual failure; stigmatizing language and narrow focus on “rough sleeping” hinder systemic solutions.
Solutions: Use people-first, solution-focused language emphasizing community responsibility; explain systemic causes and inclusive forms of homelessness.
Implement: Apply people-first framing in communications, outreach, and policy messaging; train THRIVE partners to use solution-oriented narratives that humanize homelessness and inspire community engagement.

Title: Finding & Building Paths to the Jobs That Matter for Central Florida
MainIdea: "Jobs That Matter" with strong wages, growth, and diversity potential can drive regional economic mobility in CF
Problems: Rapid skill obsolescence; workforce shortage in key sectors like construction, manufacturing, transportation; Orlando ranks low (92nd) in upward economic mobility; local workers stagnate while talent outsourced; significant racial/ethnic workforce disparities; 65% of workforce lacks 4-year degree
Solutions: Identify and focus training on "Jobs That Matter" that offer good wages, mobility, and employer pain point relief; leverage Valencia College as a pull-up engine offering short-term, skills-based pathways into in-demand occupations; create partnerships between education and industry for on-the-job training and career advancement; target underrepresented talent pools to broaden workforce diversity; implement upskilling programs in sectors like construction, transportation, and claims adjustment; use data-driven labor market info to guide program focus.
Implement: Flesh out task force with Valencia College, local employers, workforce boards, and nonprofits to design and implement modular, skills-based training programs aligned with "Jobs That Matter" in Central Florida; create scholarship and apprenticeship incentives to attract underrepresented populations into these pathways

Title: Improving Employee Engagement: Develop and Energize Your Employees, Create a Culture of Advocacy
MainIdea: Employee engagement is a critical asset driving motivation, productivity, and company success--can be achieved through strong internal brand, consistent communication/modeling, and by encouraging social advocacy
Problems: Low employee engagement can hinder business growth, lower morale, and reduce productivity

Title: Master-Planned Community in Northeast Houston Puts Affordable Housing Within Reach
Authors: John Brannen, Kinder Institute for Urban Research
MainIdea: The Robins Landing development in Northeast Houston,led by Houston Habitat for Humanity, offers scalable model for equitable homeownership, combining affordability, climate resilience, and community infrastructure in a mixed-income, master-planned development
Problems: Affordable homeownership gap and income-restriced access to housing (w/ trad'l marketrate housing failing to serve those earning below 80-120% Area Median Income); vulnerability to climate events; historical underinvestment or community disinvestment in certain neighborhoods and zipcodes
Solutions: Mixed-income, master-planned developments (Robins Landing: 468 homes total; 100 for families below 80% AMI, 300 for families at/below 120% AMI, 68 homes at full market rate); equity building ownership --> multigenerational asset and wealth building
Implement: Model equitable housing developments (Robins Landing as blueprint); advocate for requirement implementation of resilience planning (e.g., elevation, energy systems, stormwater); promote use of tax increment reinvestment zones (TIRZ)

Title: The Gap: A Shortage of Affordable Homes
MainIdea: The US faces a severe, persistent, and growing shortage of rental homes affordable to extremely low-income (ELI) renters;  only 33 affordable and available homes for every 100 ELI households, requiring significant, sustained federal investment, zoning reform, and targeted local action
Problems: Deficit or affordable and available rental homes; disproportionate imapct by race, e.g., 19% black, 17% indig., 14% latino households are ELI renters; severe housing cost burden (72% ELI renters spend over half income on rent/utilities)' pandemic-era setbacks; market failures
Solutions: Reformed zoning and land use to enable multifamily, smaller-lot, mixed-income housing; housing trust funds, bonds, state incentives; ACS PUMS to measure affordability
Implement: Identify high-opportunity areas where exclusionary zoning could be relaxed; city/county use of deep subsidy programs, ARPA, HOME, or general fund dollars to support subsidies/emergency rent assistance

Title: The benefits and costs of broadband expansion
Authors: Sophia Campbell, Jimena Ruiz Castro, and David Wessel
MainIdea: Broadband internet is a valuable resource for bridging the homework gap (as seen in 2020),  increasing job and population growth, and improving health and life outcomes but is not available to a large number of individuals. The Federal Communications Commission found that 18.3 million Americans lack broadband, but independent research groups have suggested that almost 43 million Americans lack access to broadband.
Problems: Inequitable access to broadband internet, homework gap, digital illiteracy, inadequate census and data of accessibility.
Solutions: Promote expansion of broadband efforts. Conduct state-level census of broadband users and accessibility, tailor broadband investments to who needs it and standards that need to be met.
Implement: Partner with independent research groups to conduct surveys and provide data on accessibility. Provide educational resources for digital literacy. Advocate for broadband expansion.

Title: One Good Job: A Strategic Plan to Cut Poverty in Half in Lancaster City by 2032
Authors: The Mayor’s Commission to Combat Poverty (Lancaster, PA)
MainIdea: Presents a community-driven, data-based plan to reduce poverty by 50% through employment, education, housing, and community engagement.
Problems: Poverty linked to structural racism, stagnant wages, high housing costs, limited access to education, and disconnection from resources; systemic barriers and lack of collaboration hinder progress.
Solutions: Emphasizes collective impact, workforce development, quality education, affordable housing, and local leadership. Focuses on job training, pre-K access, post-secondary completion, community trust, and long-term investment partnerships.
Implement: Use Lancaster’s “collective impact” model to unite cross-sector partners; expand workforce programs tied to living-wage jobs; strengthen data-driven policy coordination; prioritize equity, housing, and education linkages across Central Florida.

Title: Jobs That Matter for Orlando
MainIdea: Identifies “Jobs That Matter”: roles in Orlando that combine strong wages, mobility, and diversity potential. Emphasizes upskilling local workers and aligning education with employer needs to boost regional mobility
Problems:
Orlando ranks low nationally in economic mobility (92nd of 100).
Shortages in mid-skill, high-wage jobs; limited training alignment.
Underrepresentation of Black and Hispanic talent in growth sectors.
Solutions:
Create skills-based pathways (e.g., mechanics → inspectors → managers).
Expand short-term credential programs at Valencia.
Build industry-education partnerships to close talent gaps.
Recruit from diverse, skill-adjacent talent pools.
Implement:
Support regional career-pathway pilots with local employers.
Use data dashboards to track mobility and diversity outcomes.

Title: Hispanic homeownership sees steady growth in the US
Authors: Unsure– no details on the article and cannot find online
MainIdea: There has been a significant increase in the number of Hispanic homeowners over the past eight years, with Latinos adding 349,000 homeowner households to the US over the decade.
Problems: High interest rates and low inventory are making for a challenging housing market, but this hasn't limited many Hispanic homeowners.
Solutions: Support Hispanic community education organizations. Increase homeowner accessibility through the continued accessibility of mortgages.
Implement: Partner with Hispanic education organizations to promote academic efforts and the pursuit of college/trade school. Demystify mortgages and the home buying/owning process through education.

Title: The Cost of Connectivity 2020
MainIdea: Examines internet pricing across U.S. cities and identifies a national affordability crisis.
Problems: High monthly internet costs; promotional pricing increases after initial period; additional modem fees inflate bills.
Solutions: Encourage municipal networks or other affordable internet options; reduce hidden fees; promote transparency in pricing.
Implement: Advocate for affordable broadband programs, support municipal internet expansion, and raise awareness of cost-effective options for residents.

Title: Employment by Industry — Orlando MSA
MainIdea: Orlando MSA employment in Jun 2024 totaled approximately 1.47 million nonagricultural jobs; Leisure & Hospitality and Professional & Business Services dominate with nearly 20% each of total employment
Problems: Employment growth is uneven across sectors, with declines or stagnation in some higher-paying industries like Financial Activities and Information; Leisure & Hospitality, a key local sector, shows slight recent declines

Title: Notes on the Future: 2022 Report to the Community
MainIdea: MetroPlan's long-run transportation vision for CF integrating safety, accessibility, environmental sustainability, equity, and technological innovation
Problems: High rates of traffic deaths and injuries, esp among vulnerable users; speed misalignment on major corridors; inequitable access to safe, multimodal travel for underserved communities; limited infrastructure for walking, biking; emissions from road travel; disconnected regional planning across counties and MPOs; insufficient community engagement from underserved populations
Solutions: Implement regional Vision Zero plan and speed management strategies; design, fund Complete Streets projects; expand, modernize pedestrian, bike, and transit infrastructure; improve air quality via Carbon Reduction Program investments; coordinate across a 13-county TSMO mega-region for smarter transportation; fund traffic signal retiming to improve flow and pedestrian safety; develop a regional Active Transportation Plan with a 2050 goal; public outreach tailored to underserved groups; incorporate health equity into transportation decisions
Implement: fund local Complete Streets pilot projects in underserved neighborhoods with walk audits and redesign; pilot “Mobility Rx” program where healthcare providers prescribe transit passes or escooter credits to patients facing transportation barriers

Title: Gender Pay Gap Improvement Slowed During the Pandemic
MainIdea: After years of slow improvement, gender pay gap progress stalled during the pandemic, with women earning 82 cents per dollar compared to men overal
Problems: Promote pay transparency and accountability in CF businesses, encouraging adoption of regular pay equity audits; best practices in pay equity from national models (e.g., Payscale, Visier)
Solutions: Promote pay transparency and accountability in CF businesses, encouraging adoption of regular pay equity audits best practices in pay equity from national models (e.g., Payscale, Visier).
Implement: "Central Florida Gender Pay Equity Report Card” for municipalities and large employers;  organize workshops for CF employers on conducting internal pay equity analyses; advocate for CF government policies linking public incentives (e.g., tax breaks, contracts) to employer pay equity commitments.

Title: Florida Network (Findhelp.org / Aunt Bertha)
MainIdea: A statewide social care platform connecting residents to local resources for housing, food, healthcare, and other essential needs.
Problems: A statewide social care platform connecting residents to local resources for housing, food, healthcare, and other essential needs.
Solutions: Centralized database of 20,000+ programs and 8,000+ community-based organizations enabling residents to quickly find aid and services.
Implement: Integrate Findhelp.org into community outreach efforts; train local partners to refer residents through the platform and track service outcomes to address resource gaps.

Title: Dangerous by Design: Orlando-Sanford-Kissimmee Region and Florida Top Lists as Most Dangerous for Pedestrians
MainIdea: The number of predestrians hit and killed by vehicles increased by 45% in the US from 2010-2019. Our road/city designs are tailored to vehicles, not pedestrian accessibility and safety.
Problems: Metro Orlando-Sanford-Kissimmee are at the top of the daner index list for pedestrian/road safety.
Solutions: This area saw an 18.3% drop in their danger index after adopting a complete streets policy, adding miles of new sidewalks, and enhancing pedestrian signals and crosswalks with audible countdowns, flashing beacons and textured pavement.
Implement: Work with fundraising entities and stakeholders to raise funds for the expansion of the Best Food Forward predestrian safety coalition. Promote predestrian and driver awareness education.

Title: Vast New Study Shows a Key to Reducing Poverty: More Friendships Between Rich and Poor
Authors: Claire Cain Miller, Josh Katz, Francesca Paris, and Aatish Bhatia
MainIdea: A nationwide study found that cross-class friendships—what researchers call “economic connectedness”—strongly predict upward mobility for children in low-income communities.
Problems: Growing economic segregation and limited cross-class interaction restrict social mobility and perpetuate poverty.
Solutions: Foster environments that promote relationships between different income groups through inclusive schools, shared public spaces, and mentorship programs.
Implement: Encourage community spaces, mentorships, and education programs that connect residents across class lines; support local initiatives that build social capital and reduce economic isolation.

Title: Ban the Box: A Fair Chance for Workers With Records
MainIdea: Removing conviction history questions from job applications reduces hiring barriers and creates fairer employment opportunities for people with criminal records
Problems: High unemployment for individuals with records; employers reject many qualified candidates due to conviction stigma; narrowed applicant pools; economic loss due to underemployment of this group; lack of individualized hiring assessments based on qualifications
Solutions: Adopt "Ban the Box" policies removing conviction questions from initial job applications; require employers to do individualized assessments of convictions considering time elapsed and job relevance; allow applicants to review background check results and submit rehabilitation evidence; integrate EEOC guidelines on conviction screening; ensure transparency and accuracy in background checks; promote fair-chance hiring policies for both public and private employers.
Implement: Collaborate with Central Florida employers to implement Ban the Box policies in hiring processes; develop training programs for HR professionals on individualized conviction assessments following EEOC guidelines; create a public campaign to educate employers and applicants about fair-chance hiring benefits

Title: Orlando Area Assisted Housing: Risks & Preservation
Authors: Anne Ray, Shimberg Center for Housing Studies
MainIdea: Analyzes the risk of affordable-housing loss in the Orlando metro due to expiring subsidies and aging properties. Nearly 15% of assisted housing units in the region face affordability expiration by 2032
Problems:
Expiring Low-Income Housing Tax Credits (LIHTC) and federal mortgages.
Aging stock (built in 1990s–2000s) with deferred maintenance.
Market-rate conversion due to rising rents (median 2BR rent: $1,630 vs. LIHTC $1,120).
Limited renewal opportunities for USDA and Florida Housing properties.
Solutions:
Preservation financing, such as, new subsidies, rehab funds, affordability extensions.
Extend affordability period from 50 years to permanent affordability.
Implement:
Create a Preservation Compact with local funders and developers.
Partner with Florida Housing and local trust funds for rehab and affordability extensions.

Title: UNIVERSAL BASIC INCOME IN THE US AND ADVANCED COUNTRIES
Authors: Hilary Hoynes and Jesse Rothstein - National Burequ for Economic Research
MainIdea: This paper reviews empirical & theoretical evidence on the effects of adopting a Universal Basic Income (UBI), focusing on short- and long-run labor supply, human capital investment, child development, and social/political implications
Problems: Existing transfer programs create work disincentives (means-testing, phase-outs, and stigma); welfare system is fragmented, stigmatized, doesn’t always reach those in need; most programs poorly support skill development or human capital accumulation; inequities in child outcomes bc of resource constraints; concerns about long-term economic insecurity, automation, and unmet care work needs
Solutions: UBI → reduce stigma, increase social inclusion, simplify access to aid; may reduce labor supply in the short run, but could support better job matching, entrepreneurship, and human capital development; child health, development, and education - with 2-generation benefits; small income effects on labor supply and positive effects on wellbeing, especially for children; universal nature → ensure higher take-up and political stability compared to means-tested; pilots in Finland, Ontario, Alaska exploring implementation and outcomes
Implement: Use local philanthropic funds to test small, unconditional cash programs targeting caregivers/low-wage workers; track effects on health, time use, employment, and family stability; mixed-methods evaluations capturing both outcomes and lived experiences.

Title: Orlando, FL Ranks Among the Least Affordable Cities for Renters
MainIdea: The report ranks Orlando as one of the least affordable rental markets in the U.S., citing a severe gap between median wages and rental costs. It highlights that renters in Orlando spend a disproportionate share of income on housing compared to national averages, intensifying local inequality.
Problems:
Median rent exceeds $1,800, while median income is below $60K.
Renters spend nearly 40% of income on housing (well above the federal affordability threshold (30%)).
Rising rents outpace wage growth, driving displacement and homelessness.
Solutions:
Expand affordable housing units and inclusionary zoning.
Increase wage growth and local housing subsidies.
Strengthen tenant protections and rental assistance programs.
Implement:
Collaborate with city and county housing authorities on equitable housing plans.
Advocate for living wage ordinances in high-cost regions.
Support impact investments in affordable housing development.

Title: The Racial Wealth Divide in Orlando
Authors: Dedrick Asante-Muhammad, Chuck Collins, Josh Hoxie, Emanuel Nieves
MainIdea: Black and Hispanic residents of Orlando face systemic barriers to wealth accumulation due to low income, lack of assets, housing cost burdens, and historic disinvestment
Problems: White households in Orlando median net worth 23 times greater than Black households; more  than 60% of Black and Latino households are liquid asset poor; homeownership rates  significantly lower among Black and Latino residents; historic/ongoing segregation and disinvestment in communities of color; high  housing cost burden across the region; Orlando  among the most unequal large metros for racial wealth gaps
Solutions: Affordable homeownership and community land trusts in Black and Latino neighborhoods; financial coaching and credit repair programs in communities of color; strengthen renter protections and develop permanently affordable housing; target anchor institution investment (e.g., hospitals, universities) to underserved neighborhoods
Implement: Pilot neighborhood-based asset-building hub offering credit repair, financial coaching, and homeownership assistance; partner with CDFIs to expand lending in historically redlined areas

Title: United Way of Florida
MainIdea: Examines households earning above the federal poverty level but below the basic cost of living (ALICE households) and tracks the economic challenges they face.
Problems: 35% of households are ALICE; 14% are in poverty; wages lag behind costs of essentials such as housing, childcare, food, healthcare, and transportation; significant portion of workers are hourly or outside the labor force.
Solutions: While the report doesn’t propose direct solutions, insights from related studies suggest policies and programs targeting affordable housing, childcare, transportation, living wages, and financial support for low-income and ALICE households.
Implement: Use data to inform local policies and programs addressing cost-of-living gaps; prioritize housing, childcare, and workforce development initiatives; develop targeted support for ALICE households.

Title: Report by the Commission on the Measurement of Economic Performance and Social Progress
Authors: Joseph E. Stiglitz, Amartya Sen, Jean-Paul Fitoussi
MainIdea: This report aimed to improve the state of statistical information about the economy and society, covering the limits of GDP, best indicators of social progress, and the appropriate presentation of statistical information.
Problems: There is a gap between the measurements and perceptions of socio-economic variables due to inaccurent measurements methods or collected variables that don't capture certain phenomena like quality of life.
Solutions: This report emphasizes the importance of measuring both current well-being (economic resources like income and other non-economic aspects) and its sustainability. Focus on trends in consumption, collect info on objective and subjective aspects of well-being, pragmatically measure sustainability, and collect information on environmental pressures indicators.
Implement: Identify and advocate for the societal values held by our communities, uplift scientific voices regarding environmental indicators to consider, involve stakeholders in the identification of priorities, work on developing better metric for assessing economic performance and social progress.

Title: Benefits Cliff Coaching Program Beta: Learning & Insights
MainIdea: This PPT explains Leap Fund’s Benefits Cliff Coaching Program Beta to help clients identify and navigate benefits cliffs (i.e., when earning more money results in a net loss due to public benefit loss) empowering low-income workers to make informed financial and career decisions
Problems: IN CF as elsewhere people may be unaware of benefits cliffs, and fear of losing benefits drives risk-averse decisions (e.g. declining raises or more hours); public benefits systems lack transparency and proactive guidance; ;cliffs force peopleto choose between income and stability
Solutions: Leap Fund trained 270 coaches from 40 orgs → reaching 670 clients with a benefits cliff calculator and coaching tools--the tools revealed hidden benefits eligibility and provided clear, personalized financial projections →  75% of clients using the tool pursued new jobs/hours; 44% made plans to apply for new benefits; coaches IDed optimal points in client journeys to introduce cliffs (intake, graduation, income planning); multilingual and non-citizen-accessible materials were developed
Implement: Train THRIVE-affiliated financial and workforce coaches to use Leap Fund’s calculator to help clients plan job decisions, wage increases, or transitions off benefits; fund a CF-specific version of the benefits cliff calculator that includes state/local policy variables (SNAP, TANF, housing vouchers, etc); financial responsibility public campaign?

Title: The Economic Empowerment Program: 5 Years of Transforming Lives
Authors: Sanctuary for Families – Hon. Judy Harris Kluger, Angelo J. Rivera, Sarah Hayes, Saloni Sethi
MainIdea: EEP, a New York State workforce development program for survivors of gender violence, offering holistic support and structured career training to help participants transition into living-wage jobs, receives its 5-year programmatic review and evaluation
Problems: Like New York State, Central Florida has significant issues with domestic violence, homelessness, and chronic poverty among participants; digital divide, low literacy/English proficiencyy; gaps in childcare, housing, financial support; lack of trauma-informed workforce programming; nreliable or exploitative low-wage work cycles; gaps in public benefits versus true economic stability
Solutions: 15-week, multi-phase training including literacy, Microsoft Office certification, professional development, internships; wraparound supports (e.g., stipends, MetroCards, meals, childcare); trauma-informed, social work-led programming; alumni network and Career Advancement Network; employer partnerships for internships and job placements
Implement: Fund economic “mobility hub” modeled after EEP in Florida/Central FL with emphasis on domestic violence survivors, embedding economic empowerment coaching into family shelter and DV support services; partner with local colleges to offer subsidized certifications (e.g., Microsoft Office, bookkeeping, pharmacy tech) and pipeline → jobs

Title: What Is the Gender Wage Gap in Your State?
MainIdea: Census Bureau brief analyzing gender wage gaps at the national and state levels using ACS 2019 data, including differences by occupation and state-level wage gaps
Problems: In FL women earn less than men across the most common occupations, w pronounced occupational segregation is pronounced (women concentrated in lower-paying occupations like service, retail, and care work); tourism-driven economy includes many gender-segregated, lower-wage roles
Solutions: Leverage the Census interactive tool to generate CF-specific data visualizations for public campaigns?
Implement: Support CF workforce development partners in building gender-focused upskilling pathways?

Title: In Puerto Rico, No Gap in Median Earnings Between Men and Women
MainIdea: Examines gender wage gaps across U.S. states using 2019 ACS data, highlighting occupational differences and narrowing disparities.
Problems: Persistent national gender wage gap (women earn $0.83 per $1 for men); occupational segregation contributes to uneven pay distribution.
Solutions: Use data visualization tools to identify wage disparities by state and occupation; promote equitable pay policies and job diversification.
Implement: Analyze local workforce data to identify pay inequities; collaborate with employers and education systems to expand women’s access to higher-paying occupations and leadership roles.

Title: Hospitality workers need investment in their digital skills
MainIdea: Many hospitality workers lack essential digital skills needed to adapt to rapidly changing technology in the workplace.
Problems: Large share of workers in hospitality and service sectors have limited or no digital literacy, restricting job mobility and wage growth.
Solutions: Invest in upskilling programs, promote industry-led digital training, and leverage federal policies like the Digital Equity Act to expand access.
Implement: Partner with local hospitality employers, workforce boards, and colleges to fund digital literacy programs that prepare workers for tech-driven roles.

Title: Next Steps on the Child Tax Credit
MainIdea: Short term reforms of the CTC should prioritize making the CTC fully refundable, esp for the most vulnerable, without cutting other supports for low-income families
Problems: CTC not fully refundable, excluding nearly 19 million of the poorest children (disproportionately Black and Latino); high-income families still receive full benefits while low-income families do not
Solutions: restore and prioritize full refundability of the CTC, especially for very poor families, young children, and children of disabled or elderly caretakers

Title: Employer Hiring Plans Jump for Class of 2022 Graduates
MainIdea: Tight 2022 labor market indicated growing optimism among employers with 60% rating jobmarket for new grads as "very good" or "excellent"
Problems: 2022 stats no longer reflect hiring reality of 2025
Solutions: Expand college-to-career partnerships between employers and regional institutions like UCF, Valencia, and HBCUs to ensure pathways from graduation to employment are equitable

Title: Where Are the Jobs? – Career Exploration & Credentialing Guide for Central Florida
MainIdea: Overview of high-demand, entry-level career pathways in Central Florida, especially for individuals with a career certificate or associate degree
Problems: Regional skills gap; awareness gap re: high-opportunity careers that don't require a bachedlors' degree
Solutions: Guide advocates for increased awareness and enrollment in career certificate programs, especially in Advanced Manufacturing, Healthcare, Construction, Hospitality, IT/Finance, and Logistics; stackable credentials, on-the-job training, and real wage progression
Implement: Expand awareness campaigns that promote career certificates and apprenticeships as viable alternatives to 4-year degrees; partner with CareerSource Central Florida and local colleges to host hands-on “career preview” events for youth and adults; CREATE MORE DOCUMENTS JUST LIKE THIS ONE - EXTRAORDINARILY GOOD

Title: Connecting People with Charitable Food Through New Home Delivery Partnerships
Authors: Urban Institute (Income and Benefits Policy Center; Center on Labor, Human Services, and Population)
MainIdea: Examines how home delivery partnerships, such as DoorDash Project Dash, can improve access to charitable food for populations with barriers.
Problems: Seniors and individuals with mobility limitations or chronic health conditions face difficulty accessing food pantries; transportation and time barriers; potential lack of sustainable funding.
Solutions: Expand home delivery programs; secure sustainable funding through philanthropy, government, and private sector partnerships; increase delivery frequency and geographic coverage; integrate delivery with complementary services.
Implement: Support partnerships between private delivery services and non-profits; identify long-term funding streams; target delivery services to populations with mobility or transportation barriers; integrate with local food security programs.

Title: Less Poverty, More Prosperity: The Florida Fiscal Cliffs Report
MainIdea: Small income increases– or "cliffs"– in social services often disqualify families from receiving financial assistance. These cliffs may cause people to pass on raises, positions, or promotions because it might disqualify them.
Problems: Poverty in families and lack of economic stability, generational poverty
Solutions: Amend current eligibility policies (particularly in the School Readiness Program) to reduce the "cliff effect," incentivise efforts to increase earnings and reach economic self-sufficiency. Two-generational strategies (focusing on needs of parent and child), early learning interventions and incestments, increase access to transportation and services.
Implement: Advocate for School Readiness Program and other policy amendments to reduce these "hard cliff" effects to include only "soft cliffs" or ideally bridges to provide families with greater access to resources.

Title: First Workforce Data Released for 2021
MainIdea: Orlando region, mid pandemic, faced deep labor market disruptions, esp in leisiure & hospitality
Problems: Massive job losses in leisure/hospitality in Orlando's largest pre-pandemic sector; labor force shrinkage; daunting reskilling scale
Solutions: Diverisification of Orlando economy beyond yourism (e.g., tech, prof/business services, advanced manufacturing); sector=based training, esp for young adults; local talent retention strategies;
Implement: Programs for short-term credentialing and on-the-job learning for displaced workers and scale career transition programs for healthcare, logistics, tech;

Title: Advancing Economic Mobility in our Communities
MainIdea: Shows how community foundations, through the Economic Mobility Action Network, effectively advanced economic mobility by integrating racial equity into their strategies, partnerships, and policy efforts
Problems: Community foundations often lacked the internal capacity, authentic community engagement, data use, and policy influence needed to effectively advance racial equity and economic mobility.
Solutions: Strengthening racial equity capacity, engaging residents as partners, using disaggregated data, collaborating across sectors, and actively shaping publc policy to drive systemic change.
Implement: By embedding racial equity into its framework, partnering with impacted communities, leveraging data to drive decisions, aligning with cross-sector allies, and advocating for policy changes that promote economic mobility.

Title: Map the Meal Gap 2018
MainIdea: The report provides detailed, county and district-level estimates of food insecurity and meal costs across the US. It shows where food insecurity is the most severe and the hurdles to meet basic needs, particularly among children and rural populations.
Problems: Central Florida faces these challenges as well, including high rates of child food insecurity, uneven access to affordable food, and a significant number of residents who are food insecure but earn too much to qualify for SNAP or other federal programs.
Solutions: Central Florida can improve outcomes by supporting community-based food distribution, subsidizing meal programs for ineligible families, and aligning philanthropic and nonprofit efforts to fill service gaps left by federal programs.
Implement: The THRIVE working group can use Map the Meal Gap data to prioritize high-need counties in Central Florida, advocate for equitable food systems, and collaborate with food banks and schools to expand access.

Title: Job Seekers with Criminal Records Get Second Chance with New Law
Authors: Micah Saul, McNees Wallace & Nurick LLC
MainIdea: Pennsylvania’s 2016 law restricts employer access to certain criminal records by limiting background check disclosures
Problems: Professionals with criminal records face very rough hiring market, making re-integration into society more difficult
Solutions: Hring policies that balance second chances with risk management; advocate for or design local legislative frameworks that address background check transparency and fairness; provide legal aid and resources for applicants navigating record sealing or expungement; encourage reentry programs to support formerly incarcerated individuals with training and employment; promote community dialogues on fair hiring and public safety tradeoffs
Implement: Partner with legal aid, reentry organizations, and courts to facilitate applicant petitions for record sealing and educate on the process

Title: Lessons Learned from Implementation of the Food Insecurity Screening and Referral Program at Kaiser Permanente Colorado
Authors:
Sandra Hoyt Stenmark, MD; John F Steiner, MD, MPH; Sanjana Marpadga, MSc;
Marydale DeBor, JD; Kathleen Underhill; Hilary Seligman, MD, MAS
MainIdea: Kaiser Permanente Colorado integrated routine food insecurity screening into clinical care using validated questions (Hunger Vital Sign), embedded referrals via EHR to community partners like Hunger Free Colorado, and co‑located nutrition services and a food pantry to systematically identify and address patients’ food needs, improving health outcomes and care engagement.
Problems: Central Florida healthcare systems similarly lack systematic screening for food insecurity, have limited EHR infrastructure for referral workflows, and face barriers like insufficient clinic–community partnerships, staff time constraints, and lack of nutrition services integration.
Solutions: Based on Kaiser’s model, Central Florida could standardize food insecurity screening using tools like the Hunger Vital Sign, build EHR-triggered referral pathways to partners (e.g., Feeding America networks, local pantries), co-locate nutrition services, and use team-based care roles to share referral workloads.
Implement: THRIVE can pilot a clinical screening initiative in select Central Florida clinics: train providers on the Hunger Vital Sign, configure EHR prompts linked to community resource lists, establish formal referral agreements with local food partners, and build a workflow that includes follow-up and monitoring of food assistance uptake.

Title: The State of Senior Hunger in 2020
Authors: Feeding America, Dr. James P. Ziliak, Dr. Craig Gundersen
MainIdea: Food insecurity among U.S. seniors has grown significantly over the past two decades, with 6.8% of those aged 60 and older food insecure and 2.6% experiencing very low food security in 2020, creating ongoing public health and nutrition challenges.
Problems: Central Florida seniors, particularly Black and Hispanic residents, lower-income households, renters, and those aged 60-69, remain highly vulnerable to food insecurity, which can exacerbate poor health outcomes and strain community resources.
Solutions: CFF can fund senior-focused food assistance programs, expand access to SNAP enrollment support, partner with local food banks and meal delivery services, and support nutrition education programs tailored for older adults.
Implement: THRIVE can coordinate with nonprofits, faith-based organizations, and local agencies to identify high-need senior populations, distribute food resources efficiently, monitor food security trends over time, and develop outreach campaigns to ensure seniors are aware of and can access available nutrition programs.

Title: Dangerous By Design 2022
Authors: Ken Rose, Charles Brown, Rayla Bellis
MainIdea: Pedestrian fatalities in the U.S. reached record highs during the COVID-19 pandemic, driven by streets designed for fast-moving cars rather than safety, with low-income communities and people of color disproportionately affected.
Problems: Central Florida residents, particularly in low-income neighborhoods and among Black and Native American populations, face dangerous walking conditions due to a lack of sidewalks, marked crosswalks, safe recreational areas, and high-speed arterial roads, resulting in rising pedestrian injuries and deaths.
Solutions: CFF can fund projects that redesign streets for pedestrian safety, including adding sidewalks, crosswalks, traffic calming measures, and safe recreational spaces, while also supporting advocacy efforts to prioritize safety over vehicle speed in transportation planning.
Implement: THRIVE can partner with local municipalities, transportation agencies, and community organizations to identify high-risk areas, implement pedestrian safety infrastructure, run public education campaigns on safe walking, and track injury and fatality data to guide ongoing safety improvements.

Title: Dangerous by Design: Orlando-Sanford- Kissimmee Region and Florida Top Lists as Most Dangerous for Pedestrians
MainIdea: The Orlando-Sanford-Kissimmee region and the State of Florida rank among the most dangerous areas in the U.S. for pedestrians, though local efforts like Vision Zero, Complete Streets policies, and traffic safety programs have begun reducing fatalities and improving pedestrian safety.
Problems: Central Florida continues to face high pedestrian injury and death rates due to car-centric road designs, inadequate crosswalks, wide lanes, and insufficient funding for pedestrian infrastructure, putting residents at ongoing risk.
Solutions: CFF can fund initiatives that expand pedestrian infrastructure, such as sidewalks, crosswalks, and traffic-calming measures, support community education programs to improve driver and pedestrian behavior, and provide grants to local organizations advocating for safer streets and equitable urban design.
Implement: THRIVE can partner with municipalities, the Best Foot Forward coalition, and MetroPlan Orlando to identify high-risk areas, coordinate funding for infrastructure improvements, organize community awareness campaigns, and track pedestrian injury and fatality data to evaluate program effectiveness and guide further safety investments.

Title: Orange County Housing For All 10-Year Action Plan
MainIdea: The Housing for All Action Plan is a 10-year strategy for Orange County that seeks to increase housing supply, improve affordability, and create diverse, integrated neighborhoods through regulatory reform, new financial resources, targeted development, and community engagement.
Problems: Central Florida faces a growing population and a housing shortage, compounded by high costs, limited affordable options, and regulatory barriers that restrict the development and preservation of diverse housing types.
Solutions: CFF can support affordable housing by funding nonprofit developers through revolving loan programs, contributing to a local housing trust fund, promoting financial literacy programs for homebuyers and renters, and advocating for policies that remove regulatory barriers to diverse and affordable housing development.
Implement: THRIVE can partner with local governments, community organizations, and developers to identify sites for affordable housing, coordinate outreach and educational programs for prospective renters and buyers, track funding impact, and facilitate collaborations that ensure equitable access to housing near transit, jobs, and opportunity-rich neighborhoods.

Title: Hispanic homeownership sees steady growth in the US
MainIdea: Hispanic homeownership in the U.S. has seen steady growth for eight consecutive years despite market challenges, and Latinos are projected to drive 70% of national homeownership growth over the next two decades due to their young, growing, and increasingly affluent population.
Problems: Despite growth, many Latino households still face barriers such as limited access to credit, high interest rates, low housing inventory, and affordability challenges, particularly for first-time buyers and multigenerational families in Central Florida.
Solutions: CFF can support Latino homeownership by funding down payment assistance programs, financial literacy and credit-building initiatives, partnerships with local banks and credit unions for affordable mortgage products, and community education on homebuying processes.
Implement: THRIVE can identify Central Florida neighborhoods with growing Latino populations, collaborate with real estate professionals and nonprofit housing organizations to run workshops on homeownership, provide mentorship for first-time buyers, and track participation and outcomes to ensure equitable access to affordable housing opportunities.

Title: Improving Housing Affordability and Stability to Advance Health Equity
MainIdea: Safe, stable, and affordable housing is associated with better physical and mental health. Millions of American families, especially families of color, are denied this.
Problems: For the past century, discriminatory housing policies such as redlining and exclusionary zoning have created residential segregation and disinvestment in communities of color, leading to unequal opportunities, poorer health outcomes, and even shorter life expectancy.
Solutions: Expand affordable housing development in historically underserved neighborhoods, strengthen fair housing enforcement, increase federal and state funding for housing assistance, and invest in community infrastructure to reduce segregation’s effects.
Implement: Fund local housing equity initiatives, partner with legal aid organizations to combat housing discrimination, support nonprofits building affordable housing in marginalized communities, and convene cross-sector coalitions to align public and private investments toward integrated, opportunity-rich neighborhoods.

Title: Displaced in America
MainIdea: 5 million Americans lose their homes through foreclosures and evictions each year, causing adverse effects on families and communities. New America and its partners created the National Housing Loss Index, which ranks US counties on their combined eviction and foreclosure rates.
Problems: The findings show that the United States faces a severe housing loss crisis, worsened by the pandemic’s impact on stagnant wages, a shortage of affordable housing, limited federal housing support, and discriminatory policies that fuel displacement.
Solutions: CFF can partner with local governments, nonprofits, and housing organizations to fund eviction prevention programs, expand access to affordable housing, and advocate for policy changes addressing wage stagnation and housing discrimination.
Implement: Use the National Housing Loss Index to identify high-risk counties in Central Florida, coordinate with housing advocacy groups, and direct resources toward rent relief, legal aid for tenants, and community-based housing stability initiatives.

Title: Foundational Digital Skills
MainIdea: The infographic outlines that digital skills are becoming more and more necessary throughout the American workforce, even in fields that traditionally didn’t require them.
Problems: 16% of adults lack basic digital skills, with higher rates for some groups, such as 35% of Hispanic adults, 41% of adults with less than a high school diploma, and 22% of black adults.
Solutions: CFF can fund community-based digital literacy programs, partner with local employers to offer free or low-cost training, and provide grants for technology access in underserved communities.
Implement: Thrive can coordinate with libraries, schools, and nonprofit organizations to host digital skills workshops, connect participants with mentorship opportunities, and advocate for regional workforce policies that prioritize equitable access to digital training.

Title: Hospitality Workers need need investment in their digital skills
MainIdea: As the American job market continues to undergo massive technological transformation, there are some workers who are being left behind. One in ten Americans are employed in the hospitality sector, and a significant part of them are not digitally equipped to handle new technological innovations in the workplace.
Problems: An alarming percentage of American hospitality workers lack the digital skills necessary to keep up in their jobs.
Solutions: CFF could create a grant program specifically for digital skill-building, funding community-based training centers, nonprofit tech-bootcamps, and scholarships for certifications in high-demand digital fields.
Implement: Thrive can partner with libraries, schools, and workforce agencies to host free digital skills workshops, provide loaner laptops and software access, and establish mentorship networks connecting trainees with tech professionals. They can also track participant employment outcomes to demonstrate impact and attract further investment.

Title: The Cost of Connectivity 2020
MainIdea: This is an infographic analyzing the average cost of monthly internet plans across various countries and cities.
Problems: The United States is #1 for highest average monthly internet price.
Solutions: CFF could support initiatives that expand affordable broadband access, such as funding community Wi-Fi programs, partnering with local ISPs to offer subsidized plans, etc.
Implement: Thrive can collaborate with local governments, schools, and nonprofits to pilot free or low-cost internet zones in underserved neighborhoods, integrate internet access into existing community resource hubs, and measure the impact on education, job access, and healthcare connectivity.

Title: Guidance for Workforce Development Professionals on Preparing Employers to Engage in Conversation With Young Workers
Authors: Michael Bradley, Hannah Lantos, and Vanessa Sacks
MainIdea: Young adults are the fastest-growing group of workers, and their perspectives are important. Employers should learn to interact with their younger workers in a way that improves the workplace.
Problems: Employers often create strategies to improve retention and productivity without meaningfully engaging young workers, missing insights into their needs, experiences, and aspirations.
Solutions: CFF can help employers engage young workers through committees, focus groups, and projects that elevate youth perspectives in workplace decisions. Workforce program staff can facilitate these interactions to ensure conversations are meaningful, productive, and reflective of young workers’ needs and aspirations.
Implement: Thrive can partner with employers to build structured youth engagement channels while training staff to effectively guide employer-youth dialogue. By embedding youth-centered design and scaling successful pilot projects, Thrive can ensure young workers’ voices shape workforce practices and policy.

Title: Short Shifted
Authors: Stephanie Luce, Sasha Hammad, and Darrah Sipe
MainIdea: Retailers are increasingly using “lean” scheduling practices, such as unpredictable and just-in-time shifts, which maximize profits but create severe instability for workers struggling with low wages and underemployment.
Problems: Workers face irregular hours, on-call shifts with little notice, part-time limbo, barriers to advancement, wage theft, and difficulty balancing work with childcare or school, leaving many economically insecure despite the growing retail sector.
Solutions: Support policies that mandate predictable work schedules, stronger enforcement of wage protections, and incentives for full-time work; promote union representation and collective bargaining to ensure fair hours, wages, and career development opportunities.
Implement: Collaborate with local retailers to pilot fair scheduling programs, provide resources and training for workers to organize or join unions, advocate for local and state policy reforms around predictable scheduling, and create community workshops on worker rights and income stability.

Title: Women in the Workplace 2020
MainIdea: The Covid-19 pandemic has intensified workplace challenges, particularly for women and caregivers, creating burnout, career disruptions, and heightened stress, while also presenting an opportunity for companies to rethink flexibility, remote work, and employee support.
Problems: Women, especially women of color, face disproportionate job losses, stalled careers, and the emotional and logistical strain of “double shifts,” with 1 in 4 considering downshifting or leaving the workforce, threatening gender diversity and long-term leadership pipelines.
Solutions: Encourage companies to invest in flexible work policies, support managers in alleviating non-critical workloads, expand remote work options, and provide resources for work-life balance and caregiving support.
Implement: Develop programs to help local businesses implement remote work policies and flexible scheduling, offer training for managers on employee well-being and equity, and provide workshops or resources specifically for women and caregivers to maintain career growth and stability.

Title: Opportunity Knocks: Lessons Learned From Leveraging Opportunity Talent
MainIdea: Employers facing labor shortages are broadening recruiting efforts to include traditionally marginalized groups, or Opportunity Population (OP) talent, and using strategic workforce planning to better identify, communicate, and fill critical jobs.
Problems: Despite efforts to hire OP talent, many employers struggle to fully integrate these workers into their workforce, facing gaps in demand projections, credential alignment, and access to untapped talent pools.
Solutions: Encourage employers to adopt data-driven workforce planning, standardize competency and credential communication, analyze and expand talent sources, co-design talent supply chains with partners, and implement continuous improvement processes to diversify and strengthen their workforce.
Implement: Develop programs to help employers forecast job demand and identify critical skills, facilitate partnerships with educational institutions and community organizations to expand OP talent pipelines, and provide guidance on continuous improvement and talent supply chain management to ensure sustained hiring success.

Title: Designing Career and Technical Education Programs that Help Students Get Good Jobs
MainIdea: Postsecondary education programs, particularly short-term career and technical education (CTE) programs, must be workforce-aligned to help students, especially nontraditional and underserved learners, secure good jobs with family-sustaining wages and opportunities for advancement.
Problems: Many college graduates struggle to find well-paying jobs in their field, while employers face difficulty filling entry-level positions; structural barriers, low income, and high debt hinder nontraditional students’ access to education and career pathways.
Solutions: Design programs that center students’ workforce goals, align curricula with local labor market demand, provide enhanced support for nontraditional learners, and involve employers in shaping program content to ensure students gain relevant skills.
Implement: Partner with educational institutions to implement workforce-aligned program frameworks, provide resources and guidance for student-centered design, and facilitate collaborations between colleges and employers to expand pathways for diverse learners into high-demand jobs.

Title: Rethinking Gender Pay Inequity in a More Transparent World
Authors: Mita Goldar, Christopher Ryan, and Ahu Yildirmaz
MainIdea: Gender pay disparities persist in the U.S. workforce, driven by differences in base pay and incentive pay at hiring and amplified over time, creating long-term earnings gaps between men and women.
Problems: Women are more likely to start in lower-paying jobs, receive smaller bonuses, and face systemic biases in recruitment, promotion, and performance evaluation, which cumulatively limit career advancement and total earnings.
Solutions: Employers should identify and address pay inequities at hiring, establish transparent incentive and bonus structures, and implement equitable promotion and performance review practices to reduce systemic gender bias.
Implement: Thrive can provide data-driven insights and tools to track pay equity, advise organizations on best practices for equitable compensation and incentives, and support implementation of transparent promotion and bonus policies to ensure fair treatment of all employees.

Title: CareerSource Central Florida Regional Skills Analysis
MainIdea: CareerSource Central Florida (CSCF) connects residents to careers while developing skilled talent for businesses. The region is rapidly growing, highly diverse, and faces both workforce shortages and equity challenges.
Problems: Central Florida faces an aging workforce, post-pandemic disruptions, and a mismatch between education and employer demand. Many workers are underemployed or stuck in low-wage jobs, with people of color disproportionately represented.
Solutions: CSCF can use Lightcast data to identify skill gaps and promote career pathways in high-demand fields such as Business & Finance, Education & Library Services, and IT & Math. These sectors offer good wages, growth potential, and multiple entry points for a diverse range of workers.
Implement: Thrive can expand training and reskilling programs tailored to high-growth sectors while increasing outreach to underrepresented communities. By partnering with employers, Thrive can create accessible entry-level opportunities, advancement tracks, and equity-focused workforce initiatives.

Title: Voices from the Field: Engaging Employers to Connect Young Adults to Good Fit Employment
Authors: Ranita Jain, Vivian Vázquez, & Amy Blair, The Aspen Institute Economic Opportunities Program
MainIdea: The report highlights workforce development practices based on surveys from professionals in three Generation Work communities, focusing on how they engage employers about job opportunities and workplace practices. It emphasizes the urgent need to connect young adults, especially from marginalized groups, to sustainable employment pathways in a post-pandemic labor market.
