Problems: Young adults, particularly Black, Latinx, and Indigenous populations, face disproportionately high disconnection rates from school and work, which worsened during COVID-19. Many of these workers held low-wage service jobs that have since disappeared, leaving them with limited opportunities and financial hardship.
Solutions: Workforce providers must prioritize helping young adults evaluate employment quality (wages, benefits, scheduling, safety, and career advancement) alongside job placement. Engaging employers more deeply on DEI practices can also help ensure that opportunities are equitable and sustainable.
Implement: Thrive can build employer partnerships that prioritize quality job standards while creating transparent pipelines into growth industries for disconnected youth. It can also strengthen its role in DEI by training staff to consistently ask employers about workforce composition, hiring goals, and equitable advancement opportunities.

Title: Glassdoor Workplace Trends for 2022
MainIdea: The workplace has shifted dramatically due to the pandemic, and 2022 is about adapting to the “new normal.” Employees now hold more power in a tight labor market, which forces companies to rethink hiring, engagement, and workplace culture.
Problems: Hiring talent will remain difficult in 2022, with shortages and competition driving challenges. In addition, remote work raises costs while employee burnout and turnover remain high.
Solutions: CFF can address these challenges by focusing on inclusive hiring, strengthening DEI accountability, and offering career pathways that emphasize growth and retention. Expanding access to remote work opportunities and building strong community connections can also increase talent engagement.
Implement: Thrive can support CFF by helping design data-driven hiring strategies and employer branding to compete for talent. It can also operationalize solutions by facilitating DEI initiatives, developing virtual training programs, and creating community-building efforts that extend beyond the workplace.

Title: Social Determinants of Health: Economic Stability
MainIdea: Economic stability, a key social determinant of health (SDOH), directly impacts access to healthcare, quality of life, and overall well-being. Factors like employment, income, housing stability, and food security shape health outcomes across populations.
Problems: Unemployment and underemployment are linked to higher rates of chronic illness, mental health issues, and food insecurity, particularly among children. Lack of access to health benefits such as sick leave, maternity leave, and insurance deepens healthcare inequalities.
Solutions: CFF can support programs that connect individuals to steady employment and advocate for policies that expand access to employer-based or marketplace insurance. Promoting affordable housing, nutritious food access, and career counseling can strengthen economic stability and improve health equity.
Implement: Thrive can operationalize these solutions by creating partnerships with employers to expand benefit programs and offering workforce training that builds long-term job stability. It can also collaborate with local organizations to address food insecurity and housing challenges while ensuring community members are connected to healthcare resources.

Title: Homeless Shelters & Their Neighbors: Neighborhood opposition to temporary housing facilities and addressing the homelessness crisis in New York City
MainIdea: Homeless shelters are a vital resource in addressing New York City’s homelessness crisis, but public perception and flawed studies have fueled stigma and resistance to their expansion. Despite claims that shelters harm property values, evidence suggests that the issue stems more from stigma than from the shelters themselves.
Problems: The IBO study framed homeowners as the injured party, reinforcing stigma against shelters and their residents. Even as the city expands shelters, neighborhood opposition, including lawsuits and protests, creates barriers to implementing effective housing solutions.
Solutions: CHPC recommends reframing homelessness policy to center on helping New Yorkers exit poverty, rather than prioritizing property value concerns. Further, CFF can advocate for research and education that challenge stigma, while supporting shelter expansion in underserved neighborhoods.
Implement: Thrive can build community education campaigns to normalize shelters as essential infrastructure, reducing neighborhood opposition. It can also support coalition-building with local leaders, residents, and policymakers to align housing solutions with long-term poverty alleviation goals.

Title: Strategies to Support Young People’s Access to Public Benefits
Authors: Amelia Coffey, Laura Wagner, Michelle Casas, Heather Hahn
MainIdea: Many young people ages 16–24 transition to adulthood without sufficient resources for food, housing, or health care. Public benefit programs like SNAP, Medicaid, and TANF can provide stability, but many eligible youth struggle to access them.
Problems: Barriers include complex eligibility rules, lack of awareness, and administrative burdens that discourage enrollment. These challenges are especially severe for Black and Latinx youth, young parents, and those who have experienced foster care, homelessness, or the justice system.
Solutions: Promising strategies include targeted youth outreach, benefit navigation, cross-organizational partnerships, simplifying or expanding eligibility, and reducing administrative barriers. Comprehensive service models that combine multiple strategies show the strongest evidence of effectiveness.
Implement: Thrive can partner with public agencies and nonprofits to provide youth-centered outreach, navigation assistance, and coordinated services. It can also advocate for policy reforms that expand eligibility, streamline applications, and prioritize human-centered administrative practices.

Title: Florida Chamber Foundation’s Path to Prosperity: Seminole County
MainIdea: The Florida Chamber Foundation’s Path to Prosperity initiative uses data tools like the Scorecard, Heat Maps, and Gap Map to identify root causes of poverty at the zip code level. Their approach aims to ensure that zip code, race, or background no longer determine opportunity in Florida.
Problems: In Seminole County, 9,785 children live in poverty, with half concentrated in just four zip codes, and 55% of families are housing-cost burdened. Additionally, 40% of third graders are not reading at grade level, and 43% of households fall below the ALICE threshold, highlighting systemic barriers.
Solutions: The Prosperity Project identifies ten root causes of poverty, including education, housing, health, food security, transportation, and family care. By addressing each root cause at its source, particularly in high-need zip codes, the initiative seeks to cut childhood poverty in half by 2030.
Implement: Thrive can apply data-driven tools to pinpoint local needs, then align programming to tackle root causes such as early literacy, housing insecurity, and food access. Partnering with the business community and local organizations, Thrive can help replicate the Prosperity Project model to break cycles of generational poverty.

Title: Orlando MSA Market Update Q4 2024
MainIdea: The Orlando region experienced strong economic momentum at the close of 2024, with record-low unemployment, rising consumer sentiment, and major corporate investments. Significant projects like Universal’s Epic Universe opening, Nemours’ expansion, and United Airlines’ new facility, show a climate of business growth and infrastructure development.
Problems: Despite economic growth, home sales and housing affordability remain challenges, as mortgage rates stayed high and overall sales fell 7% from 2023. Additionally, while jobs are being added, many households still face barriers to prosperity due to rising housing costs and uneven access to new opportunities.
Solutions: CFF can use momentum from corporate expansions and infrastructure growth to promote workforce readiness programs that align with emerging industries. Strengthening partnerships with transit systems like SunRail can also expand access to new jobs, particularly for workers in underserved communities.
Implement: Thrive can operationalize these solutions by creating targeted training initiatives in sectors where jobs are growing, such as healthcare, finance, and aviation. It can also advocate for affordable housing and expanded transit routes to ensure economic mobility is accessible to ALICE households and low-income families.

Title: Public Participation Plan: 2050 Metropolitan Transportation Plan
MainIdea: MetroPlan Orlando is developing the 2050 Metropolitan Transportation Plan (MTP), set for completion in December 2025, to shape Central Florida’s future transportation system. The plan emphasizes technical analysis and extensive public participation to ensure a system that serves residents, visitors, and underserved communities.
Problems: Planning for 2050 requires addressing rapid regional growth, rising costs, and equitable access to transportation. Transportation-underserved communities risk being left out of decision-making unless inclusive engagement strategies are prioritized.
Solutions: MetroPlan Orlando proposes three main objectives: inclusive engagement, community events and outreach, and ensuring public input reaches decision-makers. Strategies include partnerships with community groups, accessible materials, creative outreach methods, and regular reporting of public sentiment.
Implement: Thrive can align with these objectives by leveraging its community networks to amplify engagement, particularly in ALICE and underserved populations. It can also act as a bridge between residents and policymakers by collecting grassroots feedback, sharing it with MetroPlan decision-makers, and ensuring transparency in how public input shapes the 2050 Plan.

Title: Employment by Industry
MainIdea: Orlando’s MSA closed 2024 with 1.5 million nonagricultural jobs, marking a 1.1% year-over-year gain. The largest sectors are leisure & hospitality (19.4%) and professional & business services (18.9%).
Problems: Several industries lost jobs in 2024, including manufacturing (-3.0%), retail trade (-1.2%), and professional & business services (-0.5%). These declines show that job growth is uneven and could leave workers in vulnerable positions.
Solutions: CFF can emphasize workforce programs in growing sectors like construction (+6.7%) and transportation/warehousing/utilities (+2.3%). Reskilling programs can help redirect workers from shrinking industries toward fields with rising demand.
Implement: Thrive can partner with employers and training providers to create career pipelines in high-growth industries. It can also connect displaced workers to upskilling opportunities that align with Central Florida’s future economy.

Title: Social Determinants of Health: Social & Community Context December 2024
MainIdea: Social and community context, a key social determinant of health (SDOH), significantly influences physical and mental well-being. Strong social relationships, community engagement, and supportive family structures are linked to better overall health outcomes.
Problems: Many U.S. adults report declining social support, with fewer discussing health concerns with friends or family, increasing vulnerability to stress and health problems. Community-level challenges like obesity, adolescent bullying, and limited civic participation further exacerbate disparities in health and well-being.
Solutions: Programs that strengthen social networks, encourage civic participation, and promote healthy community environments can improve health outcomes. Interventions like school-based physical activity, BMI screenings, and community health initiatives have proven effective in fostering healthier communities.
Implement: Thrive can support initiatives that provide youth and families with social, educational, and community resources, including mentorship and recreational programs. It can also partner with local organizations to increase civic engagement and community-driven health programs that target underserved populations.

Title: Beyond Dollars and Cents: Exploring Budgeting, Saving, and Financial Security in the Houston Area
Authors: Joy Njeh, Aaron Niznik, and Dan Potter
MainIdea: Financial security in Harris County, Texas, depends on individuals’ ability to cover daily expenses and withstand economic shocks. Saving regularly, rather than simply budgeting, is strongly linked to greater financial security across income and racial/ethnic groups.
Problems: Many residents live paycheck to paycheck, with nearly half unable to cover a $400 emergency expense, and minority populations disproportionately affected. Despite widespread budgeting, only one-third of residents report being financially secure, highlighting the limits of budgeting alone.
Solutions: Encouraging and supporting regular saving practices can increase financial security for residents across income levels. Programs should combine savings strategies with other financial tools and guidance to help households withstand economic shocks and build resilience. Similar research can be done in Florida to compare.
Implement: Thrive can partner with local nonprofits and community organizations to provide accessible savings programs and financial literacy resources. It can also design initiatives that help residents track progress toward financial security, even if full security is not immediately attainable.

Title: Interim Strategies for Responding to Unsheltered Homelessness
MainIdea: Permanent housing is the only long-term solution to homelessness, while interim strategies can provide temporary safety and support. These strategies should be evidence-based, low-barrier, and housing-focused, ensuring people can access immediate shelter without coercion.
Problems: Unsheltered homelessness is rising nationwide due to a lack of affordable housing, stagnant incomes, inadequate healthcare, and systemic discrimination. Short-term responses that prioritize making homelessness less visible, such as criminalizing encampments, worsen conditions for vulnerable populations.
Solutions: Interim strategies should create safe, voluntary spaces that meet immediate health and service needs while connecting people directly to permanent housing. Long-term solutions require culturally appropriate supportive services paired with affordable housing to address systemic barriers.
Implement: Thrive can develop or support programs that provide temporary, low-barrier shelter while facilitating access to permanent housing options. It can also advocate for policies that expand affordable housing, ensure voluntary supportive services, and prevent criminalization of people experiencing homelessness.

Title: Universal Income: What’s in a Name?
MainIdea: Universal Basic Income (UBI) has gained renewed attention as a policy solution to address economic insecurity, job displacement from automation, and systemic inequities. It is being experimented with globally, with diverse designs and implementation strategies shaping lessons for future large-scale programs.
Problems: Workers face threats from automation, rising job precariousness, and persistent racial and gender inequities, which expose gaps in the existing U.S. safety net. The COVID-19 pandemic further revealed weaknesses in support systems, underscoring the need for regular, unconditional income transfers.
Solutions: Experiments in UBI focus on unconditional cash transfers paired with wrap-around services, community engagement, and careful monitoring and evaluation to determine best practices. Choosing clear, consistent naming for the policy, such as “Basic Income” or “Universal Basic Income,” can strengthen communication, reduce confusion, and build broader public understanding.
Implement: Thrive can collaborate with research institutions, policymakers, and community organizations to pilot or analyze UBI programs that target vulnerable populations. It can also support public education campaigns to clarify the objectives and design of UBI, emphasizing inclusivity, equity, and measurable outcomes.

Title: The 2020 Hispanic Market Report
MainIdea: Hispanic consumers in the U.S. are a fast-growing and economically influential population, driving both population growth and consumer spending. Their digital savvy, cultural connections, and increasing earning potential position them as key contributors to economic recovery and business growth.
Problems: Hispanics have been disproportionately impacted by health and economic challenges, especially during crises like the COVID-19 pandemic. Companies that fail to engage with this community in culturally relevant ways risk missing out on a significant and growing consumer segment.
Solutions: Businesses should adopt culturally relevant marketing strategies and leverage social media platforms to educate, inform, and connect with Hispanic consumers. Investments in education and workforce development for Hispanics can further increase their earning potential, strengthening economic contributions over time.
Implement: Thrive can support programs that promote higher education and professional development for Hispanic communities to maximize earning potential. Additionally, Thrive can partner with businesses to provide guidance on culturally relevant engagement and digital marketing strategies to reach and empower Hispanic consumers.

Title: Citrus to Brevard: Community Needs of Central Florida
Authors: Kimberly Sanchez, Jeffrey Harvey, Lisa Witkowski
MainIdea: Community Legal Services of Mid-Florida (CLSMF) identified affordable housing and living-wage employment as the two most urgent needs in their 12-county region. Additional concerns include family stability, education, healthcare, transportation, and physical safety.
Problems: Nearly half of Florida households live in poverty or are ALICE, struggling to afford basic needs like housing, childcare, and transportation. Low wages dominate the state, with 67% of jobs paying under $20/hour, worsening inequality, health crises, and “deaths of despair.”
Solutions: CFF can address these systemic gaps by supporting housing access, transportation programs, and workforce development. It can also strengthen community well-being through health, counseling, and navigational support services.
Implement: Thrive can fund and scale programs that expand affordable housing, improve transportation access, and connect residents to living-wage employment. It can also build partnerships for wraparound services, like financial literacy, healthcare, and counseling, that untangle clients’ “hairball” of challenges.

Title: Covid and Financial Hardship in Florida
MainIdea: Nearly half of Florida households, 3.9 million, or 45%, lived below the ALICE Threshold in 2021, meaning they could not afford basic survival needs. These households include both those in poverty and the working poor who earn above the federal poverty line but still fall short of a livable wage.
Problems: Wages remain too low to match the rising cost of essentials, with 70% of common Florida jobs paying under $20/hour and many workers, like cashiers, unable to meet survival budgets even full time. Disparities also persist, as Black, Hispanic, Native, young, elderly, and single-parent households are disproportionately below the threshold.
Solutions: CFF can advocate for stronger wage growth, workforce training, and affordable childcare to reduce barriers for struggling households. It can also invest in housing stability, emergency savings programs, and equitable resource access to close racial, age, and family-structure gaps.
Implement: Thrive can expand partnerships to deliver financial literacy, savings incentives, and workforce pathways that help ALICE families build assets over time. It can also design programs that combine immediate relief (housing, food, healthcare access) with long-term stability strategies to prevent recurring hardship.

Title: The American Opportunity Index: A Corporate Scorecard of Worker Advancement
Authors: Matt Sigelman, Joseph Fuller, Nik Dawson, Gad Levanon
MainIdea: The American Opportunity Index is a new corporate scorecard that measures how well the 250 largest U.S. firms foster economic mobility, especially for workers without college degrees. It evaluates outcomes like access, pay, and advancement using data from over 3 million workers, job postings, and salaries.
Problems: Not all companies create equal opportunity: workers at bottom-quintile firms earn up to 2.5 times less and advance nearly three times slower than peers at top firms. Gaps also exist in access, as firms in lower quintiles are far less likely to hire workers without degrees or significant experience.
Solutions: The Index provides actionable benchmarks, such as promotion within five years, 70% retention at five years, and wages at least 40% above occupational medians. By highlighting top-performing firms, it shows that business practices—not just sector—drive worker outcomes, offering models for replication.
Implement: Thrive can use the Index to identify strong employer partners who demonstrate proven mobility pathways and prioritize access for ALICE households. It can also align training, advocacy, and workforce initiatives around the benchmarks (promotion speed, retention, and fair wages) to better prepare workers for advancement.

Title: Orange County Community Action Division Community Needs Assessment
MainIdea: The Community Needs Assessment provides a comprehensive analysis of public health, education, housing, and economic data to guide Orange County’s Community Action Division. Its goal is to strengthen existing programs and plan future services that support economic self-sufficiency, especially for residents below the federal poverty level.
Problems: Despite overall prosperity, neighborhoods like Holden Heights (Distress Score 91.8) and the Hal Marston area remain among the most distressed in the nation, with high poverty, housing vacancy, and low business and employment growth. Housing affordability is a major barrier, as rents are $127 higher than the national average while local incomes are over $6,000 lower.
Solutions: CFF can expand housing stability programs, such as rental assistance, financial literacy workshops, and partnerships for affordable housing development. It can also scale up education and workforce supports—like GED, ESOL, and digital literacy programs—to reduce barriers to long-term self-sufficiency.
Implement: Thrive can target investment and partnerships in distressed neighborhoods, aligning resources with data-identified priority areas like Holden Heights and Hal Marston. It can also coordinate countywide efforts in housing, workforce development, and small business growth to ensure equitable opportunities across all communities.

Title: Kids, Families and Covid-19
MainIdea: Before COVID-19, children and families in America were making steady gains in economic well-being, health, and education, though progress had slowed and racial disparities persisted. The pandemic reversed much of that progress, deepening inequities and threatening children’s health, stability, and educational outcomes.
Problems: COVID-19 devastated family stability: millions lost jobs, income, health care, and housing security, while parents struggled to balance work, child care, and online schooling. Communities of color bore a disproportionate toll, both in health outcomes and in economic vulnerability, magnifying systemic inequities.
Solutions: Federal, state, and local leaders should center racial equity in policymaking, expand access to affordable health care (including mental health), and guarantee vaccines for all. Families need strengthened supports through expanded unemployment insurance, child allowances, housing vouchers, TANF reform, child care stabilization, and tax credits.
Implement: Thrive can partner with local schools, health providers, and community organizations to expand mental health access, technology equity, and child care support in Orange County. It can also embed racial equity benchmarks into program design, align with federal relief opportunities, and focus on neighborhoods where families are most at risk of housing and income insecurity.

Title: Communities That Work Partnership Playbook
Authors: Amy Blair, Stephen Michon, and Maureen Conway
MainIdea: The Communities that Work Partnership (CTWP), led by Aspen Institute and FutureWorks, highlights regional strategies to align workforce development, education, and business needs. Their goal is to create talent pipelines that increase worker skills, strengthen local economies, and support business growth.
Problems: Workforce programs are often siloed, serving limited industries, geographies, or populations, which restricts broader impact. Businesses, workers, and development efforts are frequently disconnected, leaving gaps in support for both employers and job seekers.
Solutions: CTWP emphasizes cross-sector collaboration, industry-specific training, and partnerships with schools and colleges to expand access to credentials and career advancement. It also recommends strategies to support contingent workers through financial skills, business management, and engagement with contracting businesses.
Implement: Thrive can replicate CTWP’s model by fostering regional partnerships that bridge business, education, and workforce systems. It can also develop programs for youth, contingent workers, and underrepresented groups while scaling proven models across industries.

Title: Low-Income and Low-Foodstore-Access Census Tracts, 2015–19
Authors: Alana Rhone, Ryan Williams, and Christopher Dicken
MainIdea: The USDA report updates data on food access by measuring low-income (LI), low-access (LA), and combined low-income low-access (LILA) census tracts between 2015 and 2019. While income and vehicle access improved nationally, many households still face barriers to healthy and affordable food.
Problems: Limited access to supermarkets, supercenters, and grocery stores continues to affect low-income and SNAP households, with 33% living over a mile from a foodstore. Barriers such as income constraints, vehicle availability, and urban proximity to foodstores persist, particularly in vulnerable communities.
Solutions: Expanding healthy food access requires addressing proximity challenges, supporting vehicle-less households, and improving local retail options in both urban and rural areas. Additional research on dollar stores and combination retailers could identify alternative sources of nutritious food in underserved tracts.
Implement: Thrive can invest in community partnerships to increase the presence of affordable food retailers in low-access areas. It can also support mobile markets, transportation assistance, and research on alternative store models to better serve food deserts.

Title: Feeding the Line, or Ending the Line?
MainIdea: Food banks across the country are undergoing a paradigm shift, broadening their missions beyond food distribution to include health, education, workforce development, and community empowerment. They are adopting holistic approaches, building partnerships, and aiming to address the root causes of hunger rather than just feeding the line.
Problems: Challenges include outdated metrics focused only on pounds of food distributed, which fail to capture the broader impact of innovative programs. Food banks also face pushback over mission alignment, funding gaps for innovation, and limited capacity to influence broader public policy.
Solutions: CFF can support food banks by promoting new impact metrics such as jobs created, health outcomes, and community resilience. It can also help secure flexible, long-term funding and encourage policy advocacy that addresses systemic drivers of hunger.
Implement: Thrive can build cross-sector collaborations with farmers, healthcare providers, and local businesses to strengthen healthy food infrastructure. It can also invest in pilot programs, support evaluation research with universities, and advocate for policies that integrate food access with housing, wages, and childcare.

Title: Housing and the Role of Hospitals
MainIdea: Wellness and well-being are closely tied to social and economic conditions, with up to 40% of health outcomes shaped by nonmedical factors such as income, housing, and food access. Hospitals and health systems are beginning to address these social determinants of health through housing, education, and community partnerships.
Problems: Individuals facing poverty, housing instability, and food insecurity often experience worse health, higher care utilization, and rising medical costs. Traditional payment models have not supported addressing these needs, leaving health providers limited in their ability to intervene.
Solutions: Hospitals across the U.S. have created innovative programs such as affordable housing units, healthy homes initiatives, medical respite programs, and Housing First partnerships. These efforts show that integrating social supports with medical care improves health, lowers costs, and strengthens community well-being.
Implement: Thrive can collaborate with health systems, local governments, and community groups to build housing interventions tied to health outcomes. By investing in affordable housing, supportive services, and resource navigation, Thrive can advance population health while stabilizing vulnerable households.

Title: Regional Affordable Housing Initiative
Authors: Orange County, City of Orlando, Seminole County, Osceola County
MainIdea: Orange, Seminole, Osceola counties and the City of Orlando launched the Regional Affordable Housing Initiative to address Central Florida’s worsening housing crisis. The effort emphasizes collaboration, diverse strategies, and sustained monitoring to expand affordable housing options.
Problems: Housing costs have reached crisis levels, with over 230,000 households spending more than 30% of income on housing. Production shortages, rising construction costs, and limited affordable rentals are driving greater financial strain for low-income residents.
Solutions: The initiative recommends creating housing through regulations and incentives, diversifying housing types with mixed-income development, and preserving existing affordable stock. These strategies are designed to increase supply at multiple price points and reduce the share of cost-burdened households.
Implement: Thrive can partner with local governments, developers, and employers to pilot incentive-driven projects that expand affordable housing. It can also strengthen preservation efforts by funding nonprofit collaborations and supporting sustainable housing solutions for cost-burdened families.

Title: RCT of Sources of Strength Testing Impact on Suicide Attempts and Tests of Moderation by Sexual Violence Victimization and Perpetration
Authors: Peter A. Wyman, Ian J. Cero, Dorothy L. Espelage, Tomei Reif, Sasha Mintz, Scott LoMurray, Kyle Nickodem, Karen H. Schmeelk-Cone, Alberto Delgado
MainIdea: Sources of Strength (a kind of peer-led intervention significantly reduces suicide attempts among high school students
Problems: High youth suicide rates, ineffective universal interventions for high-risk subgroups (e.g., severse sexual-violence survivors), lack of crossover impact on sexual violence outcomes. The reductions in the study were less effective for students with, for example, histories of experiencing sexual violence, which may be more prevalent in Central Florida owing to a variety of factors
Solutions: Implementation of school-wide peer-led interventions (Sources of Strength or similar programs), with focused programming or attention on high-risk groups
Implement: Recruit mental-health-organziation partners to design and  embed Sources of Strength pilots in Central Florida school districts; mini-grant program funded by THRIVE working group to enable schools and youth orgs (YMCA, BGC) to implement program

Title: The SDGs Are Good For Us: Report on Workshop
MainIdea: The workshop report contends community foundations (CFs) across Europe can use the UN SDGs as a strategic framework to deepen impact, strengthen partnerships, and enhance community relevance.
Problems: CFs may lack clear frameworks to demonstrate long-term community impact; disconnect between local CF work and global development narratives and/or cultural resistance or skepticism towards SDG relevance; lack of capacity (staff time, financial resources) to implement SDG frameworks; partnership-building skills within CFs are uneven or underdeveloped
Solutions: Embed SDGs into CF strategies, business plans, communications; use SDGs to foster cross-sector partnerships, especially through Goal 17; engage donors and community members through SDG-aligned giving; position CFs as local-global connectors, promoting global goals locally
Implement: Align local grantmaking strategies with SDGs, adapting examples from Northamptonshire CF; incorporate SDG-aligned communications tools and impact reporting

Title: The Information Barriers Holding Back Climate Action and How to Break Them
MainIdea: Cities struggle to meet climate goals because incomplete and inequitable information systems limit their ability to act boldly/decisively → article identifies three key strategies to improve information systems for climate action: improving public access to data, strengthening institutional relationships, and leveraging big data tools like AI responsibly
Problems: Lack of representative, community-centered data inhibits equitable decision-making; weak institutional knowledge → inconsistent cross-sector collaboration; limited analytic capacity makes it hard for cities to process data into actionable insights; risk of AI and data tools reinforcing social bias if poorly designed.
Solutions: Public-facing data dashboards that reflect community experiences; institutionalize cross-sector (private-public, etc.) relationships with documentation and internal knowledge sharing; use AI and predictive data tools (cautiously) to fill gaps and prioritize actions
Implement: Vetted, equity-focused data analysis tools (including AI) to support capacity-constrained/limited municipalities and host workshops on data democratization, participatory data collection, and climate storytelling.

Title: Civic Virus: Why Polarization is a Misdiagnosis
MainIdea: Our country is infected with a "civic virus" of polarization that separates people and promotes confusion, destabilization, and anxiety about our country. These divisions are not ideological polarization, but about social and psychological conditions (fear, apathy, lack of belonging).
Problems: People are living in a state of fight or flight when met with pressure to take a position, dont trust many others, view. There is a real search for acceptance and belonging that can lead to isolation and subsequent division.
Solutions: Create uniting safe spaces in local communities to help people cocreate solutions to identified challenges. Invest in and support local journalism and media and create more organizations and groups that unite a broader group of communities and individuals.
Implement: Promote states of empathy, openness, and belonging through formation and support of safe spaces in the community.

Title: The Impact of the Coronavirus on Food Insecurity in 2020 & 2021
Authors: Feeding America; Dr. Craig Gundersen and Feeding America Research Team
MainIdea: Feeding America evaluated the data on the how food insecurity spiked due to COVID-19 in 2020 and for 2021, with a special emphasis on how racial and geographic disparities were exacerbated.
Problems: Food insecurity rose from 10.9% (2019) to 13.9% (2020), impacting 45 million people and racial  disparities widened - reversing years of progress and hitting children especially hard.
Solutions: Lobby for expansion of federal nutrition assistance (e.g., SNAP, EBT, Pandemic-EBT); build out public-private partnerships that increased food aid (e.g., food banks, funders, businesses) and improve race-disaggregated data collection on food insecurity; invest in long-term anti-hunger infrastructure, including school meal programs and child-focused nutrition support.
Implement: THRIVE can fund local food security surveys and disaggregated data collection to identify disparities in Central Florida and partner with food banks and community orgs to both newly target and ensure continued high-volume food distribution in high-need ZIP codes

Title: The 2020 Hispanic Market Report
MainIdea: This article informs companies of the hidden value of the Hispanic population- their steadily increasing population growth within the US brings with it the status of major contributors to consumer spending growth. Various statistics are presented that advise companies on how to include Hispanics among their strategic business growth plans. Over the next 5 years since 2020, Hispanics are predicted to become about ⅕ of the US population.
Problems: -Companies are not yet fully engaged with Hispanics as consumers; the diverse array of values, interests, and behaviors only serve to increase the linguistic and cultural complexity of the Hispanic market. + It is important to note that Hispanics are by no means a homogenous group- their variation of Spanish and country of origin must be taken into account to successfully market to them.
Solutions: Hispanics across all generations, particularly The older ones, prefer marketing that reflects their culture. + marketing should focus on being culturally relevant and traditionally accurate. + Hispanics are also a more digitally-savvy group that embraces social media and related platforms to seek offers and discounts. Accordingly, they are also more receptive to mobile advertising.
Implement: Claritas has worked to Create CultureCodes applications that are used to identify The degree to which certain groups of consumers have adopted mainstream American values and beliefs or retained The values/beliefs of their countries of origin. this information, if disseminated, could help Companies target Hispanic consumers more accurately.

Title: State of American Well-Being: 2017 Community Rankings
MainIdea: The report measures well-being across US communities across five domains--purpose, social, financial, community, and physical
Implement: Tracking of FL communities positions on this measure as THRIVE moves?

Title: Florida Department of Law Enforcement Seal and Expunge FAQ
MainIdea: Overview of FL's legal framework and procedures for sealing and expunging criminal history records; eligibility criteria, application process, and impacts on public records and privacy.
Problems: Criminal records remain publicly accessible even after dismissal or dropped charges; complex, lengthy process to seal or expunge records; ineligibility due to certain charges or prior convictions; delays (12+ weeks) in processing applications; confusion about effects of pardons, clemency, or civil rights restoration; lack of clarity on how to proceed post-certificate issuance; persistent online presence of criminal information despite sealing/expungement.
Solutions: Streamline FDLE processing to reduce wait times; increase public awareness campaigns about eligibility and application process; provide accessible, clear guides and assistance for petition filing post-Certificate of Eligibility; improve coordination among agencies for record sealing notification; create resources for individuals to challenge online misinformation with private data aggregators; support legal aid access for petitioners; explore technological solutions to automate status updates and reminders; partner with community organizations to educate/assist affected populations.
Implement: Centralized multilingual online portal to guide residents through sealing/expungment steps; coordinate workshops with legal aid organizations and FDLE representatives in underserved neighborhoods for application assistance; collaborate with local internet advocacy groups to create “right to be forgotten” initiatives targeting mugshot and arrest data removal from private sites

Title: Easing the stress of poverty can bring down rates of child abuse and neglect
MainIdea: A new study from the American Academy of Pediatrics suggests economic stress relief through tax credits (Child Tax Credit and Earned Income Tax Credit) can lead to significant reductions in child maltreatment, especially neglect
Problems: Economic stress and child abuse/neglect in CF
Solutions: Recognize economic policy as a violence prevention strategy → use public health framing to advocate for upstream interventions (e.g. poverty reduction) to reduce child abuse and neglect; encourage multisector stakeholders (pediatricians, policymakers, community orgs) to support tax credit programs as part of a child wellbeing strategy; expand + stabilize income support programs like CTC and EITC
Implement: Advocate for economic policies (tax credits, guaranteed income pilots) as upstream child abuse prevention strategies & include financial stress reduction and income support in THRIVE's risk/protective factor mapping; support and initiate messaging campaigns that position anti-poverty programs as critical to preventing maltreatment and improving child development outcomes

Title: The Best and Worst States Overall for Women in 2015
MainIdea: Ranking of US states on women’s status across multiple areas: Political Participation, Employment & Earnings, Work & Family, Poverty & Opportunity, Reproductive Rights, Health & Well-Being, and Violence & Safety. Minnesota ranked highest overall; Alabama and Mississippi ranked worst. Florida ranked among the worst states, tied for 47th place overall.
Problems: FL among the bottom nationally on women’s overall status, signaling systemic barriers in pay equity, family supports, reproductive rights, and health outcomes
Solutions: Use 47 ranking as a call-to-action for local policy changes in CF to improve women’s conditions, especially in the areas where Florida ranks low (work-family policies, reproductive rights, poverty reduction)
Implement: Launch a “Central Florida Women’s Status Report” modeled after IWPR’s methodology/editorial process, to establish a baseline and track progress locally & organize educational forums and advocacy campaigns to address Florida’s low rankings, focusing on local opportunities for improvement

Title: Public and Private Partnerships in Disaster Relief: Leveraging Expertise for Maximum Impact
MainIdea: Pre-established partnerships, long-term planning, and coordinated aid delivery maximize impact and support community resilience during disaster responses/recovery
Problems: Disaster responses often lack coordination, rely on short-term reactive funding, and fail to account for long-term rebuilding needs; aid delivery is slowed by logistical challenges (e.g., lack of communication infrastructure); local needs overlooke/unknown by outside donors; insufficient pre-disaster planning and mitigation investment increases long-term costs and harm.
Solutions: Build and maintain cross-sector partnerships with local nonprofits, foundations, and businesses before disasters strike; prioritize cash donations and targeted giving over unsolicited goods; create streamlined platforms for peer-to-peer giving and employee relief; invest in mitigation and preparedness (e.g., planning funds, building codes); use data and feedback from previous disasters to inform strategy; leverage local knowledge to distribute resources effectively; support employee tracking and resilience tools (e.g., Safe America Foundation)
Implement: Promote use of FEMA and HUD benefit-cost data to advocate for pre-disaster investment; allocate flexible pre-disaster funding for mitigation and resilience upgrades (esp in particularly vulnerable neighborhoods)

Title: Research Brief: Findings from 1,273 US Girls on School, Technology, Relationships & Stress Since COVID-19
Authors: Lisa Hinkelman, Ruling Our eXperiences (ROX), June 2020
MainIdea: Findings from a survey of 1,273 girls (grades 5-12) on the mental health, academic, and relational impacts of COVID-19; reveals increased stress, isolation, and sadness, worsened by school closures, lack of social connection, and increased time online
Problems: 79% of girls feel more lonely/isolated since Covid-19; 25% report sadness/depression 4+ days per week; 40% higher stress levels, esp 9th-10th graders; 58% feel uncertain or scared about their future;  36% find schoolwork harder, 54% struggle with concentration and organization; ncreased family tensions; high social media use (1/3 spend 6+ hours/day)
Solutions: Schools strengthen social-emotional learning (SEL) & mental health support, mental health screenings; increased teacher/counselor engagement to support students remotely; foster healthier technology use and meaningful connection; parental monitoring of signs; expanded access to counseling and wellness resources
Implement: Deepen SEL and mental health resources into all youth-serving programs, universal mental health screenings in schools

Title: The Growing Hispanic Presence in the Sunshine State: Latinos in Central Florida
Authors: John A. Gutiérrez, PhD — Hispanic Federation
MainIdea: The report analyzes the rapid growth and diversification of Florida’s Latino population, particularly Puerto Ricans in Central Florida, and explores related challenges in education, workforce, language, and civic engagement.
Problems:
Low political participation: 1 in 3 Latinos in Central Florida not registered to vote.
Economic inequity: Over 70% earn <$50K; concentrated in blue-collar jobs.
Education gap: Only ~20% college graduates.
Few Latino voices in decision-making roles.
Solutions:
Expand Latino civic engagement and leadership pipelines.
Invest in education, workforce training, and youth development.
Encourage bilingual public communication.
Increase economic mobility through fair-wage jobs and skill development.
Implement:
Support voter registration and leadership initiatives among young Latinos.
Partner with local schools and employers on bilingual skill pipelines.

Title: Civic Life Today- A Millennial Perspective
MainIdea: This article reviews and breaks down a study of civic representation in millennials using a nationally representative sample. The report suggests that social issues today are a reflection of personal issues as well- an example being mental health services. Post-COVID has seen many millennials more willing to partake in civic engagement, and an increased focus on healthcare, criminal/social justice, education, and environment/climate.
Problems: In 2021, the top social issues for millennials were COVID, mental health and social services, and climate change. Furthermore, the #1 action taken by millennials for topics they care about was simply “learning more about it.”
Solutions: The study found that volunteering during the pandemic was seen as an influential action. This same mindset needs to be set as the new normal to have a bigger impact. + Companies should also be encouraged to be more involved in social issues- the report found that millennials check company websites for their efforts.
Implement: Encourage participation of companies in social issues, and stabilize the mindset of volunteers that their work is important and impactful. Spread even more awareness of the impact of volunteers.

Title: Here for Good
MainIdea: NHCF supports systemic, grassroots, and collaborative solutions across sectors to address major social challenges in health, education, climate, economic stability, community, and belonging
Problems: Low voter registration among youth; childcare access crisis; lack of diverse teacher pipelines; climate-related health threats; access to hygiene items; economic insecurity; youth mental health challenges; lack of Medicaid coverage

Title: 51 Useful Aging Out of Foster Care Statistics
Authors: Brandon Gaille - National Foster Youth Institute
MainIdea: 51 key statistics on the outcomes of youth aging out of foster care, highlighting systemic failures in housing, education, employment, and well-being  (incarceration, homelessness, etc.)
Problems: Nationally 23,000 youth age out of foster care each year with high rates of homelessness (20%), unemployment, school dropout, justicesystem  involvement; only 3% earn a college degree; majority experience PTSD; high rates of teen pregnancy; substance abuse; re-involvement in the justice system; high rates of placement instability, educational disruptions, lack of access to permanent supportive relationships; severe racial disparities, with black youth overrepresented and facing worse outcomes.
Solutions: Expand direct cash assistance and stable housing supports for youth aging out of foster system; increase access to postsecondary education pathways with dedicated supports; mentorship programs, particularly with credible messengers; strengthen permanency planning efforts to reduce aging out without a family; promote policies that extend foster care to age 21 with meaningful supports
Implement: Use data points in public education campaigns about the systemic failures youth face after foster care; advocate for expanded housing-first policies specifically for foster youth; invest in postsecondary completion programs targeting foster youth (grants?) microgrants?); collab with local organizations to pilot mentorship programs and housing supports focused on successful transitions

Title: Beyond the Moment- How NonProfits Can Leverage Civic Engagement
MainIdea: This article reviews the near-stranglehold that the federal government, through the use of grants, loans, and contracts has upon nonprofit organizations. The detrimental effect that the rescission of these funds, as threatened by President Trump, have led to calls for nonprofits to develop other methods to avoid overreliance on federal money.
Problems: - The main issue is that nonprofits are overreliant on federal funding and are being forced to choose whether to stay quiet and submit to the threats of the federal government (cutting funding) or to stand up for the sake of their communities (DEI).
Solutions: a potentially effective tool that Nonprofits can use to avoid total reliance and submission to federal threats is civic engagement. + civic engagement can include socially responsible investing, purchasing power, advocacy, time investments, and service provided.
Implement: help nonprofit organizations develop annual, yet active, engagement strategies that include The following: integration of civic action into existing programming, support for advocacy without a partisan stance, empowerment of local leaders, development of skills-based volunteering & more flexible options, leverage media engagement, employment of cross-sector collaborations to Create stronger movements, and remaining active year-round.

Title: Edward Kennedy Serves America
Authors: Corporation for National and Community Service (CNCS), Office of General Counsel
MainIdea: Landmark federal legislation dramatically expanding national service opportunities across the US between 2009-2017, including AmeriCorps, VISTA, Senior Corps, and new programs like the Social Innovation Fund, Volunteer Generation Fund, and service-learning initiatives in education
Problems: Underutilization of AmeriCorps and national service programs in CF, especially in rural areas with gaps in service-learning opportunities in K-12 and higher education in CF where there may be insufficient local infrastructure to scale volunteerism/innovation funding; lack of dedicated pathways for older adults, veterans, and court-involved youth in CF service ecosystems
Solutions: CF nonprofits and local governments can expand AmeriCorps partnerships across the new service corps tracks (Education, Healthy Futures, Clean Energy, Veterans, Opportunity); increase CF applications to Social Innovation Fund and Volunteer Generation Fund & expand senior engagement via Foster Grandparent and Senior Companion programs, pursue Summer of Service and Semester of Service programs targeting youth in underserved CF communities
Implement: CF institutions can pursue “Campuses of Service” designation; CF nonprofits and local governments can expand AmeriCorps partnerships across the new service corps tracks (Education, Healthy Futures, Clean Energy, Veterans, Opportunity); increase CF applications to Social Innovation Fund and Volunteer Generation Fund & expand senior engagement via Foster Grandparent and Senior Companion programs, pursue Summer of Service and Semester of Service programs targeting youth in underserved CF communities

Title: Using DEI to cultivate workplace wellness and addiction awareness
MainIdea: DEI  initiatives can produce psychologically safe, inclusive environments that support employees in recovery or those who abstain from alcohol.
Problems: Stigma and silence around addiction; workplace cultures normalization of alcohol, low EAP utilization; DEI often excludes addiction-related identity.
Solutions: Expand DEI expanses to include recovery themes; employee resource groups like Sober Force; organizations like ICARE to bring non-clinical addiction awareness education into the workplace.
Implement: Partner with ICARE to train HR reps and DEI officers across sectors (nonprofit, healthcare, government); fund the creation of recovery-friendly DEI Toolkits for companies, modeled on Salesforce’s Sober Force in the article

Title: Effective Alternatives to Youth Incarceration
MainIdea: Report argues against youth incarcerationand presents evidence-based alternatives to incarceration and recommends systemic reforms to reduce youth confinement/trauma and improve public safety outcomes
Problems: Youth incarceration increases reoffending, disrupts education/employment, causes trauma; youth of color disproportionately incarcerate; alternative programs often fail because systems still default to punitive practices, overuse confinement, apply interventions to lowrisk youth; lack intensive, community-based alternatives for highrisk youth; state and local policies incentivize incarceration over community supports.
Solutions: Implement alternatives to incarceration (e.g., credible messenger mentoring, family therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy, restorative justice, wraparound services); reform probation to reduce technical violations and focus on positive development; policies that reduce arrests, detention, and court referrals; family engagement and community connection; mandate intensive services for youth with serious offenses, not lowrisk youth.
Implement: Local and state governments funding for  community-based alternatives; credible messenger models, CBT, restorative justice; partner with local probation agencies to shift practices toward supportive supervision; legislative changes to prohibit incarceration for technical probation violations

Title: 2017 Status Report on Women & Children in Central Texas
Authors: Austin Community Foundation; RGK Center at UT Austin
MainIdea: Economic security, education, health, safety, and leadership outcomes for women and children directly impact community well-being; therefore, targeting gender-specific disparities  essential to closing the opportunity gap in Central Texas
Problems: High poverty rates among women and single mothers; wage gaps persist despite education; lack of affordable child care and housing; limited access to subsidized care and healthcare; growing suburban poverty with fewer services; disproportionate eviction and homelessness among women; teen pregnancy rates high among Hispanic teens; low kindergarten readiness and education completion among low-income students; limited leadership representation of women; rural areas have highest rates of child abuse and limited services.
Solutions: Expand access to quality, subsidized child care through provider incentives and subsidies; invest in housing solutions that support single mothers and women-led households; create job training programs tailored for women in suburban and rural communities; increase funding to teen pregnancy prevention programs targeting Hispanic youth; strengthen public transportation options connecting suburbs to job centers; fund outreach and enrollment efforts for health insurance; scale proven education interventions to improve kindergarten readiness and college completion for low-income girls; establish leadership pipelines for women in civic and nonprofit sectors; partner with housing authorities to reduce evictions through rental assistance; prioritize grants that support women’s economic security and basic needs; coordinate regional data collection and monitoring focused on racial and geographic disparities affecting women.
Implement: Require grantees to align outcomes with indicators such as wage parity, poverty reduction, child care access; identify and support child care providers willing to accept subsidies and increase capacity in rural areas; establish partnerships with school districts and nonprofits to improve early childhood education outcomes; co-develop leadership development programs for women in community leadership roles; prioritize grantmaking in suburban areas experiencing increasing poverty without matching infrastructure.

Title: Executive Summary:Amplifying Movement Knowledgefor Philanthropy
Authors: Innovation Network. Team includes: Katie Fox, Laura Lehman, Briana Rusin, and Johanna Morariu
MainIdea: Social movements are powerful but complex ecosystems distinct from other forms of social change. Philanthropy often struggles to support them effectively because of short-term funding cycles, risk aversion, and difficulty identifying non-traditional actors. The article calls for better movement literacy, long-term flexible funding, and new evaluation tools.
Problems:
Hard to distinguish movements from other change efforts.
Grassroots/emerging actors overlooked.
Risk aversion limits support for disruptive tactics.
Pressure for quick results undermines long-term movements.
Solutions:
Build movement literacy among funders.
Provide long-term, flexible funding.
Develop new evaluation tools and metrics.
Reframe success beyond short-term policy wins.
Implement:
Train funders on movement dynamics.
Share evaluation frameworks tailored to movements.
Connect funders with grassroots actors.
Advocate for flexible, long-term funding models.

Title: Task Force on Substance Abuse and Mental Health Issues in the Courts – Administrative Order AOSC14-46
MainIdea: This administrative order empowers a multidisciplinary task force for the State of Florida to develop policy and court based solutions to address the needs of individuals with mental health and substance use disorders in the justice system
Problems: High numbers of individuals with SUDs and mental illness entering courts/justice system; inconsistent application of Baker Act and Marchman Act; variability in drug, veterans, and mental health court practice of best practices; inadequate integration of behavioral health into judicial education.
Solutions: Update and enhance Baker and Marchman Acts? Integrate more behavioral health education  into judicial education
Implement: Collaborate w local judicial circuits to advocate for “fidelity” to evidence-based practices in drug, mental health, veterans courts, etc.; partner w judicial educators to introduce SUD/recovery-informed content into judge and court staff training; promote local cross sector planning teams (courts, behavioral health, public defenders, advocates) to implement recommendations from task force); plan + host policy roundtables with legislators, judges, and healthcare leaders to support modernization of the Baker and Marchman Act; ytrack implementation progress locally and elevate gaps/barriers to the state court system and task force stakeholders

Title: Vast New Study Shows a Key to Reducing Poverty: More Friendships Between Rich and Poor
Authors: Claire Cain Miller, Josh Katz, Francesca Paris, Aatish Bhatia - The New York Times
MainIdea: Economic connectedness (the extent to which low-income individuals form friendships with higher-income peerss) is a key predictor of upward economic mobility for children. Communities with more cross-class friendships significantly increase the adult earnings of children growing up in poverty, more than factors like school quality or racial composition
Problems: In CF as elsewhere persistent segregation by income→  limits cross-class friendships; most social interactions happen within similar economic classes; lower economic connectedness in many neighborhoods restricts economic mobility→economic and racial segregation reinforce each other & attempts at increasing diversity alone (e.g busing, zoning) don’t guarantee meaningful cross-class relationships.
Solutions: Foster economic connectedness by encouraging and facilitating meaningful cross-class interactions; design schools and extracurricular programs to promote, sustained group interactions (e.g., block schedules, mixed-interest clubs) & community spaces (parks, libraries) that bring together people from different income levels; mentorship programs that connect youth across socioeconomic lines; encourage colleges to assign roommates or groups from diverse economic backgrounds to foster friendships
Implement: Partner with schools to implement scheduling and extracurricular models that nurture cross-class friendships (and colleges to do cross-class roommate assignments); urban planning of parks and libraries to mix income interaction; collaborate with mentorship programs to scale cross-class pairing modelsing partnerships to track progress in economic connectedness at local levels.

Title: Trust in Civil Society: Understanding the factors driving trust in nonprofits and philanthropy
MainIdea: National trust in nonprofits is generally higher than other institutions but is declining slightly; trust in philanthropy remains low and stagnant. Trust varies significantly by socioeconomic status, gender, generation, and engagement.
Problems: Trust is lower among particular groups (women, Gen Z, rural residents); decline in trust is tied to economic precarity and lack of familiarity with nonprofits; risk of disconnection from younger generations and those who prefer direct giving rather than institutional giving
Solutions: Frequent engagement with local nonprofits can build trust; emphasize financial transparency and impact stories; policy advocacy around restoring IRS oversight to prevent scandals.; deepen community connections through grassroots engagement, especially with younger and economically marginalized groups
Implement: CFF can  invest in capacity-building for nonprofits around community engagement, transparency, and impact storytelling?

Title: Community Engagement Playbook
MainIdea: Strategies that can be implemented to commit to authentic engagement and intetgration of community members in decision and change-making efforts.
Problems: Lack of involvement of community members in decision making. Stand alone events of community engagement/change efforts.
Solutions: Lay the groundwork with inclusive + ongoing engagement frameworks.  Build a team with community groups, nonprofits, existing partnerships, and Community Liasons. Co-design a long-term plan with stakeholders. Engage with the community through media kits, inclusive events, and recording and analyzing feedback. Using the previous steps, take community-informed action.
Implement: Advocate for or act as community liasons to give a voice to all community members, build new and strengthen existing partnerships with community groups and nonprofits, conduct outreach to engage the community in decision making and gather feedback.

Title: Connecting Housing, Community, and Health
Authors: Charise Fong, Romi Hall & Jill Kunishima
MainIdea: The article contends that housing is deeply connected to community health and uses the San Pablo Area Revitalization Collaborative (SPARC) in Oakland as a model for a “housing-plus” approach that integrated resident power, cultural preservation, and integration with transit, health, and services, i.e., full social and physical infrastructure of communities
Problems: Housing is often built in isolation from health services, job access, or community spaces; lack of true resident power in planning processes; cultural erasure and displacement are concerns in Black and immigrant neighborhoods (e.g., Parramore, Pine Hills); food insecurity and poor access to care persist in low-income areas; poor transit options
Solutions: Help get mixed-use, transit-connected affordable housing off the ground; placemaking and culture-keeping in BIPOC neighborhoods. Fund Design Dash-style mini-grants for residents. Support wraparound services in housing (e.g., clinics, employment help). Form cross-sector pods for housing + health
Implement: Adopt elements of the SPARC model described in the article--e.g.,  fund and establish a Central Florida Design Dash program with microgrants for resident-led placemaking/culutral/food justice-related projects; strive for, as with SPARC, at least 51% resident participation in neighborhood-level develipment working rgoups; Cultural Equity & Placemaking Fund to support murals/public spaces/small business revitalization

Title: Connecting Housing, Community, and Health
Authors: Charise Fong, Romi Hall & Jill Kunishima
MainIdea: The article contends that housing is deeply connected to community health and uses the San Pablo Area Revitalization Collaborative (SPARC) in Oakland as a model for a “housing-plus” approach that integrated resident power, cultural preservation, and integration with transit, health, and services, i.e., full social and physical infrastructure of communities
Problems: Housing is often built in isolation from health services, job access, or community spaces; lack of true resident power in planning processes; cultural erasure and displacement are concerns in Black and immigrant neighborhoods (e.g., Parramore, Pine Hills); food insecurity and poor access to care persist in low-income areas; poor transit options
Solutions: Help get mixed-use, transit-connected affordable housing off the ground; placemaking and culture-keeping in BIPOC neighborhoods. Fund Design Dash-style mini-grants for residents. Support wraparound services in housing (e.g., clinics, employment help). Form cross-sector pods for housing + health
Implement: Adopt elements of the SPARC model described in the article--e.g.,  fudn and establish a Central Florida Design Dash program with $1K-2K microgrants for resident-led placemaking/culutral/food justice-related projects; ensure at least 51% resident participation in neighborhood-level develipment working rgoups; Cultural Equity & Placemaking Fund to support murals/public spaces/small business revitalization

Title: Florida Network
MainIdea: Social service aggregator service Find Help assembled this powerpoint to identify its Florida usage and impact
Problems: 26% of searches were housing-related, while 17% were food-related, reflecting housing and food insecurity in Florida.
Solutions: Expanded user base (only 5K distinct users)
Implement: ? Promotion of Find Help and its collaboration with Baycare?

Title: Journalism & Media Grantmaking- 5 Things You Need to Know & 5 Ways to Get Started
MainIdea: The purpose of this article is to help new grantmakers support reliable journalism efforts, as media businesses struggle with current advertising-based business models. It discusses the potentially detrimental impact that failing news organizations could leave- and the hole that nonprofit news organizations are attempting to fill.
Problems: When news organizations falter due to funding, public trust, or shifts in consumer habits, civic issues receive less independent scrutiny- leading to less informed citizens. This information vacuum is then filled by misleading or false news stories from non-reputable sources- further eroding the existing yet fragile public trust. + Influence of funders or even just the perception of influence in what stories are covered can ruin the credibility of journalism.
Solutions: new emerging business models for journalism Highlight The need for strong community interaction in an era of shifting demographics and personalized content. + more and more news organizations are experimenting with digital subscriptions, membership dues, and civic events that command sponsors. + Invest in community data.
Implement: Encourage foundations to get further involved in journalism grantmaking to sustain this crucial field As they explore and transition to reliable business models. + spread awareness about The danger of disinformation- how to Find quality sources, what information is trustworthy, and The importance of using multiple sources to verify information.

Title:
AMERICA
Goes to the Polls 2020
Policy and Voter Turnout
in the 2020 Election
Authors:
NONPROFIT VOTE REPORT TEAM:
George Pillsbury, Senior Policy Consultant
Caroline Mak, Field & Research Coordinator
Brian Miller, Executive Director
MainIdea: The report analyzes how state election policies shaped voter turnout during the 2020 election. The highest in 120 years at 67% of eligible voters. States that made voting easier (mail voting, same day registration, vote at home policies) achieved significantly higher turnout, while restrictive states lagged behind.
Problems:
Large disparities in turnout between states that ease vs. restrict voting access.
Some states still require excuses for mail voting or have early registration cutoffs.
Electoral College limits campaign engagement to only a few battleground states.
Inequitable access reduces representation and voter confidence.
Solutions:
Expand Vote at Home and Same Day Registration policies nationwide.
Simplify mail ballot procedures (no-excuse voting, ballot tracking, drop boxes).
Implement automatic and online voter registration.
Increase election competitiveness through reforms (Ranked Choice Voting, nonpartisan redistricting, or National Popular Vote compact).
Implement:
Build partnerships with nonpartisan organizations like Nonprofit VOTE and Vote at Home Institute.
Use data to identify underserved populations and promote civic literacy.

Title: 2019–2020 Tracking Report: LGBTQ+ Grantmaking by U.S. Foundations
MainIdea: The report provides an overview of philanthropic giving trends supporting LGBTQ+ communities during 2019–2020, analyzing over $200 million in U.S.-based grants. It identifies disparities in funding distribution, with most resources concentrated in coastal regions and underinvestment in transgender, BIPOC, and Southern organizations.
Problems:
Only 0.3% of total U.S. foundation funding supports LGBTQ+ causes.
Less than 20% of funds go to transgender-led or BIPOC-led organizations.
Southern states, including Florida, remain severely underfunded.
Solutions:
Target grants toward intersectional, community-led advocacy.
Build regional funding collaboratives (especially in the South).
Enhance data transparency on grant distribution and impact.
Implement:
Partner with national funders to scale intersectional initiatives (e.g., racial justice + LGBTQ+ rights).
Support research on regional funding disparities and visibility.

Title: Eight Principles to Transform Care for Young People in the Justice System
MainIdea: Eight principles that juvenile justice systems should adopt to create more restorative, community-driven, and developmentally appropriate responses for young people in custody
Problems: Overreliance on punitive, prison-like youth confinement systems that fail to rehabilitate or promote growth; structural racism; trauma and adverse childhood experiences are often ignored or exacerbated in youth detention; disconnection from family, community, education, and future opportunities; lack of meaningful, developmentally appropriate programming in custody; low standards of care and accountability within youth confinement settings.
Solutions: Replace prison-like settings with healing-centered, developmentally appropriate environments; staff culture → equity; redesign spaces to be safe, welcoming, and therapeutic; staff development focused on youth development and restorative practices; purposeful, engaging, skill-building programming; treat families as essential partners in planning and reentry; sustained community connections through mentorship, credible messengers, and organizational partnerships
Implement: Advocate for CQI (continuous quality improvement) processes in any remaining custodial settings, including direct input from youth and families; support capacity building for grassroots orgs to lead credible messenger programs, family engagement, and youth development services.

Title: Fact Sheet: National Strategy on Gender Equity and Equality
MainIdea: Factsheet issuing issues first-ever national gender strategy to advance the full participation of all people – including women and girls – in the United States and around the world
Problems: CF lacks integrated, cross-sector gender equity framework; persistent disparities exist in CF across economic security, gender-based violence services, maternal health, STEM participation, and leadership representation.; CF’s care economy (e.g., childcare, elder care) is underfunded and undervalued, relating to an dworsening economic insecurity for women of color; gender equity gaps in CF are exacerbated by low wages, poor workplace protections, lack of paid leave
Solutions: THRIVE can support the adoption of localized gender equity action plans tied to the Strategy’s priority areas (economic security, health, leadership); increasing care infrastructure investment and expanding access to gender-based violence prevention and response services.
Implement: Partner with CF chambers, city governments, and schools to promote leadership pipelines for women and gender-expansive people.

Title: The Future of Aging: Senior Cohousing as an Answer to the Loneliness Epidemic
Authors: Blue Zones, with research support from AARP and the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
MainIdea: The report explores senior cohousing (shared living communities designed for older adults) as a solution to loneliness, housing insecurity, and healthcare strain in aging populations.
Problems:
Rising loneliness and social isolation among seniors.
Lack of affordable, age-friendly housing options.
Strain on healthcare systems due to preventable isolation-related illnesses.
Solutions:
Develop senior cohousing models emphasizing connection, shared resources, and purpose.
Integrate wellness and accessibility design (universal design, walkability, gardens).
Encourage public–private partnerships for affordable housing finance.
Implement:
Pilot age-friendly cohousing partnerships with developers and nonprofits.
Promote community-building and health-integration models for seniors.

Title: From fiction to reality: How resilience hubs could help people weather disasters — and build community
MainIdea: Promotes resilience hubs as trusted, community-led spaces that build social connection and deliver essential services both in times of crisis (e.g., disasters, climate effects) and everyday life
Problems: Traditional disaster preparedness focuses excessively narrowly on individual responsibility & misses community needs; communities most impacted by climate risks often lack trusted, accessible third spaces; historical disinvestment in low-income and communities of color reduces resilience and overnment disaster infrastructure is often underutilized due to lack of trust
Solutions: Community-led resilience hubs that offer everyday services (education, celebrations, cultural events) and can transform into disaster response centers when needed; govt funding and policy shifts to support trusted community spaces rather than building top-down responses—examples from Lake City Collective in Seattle and Baltimore’s early resilience hub strategy show success through hyperlocal engagement and community ownership; s services, reliable power, safe infrastructure, trusted communications, and localized governance → create neighborhood-scale infrastructure for disaster resilience, equity, and community connection
Implement: Pilot resilience hubs?

Title: Issue Spotlight: Civic Engagement in Greater Miami
MainIdea: This article explores the definitions of a civically engaged community, the standards by which they are measured, and factors to increase participation using civic health data from Miami. Civic engagement is measured with the combination of civic participation and civic infrastructure (systems that allow participation).
Problems: There are many barriers to civic engagement- wavering trust in institutions, transitional demographics (Miami specific), and under-resources and overstretched nonprofits. + Factors that measure access to civil infrastructure, such As internet connection and access to libraries are also lower for The Miami area than The U.S. average.
Solutions: - Public engagement initiatives (Miami did one called Thrive 305) + Equity plans, increase access to local news, citizen civics education, & cross-sector collaborations.
Implement: - Encourage voter registration and action. Promote a variety of organizations that require volunteers- make it easier to mobilize. + Support local non-profits working on advocacy and civic engagement!

Title: The Women & Girls Index 2024
Authors: Equitable Giving Lab, Women's Philanthropy Institute
MainIdea: Dataset and national-level analysis of philanthropic giving to organizations serving women and girls, revealing persistent underfunding and emphasizing the importance of intentional giving strategies
Problems: Women's organizations receive small fraction of total philanthropic support; structural barriers to women’s labor participation (e.g. caregiving burdens, lack of childcare, wage disparities)
Solutions: Increase targeted funding to local organizations supporting women and girls, particularly those serving women of color and LGBTQ+ women; campaigns (e.g. around International Day of the Girl) to elevate funding and attention
Implement: Philanthropic challenge or matching grant program for funders focused on women’s equity;encourage corporate and foundation partners to commit to transparency in funding gender-focused initiatives

Title: The Status of Women in Florida by County: Population & Diversity
Authors: Gina Chirillo, Julie Anderson, Cynthia Hess
MainIdea: FL population more diverse, older, and more immigrant-rich than the national average.
Problems: Underrepresentation of women in aging and retirement-related planning; racial disparities in access to services among Black, Hispanic, and Native American women; Underserved immigrant women, especially in rural North Florida; high rates of single motherhood with limited economic support; lack of infrastructure to support elder women, who outnumber men in all counties; low representation and support for same-sex partner households in certain regions; veterans and rural women not well represented in service provision
