Solutions: Expand elder care planning and services targeted at older women; Improve culturally competent health and education access for Hispanic, Haitian, Cuban women; focus resources on counties with growing female immigrant populations (e.g., Hendry, Broward, Collier); increase supports for single-mother households through housing, employment, and childcare; strengthen rural equity programs to reach women in under-resourced northern counties; design inclusive public policy frameworks for LGBTQ+ families; address disparities in veteran support for female vets in regions with military bases
Implement: Partner with immigration and refugee support orgs to provide culturally specific outreach in Central Florida counties with high female immigrant populations; launch senior women’s livability audit to assess healthcare access, transportation, and housing in counties with >30% women over 65; pilot (?) wraparound services for single mothers in high-need ZIP codes (co-located childcare, housing navigation, job placement); establish training and capacity grants for nonprofits working with LGBTQ+ families and households in underserved counties

Title: KIDS COUNT in Florida: Child Health & Well-Being Rankings 2022
MainIdea: Florida ranks 35th in overall child well-being with significant disparities across counties; better outcomes correlating w greater investment in healthcare, education, family support
Problems: high rate of uninsured children; high child poverty levels; poor education outcomes for 4th and 8th graders; limited access to affordable childcare; administrative barriers to social safety net access; high rates of youth justice system contact; low graduation rates; uneven investment across counties
Solutions: 12-month continuous Medicaid/KidCare coverage for children; eliminate waiting periods for KidCare reinstatement; reduce cost and expand hours of VPK; implement a state-level Earned Income Tax Credit; reduce SNAP red tape and extend renewal to 12 months
Implement: Advocate FL EITC? & expansion of Medicaid/KidCare eligibility

Title:
MEASURING GIVING TO WOMEN’S
AND GIRLS’ ORGANIZATIONS
MainIdea: Women & Girls Index tracks philanthropic giving to women’s and girls’ organizations in the US; despite growth to over $10 billion in 2021, giving remains under 2% of total charity
Problems: Chronic underfunding of women’s and girls’ organizations locally, mirroring national trends; limited capacity among local women-serving nonprofits to access large-scale philanthropic dollars
Solutions: Campaign to increase giving to women’s and girls’ organizations; use data from WGI to inform donors; eEstablish a Central Florida Women & Girls Fund to pool resources from individuals, corporations, and foundations targeting gender equity; develop training programs for local nonprofits on grant writing and donor engagement specific to gender-focused funding streams
Implement: Coordinate an annual “Give to Women & Girls in Central Florida” fundraising drive aligned with International Day of the Girl (Oct 11) and WHM; regular/quarterly capacity-building workshops for women-led nonprofits on sustainable fundraising, impact measurement, coalition building;

Title: Journalism Grantmaking: New Funding, Models and Partnershipsto Sustain and Grow the Field
Authors: Sarah Armour-Jones, Consultant to Media Impact Funders
MainIdea: As traditional news business models collapse, philanthropy has become essential in sustaining and reimagining journalism. The report tracks the rise of journalism grantmaking ($1.7 billion since 2009), and explores new funding models, community foundation roles, and collaborative efforts that strengthen local and investigative reporting.
Problems:
Collapse of local newsrooms and loss of community reporting.
Public distrust and misinformation undermining journalism.
Concentration of funding in a few large organizations; limited local support.
Lack of sustainable business models for nonprofit newsrooms.
Solutions:
Expand collaborative reporting networks (such as: environmental and investigative partnerships).
Support community foundations to sustain local journalism ecosystems.
Scale NewsMatch and other matching-fund programs to boost individual giving.
Implement:
Support grants for community-based journalism that promote transparency and civic engagement.
Partner with local foundations and universities to fund media collaborations.

Title: CHMI San Diego Wrap-Up Report (2020)
Authors: Clinton Health Matters Initiative (CHMI), in partnership with The San Diego Foundation and County of San Diego
MainIdea:
The Clinton Foundation worked with San Diego to reduce racial and ethnic disparities in the
treatment and life outcomes of youth involved in the child welfare and juvenile justice systems.
Problems: San Diego had high disproportionality of African American, Native American, and Latino youth in child welfare and juvenile justice systems; lack of trauma-informed care in agencies.; fragmented behavioral health access; inconsistent mentorship for justice-involved youth & disruptive caseworker turnover; inadequate community voice in system design w/ arriers to equitable education and early care.; oor cross-sector data coordination.
Solutions: The Clinton Foundation found successful results when it implemented trauma-informed Code of Conduct across 30+ orgs, built out mentoring infrastructure and directories, created behavioral health hubs with mapped service gaps (FCI COULD HELP WITH SOMETHING LIKE THIS), expanded affordable early care via $4M+ fund., built a system of youth-driven feedback., trained CWS and Probation staff in trauma and bias, and streamlined caseworker transitions
Implement: Create a local trauma-informed "Code of Conduct" and train agencies with community co-design, early childhood and mentoring orgs to create hub-style wraparound support models

Title: Amplifying Movement Knowledge for Philanthropy
MainIdea: Social mvmts require different philanthropic approaches compared with other social change forms (e.g., funder require better and different tools, literacy, and flexibility)
Problems: Lack of tools for IDing and supporting non-traditional, emerging movement leaders; institutional restrictions limiting funding to grassroots groups; funders pressure/demand short term and concrete policy wins over long term wins
Solutions: New evaluation metrics beyond immediate policy wins; flexible unrestricted funding from philanthropic orgs
Implement: Funder education workshops and "Movement Flex Fund" for multi-year unrestructed grants

Title: Four Imperatives for Centering Community in Philanthropy
Authors: John Brothers, Funmilayo Brown, Ailun Ku, Terry Mazany
MainIdea: Philanthropy as a sector must shift power to communities by sharing decision-makingand supporting grantees holistically, empowering community ownership of data and metrics.
Problems: There are serious problems with the systems of traditional philanthropy. These systems reinforce inequity via closed power networks, burdensome application processes, lack of community input, insufficient general operating support, and extractive data practices.
Solutions: “Show up” meaningfully in communities inside and outside of funding cycles; democratize funding decisions; increase the amount of unrestricted long-term financialsupport; involve communities in metric-setting and governance; respect community ownership of data; provide tools for capacity and measurement; compensate communities for their time and input
Implement: Philanthropic groups should develop community advisory boards with funding authority and community-driven strategy sessions; simplified funding and reporting processes and unrestricted grants with inflation adjustment; co-create metrics and provide technical support; encourage organizational leadership to engage and embed w/i community

Title: America Goes to the Polls 2020
MainIdea: 2020 saw the highest voter turnout since 1900, with major increases linked to expanded mail voting, same day registration (SDR), and competitive elections, such that states that made voting easier (vote-at-home, SDR) had significantly higher turnout than those with restrictive policies.
Problems:
Disparities in turnout remain between states, especially where early/mail voting and SDR are restricted/voter-access restrcitions, plus.
structural inequities like the Electoral College reduce voter engagement in non-competitive states.
Implement: Support voter engagement projects, especially in low-turnout areas

Title: Aging with purpose: Why meaningful engagement with society matters
MainIdea: This MHI report finds older adults are healthier and happier when they are meaningfully engaged in society through work, volunteering, and community involvement
Problems: Older adults vulnerability and increasing isolation when social determinants of health are at play; FL larger older, retired population; ageism and outdated beliefs about older adults’ capacity; lack of coordinated multi-sector strategies and barriers to employment and volunteering
Solutions: Nonprofit and NGOs can empower older adults through community-led programs targeted toward getting their engagement - e.g., recruiting older volunteers in specific
Implement: Map where older adults are underserved in CF programs and policies and co-design intergenerational programs and opportunities to get involved/volunteer for and with older adults - RedActiva, Experience Corps?

Title: No Kid Hungry Florida | January 2023 Toplines
MainIdea: This document covers Change Research’s polling conducted on behalf of No Kid Hungry, which reveals high rates of food insecurity across income levels, particularly among parents and rural Floridians, and bipartisan support for addressing child hunger through school meal expansion and policy reform
Problems: Of FL adults surveyed, 41% reported symptoms of food insecurity in the past year, with parents (47%) and rural residents (48%) were especially affected; 76% of Floridians said it’s harder to afford groceries since early 2022; 1/3 of middle-income households ($50K–$100K) also experienced food insecurity; 66% say they would struggle to afford groceries after a $1,500 emergency; [parents skipping meals, worrying about school lunch costs, and accruing school meal debt
Implement: Partner with school systems to eliminate school meal debt and offer meal cost relief (grants?); use polling insights to craft messaging for legislative advocacy and voter education campaigns Support universal school meal access initiatives in Central Florida districts

Title: Resilience Hubs Guide
MainIdea: Comprehensive blueprint for planning, funding, and implementing Resilience Hubs (community-serving facilities designed to support residents, provide critical services during disruptions, and improve community well-being)
Problems: Climate change exacerbates risks for low income communities and communities of color due to historic disinvestment and infrastructure neglect; existing emergency preparedness is often top-down, fails to meet community-defined needs, and focuses narrowly on disasters; gaps in community trust of government-led services, unding and operational barriers for equitable community infrastructur; disconnected services increase community vulnerability in both normal and crisis conditions.
Solutions: Resilience Hubs piloting that can leverage trusted community spaces (centers, parks, housing sites) to provide culturally appropriate services year-round and in crises; integrated approach to emergency preparedness, climate adaptation, and social resilience.; multi-sectoral funding strategies: federal, state, foundation, impact investors, utilities; ommunity ownership and participation from project design to implementatio; examples of successful programs (e.g., Maryland Energy Administration grants)
Implement: Support policy that integrates resilience hubs into local All Hazard Mitigation Plans and sustainability strategies. Identify and inventory existing trusted community facilities to assess suitability as resilience hubs.  & co-design community resilience hubs with grassroots leadership, applying the guide’s framework; use Resilience Hub Analysis Tool to map funding streams (FEMA, DOE, HUD, impact investors, utilities)& state/city resilience funds (e.g., similar to Maryland’s Hub program); build relationships with local emergency management and climate agencies to center community hubs in disaster planning.Pilot incremental upgrades (e.g., solar+storage, resilient communications) at THRIVE sites as models.

Title: Newaygo County Area Dashboard
Authors: Fremont Area Community Foundation and The Right Place (via West Michigan Regional Dashboard)
MainIdea: Newaygo's  dashboard tracks county-wide progress on economic, social, and environmental indicators to enable strategic, data-informed community action
Problems: Lack of shared metrics for county-wide performance; difficulty tracking progress on community goals like poverty reduction, workforce development, and education; insufficient alignment between community programs and measurable outcomes; siloed data preventing collective action.
Solutions: Develop and maintain a central dashboard with regularly updated indicators; align dashboard metrics with CF strategic goals (workforce, education, poverty); educate community partners on how to use dashboard data to align their programs; highlight and prioritize “green” improving indicators for celebration and replication; identify “red” declining metrics to target with funding and support; require grantees to demonstrate how their programs move specific dashboard metrics; hold workshops to train nonprofits and partners in data analysis and dashboard use; embed dashboard orientation into onboarding for CF-funded initiatives.
Implement: Develop similar data dashboard (if one does not already exist in CF)?  train THRIVE partners in interpreting dashboard data; incorporate dashboard metrics into community convenings and reporting cycles

Title: Community News Funds 2022 Update
MainIdea: This update tracks the growth of Community News Funds (CNFs): partnerships between local newsrooms and foundations that build philanthropic support for local journalism as a public good. CNFs raised over $6 million in 2022 and are becoming a sustainable model for strengthening civic information ecosystems.
Problems:
Newsrooms lack staff for fundraising and capacity building.
Philanthropy often prioritizes short-term projects over sustainability.
Shifting donor focus (from journalism to direct services) creates instability.
Solutions:
Hire dedicated fund-development staff and train local news leaders.
Build multi-year funding streams and cross-newsroom collaborations.
Engage community foundations to steward funds and raise awareness.
Create national data and evaluation tools to track impact and equity.
Implement:
Partner with local media and community foundations for philanthropic training.
Use THRIVE’s platform to highlight news equity and information access as key determinants of community health.

Title:
WOMEN’S ECONOMIC
EMPOWERMENT AND DOMESTIC
VIOLENCE
MainIdea: Women’s economic empowerment (WEE) programs can both decrease and increase the risk of DV — economic and socio-cultural mechanisms by which DV risk can be reduced (via bargaining power, reduced household poverty) or heightened (through backlash, resource extraction)
Problems: Economic empowerment can increase DV via backlash if gender norms are challenged; well-intentioned program design often overlooks DV risk, especially in patriarchal settings and grassroots implementers may not be equipped to identify or respond to gender-based violence; lack of integrated approaches combining economic and social empowerment; potential neglect of men’s roles in changing gender dynamics, increasing tensio
Solutions: Conduct DV risk assessments before implementing WEE programs; integrate gender norms education, conflict resolution, and violence awareness into WEE programs; facilitate access to confidential services and safe spaces for women; engage men in positive masculinity, household role discussions, and anti-violence education; foster partnerships between WEE and VAW organizations for referrals and training; monitor DV risk at baseline, midline, and endline of programs
Implement: Incorporate DV risk assessments and gender dynamics training into THRIVE’s WEE-adjacent programs; partnerships between THRIVE sites and local domestic violence services; cross-training between economic development and anti-violence practitioners; community dialogues for men and women on shared economic goals and violence prevention;  tools to track unintended consequences (like backlash) and adapt programs

Title: Voices of Hope: Conclusions from a 2016 Research Project
MainIdea: Case studies for four projects  focusing on breaking the cycle of female poverty, educating girls in STEM, trauma-informed addiction therapy, and accessible reproductive health
Problems: High poverty among female-headed families; lack of integrated wraparound support for single mothers; girls underrepresented in STEM, especially in Duval and surrounding counties; high rates of trauma and substance abuse among justice-involved women; insufficient access to adolescent reproductive health services; Aging female population without sufficient elder caregiver support; Lack of affordable childcare; Work-life policies (paid leave, flexible schedules) severely limited
Solutions: Peer-mentored STEM education w hands-on learning for girls; truama-informed add'n treatment; integrated support systems for diverse family structures, elder caregivers, and paid leave policies
Implement: “Poverty to Prosperity Hub” offering bundled services: job training, childcare, budgeting help, mental health, and housing support for single mothers—modeled on Crittenton/WGA examples; partner with local STEM organizations (e.g., Girl Scouts, Black Girls Code) to launch after-school robotics and coding clubs in Title I middle schools; recruit local female STEM professionals as mentors; fund training for clinicians in trauma-informed addiction programs (e.g. Seeking Safety, SPARCS) and scale Gateway Community Services capacity

Title: How to Prepare Your Community for a Disaster
MainIdea: Key to stronger disaster preparedness is leveraging and strengthening community institutions, creating shared emergency plans, and organizing neighbors around mutual aid and resilience before professional responders arrive
Problems: Many communities lack strong social infrastructure--> it harder to coordinate during disasters; individual preparedness efforts are limited in scope and duration; vulnerable neighbors (e.g., elderly, disabled, low-income) at higher risk and often overlooked; modern disconnection and reliance on digital platforms weaken geographically grounded mutual aid networks
Solutions: Support and strengthen existing civic and social organizations (e.g., churches, PTAs, block clubs) as disaster readiness hubs; incorporate emergency prep into community events in engaging ways (e.g., trivia, workshops); encourage neighbors to form communication networks, share skills/resources, and create shared evacuation plans; assess local vulnerabilities and create inclusive support plans for at-risk residents; promote training like FEMA’s “Until Help Arrives” to empower local bystander response
Implement: Integrate disaster preparedness curriculum or training into community grantmaking; create a template or toolkit for localized preparedness planning (communication trees, skills maps, emergency roles); support peer-led outreach to assess neighborhood vulnerabilities (elderly, disabled, medically fragile residents)

Title: Age is Just a Number When It Comes to Technology
MainIdea: This AARP report finds older adults are adopting technology but their comfort and confidence are not strong, especially among those 70+. Tech is used primarily for physical health, not mental health, and adoption is uneven across age groups.
Problems: Digital divide among older adults especially among 70+ population; low confidence in privacy/security and limited use of tech for mental health; potential tech fatigue (too many subscriptions/services)
Solutions: Public-private partnerships to subsidize devices/services and community tech hubs with live tutorials; tech mentoring (intergenerational?)
Implement: Design/fund community “digital ambassador” programs pairing youth with older adults; partner with health orgs to promote & integrate wearables into wellness programs & pop-up clinics in libraries/senior centers

Title: The Spectrum of Community Engagement to Ownership
MainIdea: The goal of this doc is to provide methods to strengthen and transform local democracies through community-driven civic leadership. There are six stances leadership may take to community regarding engagement in decision making and resource allocation: they may ignore, inform, consult, involve, collaborate with, or defer to.
Problems: Equity gaps, climate vulnerability, marginalized communities, power imbalanaces. Communities are often overlooked or only informed/consulted when involving them in planning and setting goals, but communities need to have ownership and control over their resources.
Solutions: Facilitate collaborative community driven governance through collaboration with policy/planners, co-fundraising with community-based organizations, capacity-building workshops, translation of community priorities into policy.
Implement: Promote this resource/its methods to leaders of community-based organizations, local governments, philanthropic partners, and more. Assess the general state of community engagement and how members feel about their degree of ownership and plan campaigns collaboratively.

Title: The Spectrum of Community Engagement to Ownership
MainIdea: Community resilience requires deep democratic participation--FP offers Spectrum of Community Engagement Ownership tool
Problems: Marginalization/exclusion of low-income communities from local and regional decisionmaking and policymaking; tokenistic superficial “consultation” processes
Solutions: Application and dispersement (knowledge sharing) of Spectrum framework; infrastructure for participatory process (e.g., participatory budgeting, cooperatives, advisory committees with delegated authority)
Implement: Pilot the Spectrum tool with city and county governments in Central Florida

Title: Who Are Opportunity Youth?
MainIdea: Opportunity youth are teens ages 16-19 or 16-24 who are  neither in school nor working; significant barriers due to poverty, systemic inequities, disabilities, or justice system involvement; targeted supports and equitable access to education, work, and stable relationships are critical to their success
Problems: High rates of youth disconnection from school and work; racial and geographic disparities especially impacting Black, Latino, Native American, and other marginalized youth; lack of access to high-quality education, counseling, early warning systems, youth development programs, and flexible learning pathways; insufficient workforce opportunities tailored to youth needs; risks of lifelong socioeconomic instability without intervention.
Solutions: Implement early warning and support systems in schools for struggling youth; provide flexible and tailored learning and post-secondary transition programs; increase equitable access to internships, apprenticeships, and career-technical training
Implement: Partner with school districts and nonprofits to enhance early warning systems identifying disengaged youth and connecting them with academic, mental health, and social supports

Title:
Cross-System Collaborative Approaches to
Promote Workforce Development for
Youth with Justice Involvement:
A Collection of Case Studies
Authors: Ben Washington, Elijah Mercer, Krista White
MainIdea: Case study review examines five cross-system initiatives focused on improving workforce outcomes for justice-involved youth
Problems: Youth who have records face barriers to employment in short and long term despite programs because of siloed systems (justice, workforce, education, community organizations) failing to coordinate effectively in getting them opportunities; lack of data sharing and accountability systems to track youth outcomes

Title: Extended Loneliness Can Make You More Vulnerable to Extremist Views
MainIdea: Extended loneliness (exacerbated by social disconnection, economic pressures, and digital overstimulation) is associated with insecurity, fear, and increased susceptibility to extremist views and ideologies.
Problems: Widespread loneliness and social isolation, worsened by economic stress/SM/pandemic aftershocks; hyperindividualism and workaholism crowd out time for community; SM → division, outrage, and superficial connectionpsychological and physiological consequences of chronic loneliness
Solutions: Reframing of loneliness as both a public health and sociopolitical issue; reducing digital overstimulation and algorithmic outrage cycles; more opportunities for genuine social bonds, real-world connection, and creativity; acknowledging and addressing “uprootedness” as a root of extremism
Implement: “Digital hygiene” community SM campaign to educate residents on managing attention economy harms and increasing offline time

Title: Livable Orlando Age-Friendly Action Plan (2022–2025)
Authors: City of Orlando / Mayor’s Office of Livability & Healthy Aging
MainIdea: This is an outline of efforts undertaken and to be undertaken to make Orlando more inclusive, accessible, and supportive of residents of all ages (naming 25 goals and 89 action items guided by WHO’s 8 Domains of Livability)
Problems: Access to age-appropriate housing and mobility options; limited affordable healthcare and support services; gaps in intergenerational civic engagement; social isolation among elderly &  information dissemination to elderly; inaccessibility in public infrastructure for those with disabilities or age mobility-related needs
Solutions: Redesigning of outdoor spaces and transportation systems to be safer and more elderly-friendly; increased affordable and accessible housing options and civic and employment opportunities and healthcare access/social supports/dementia-friendly services; integration of elderly-frieldnly principles into citywide master plans (e.g. Vision Zero, Green Works)
Implement: Partner w city departments to implement key built environment goals, esp around parks, sidewalks, and transportation; develop & support aging-in-place programs, including cross-sector housing and health service collaborations;  intergenerational programming, volunteering, and community engagement projects to reduce isolation

Title: Working With Foundations- Community Economic Development Toolkit
MainIdea: This article takes into consideration the increasing role that foundations are playing within the world of nonprofits and explains tactics that nonprofits could use to deepen their relationships. Nonprofit, as organizations that produce change, are viewed positively as instruments that foundations can fund to meet like-minded goals. On the other hand, nonprofits appreciate the flexibility, grant frequency, application simplicity, and speed that foundations can provide funding.
Problems: public agencies are inflexible and slow to act. Furthermore, their ability to give grants is very limited. Thus, foundations have emerged As a frontrunner for Nonprofits to obtain The necessary funds to expand and operate better.
Solutions: Tips for non-profits: do Foundation-specific research, each Foundation has their own priorities, form personal relations with foundations and help them to feel involved with The work, watch and report outcomes to foundations that like to see results, and look into program related investments (PRIs) to see if The meet non-profit needs.
Implement: spread awareness about The Tips mentioned in The Solutions section. + provide guidelines or suggestions for how to get like-minded foundations/non-profits to interact. + Although There exists databases of foundations and their goals, they may often be outdated, or are a resource that require subscriptions to access. Finding a way to remove these barriers for community-based Nonprofits that do not have a lot of resources to begin with would help to solidify their base.

Title: Orlando & the Sustainable Development Goals: A Voluntary Local Review of Progress
MainIdea: Orlando Voluntary Local Review reports how city policies and programs align with the UN SDGs, identifies data gaps, and recommends next steps for localizing sustainable development goals
Solutions: Use the SDGs to guide strategy development and cross-sector partnerships.; data-sharing collaborations to fill local indicator gaps; participate in national and international SDG learning networks (e.g., ICLEI, HLPF); position SDG work as both a community engagement and policy advocacy tool
Implement: Integrate SDG framing into grant application and impact reporting processes; VLR structures to align donor advised funds with specific SDG targets (e.g., food insecurity, health equity); support CF leaders in participating in ICLEI and HLPF SDG learning exchanges

Title: Survivors Know Best:
MainIdea: Report documents the launch & impact of FreeFrom’s Safety Fund, an unrestricted cash assistance program for survivors of intimate partner violence (IPV), addressing the economic fallout of IPV and survivors’ urgent financial needs, especially during Covid →makes a strong case for cash-based support and systemic accountability for survivor financial security.
Problems: IPV creates massive financial harm: survivors experience economic abuse, theft, coerced debt, income loss, property damage; urvivors have extremely limited savings (<$250), making it hard to weather crises → COVID-19 increased financial vulnerability through escalating violence, lost income, stolen relief funds, court delay; traditional systems (banks, employers, insurance, courts) fail to provide direct, survivor-centered financial support & survivors face high barriers to accessing help and distrust from  providers
Solutions: Unrestricted cash assistance directly to survivors to meet immediate safety and stability needs and center trust in survivors’ decisionmaking about their own needs; expand survivor services across systems (banks, employers, utilities, etc.) beyond traditional IPV services; build financial infrastructure for survivors (debt relief, safe employment, utility assistance)
Implement: Embed the “trust survivors” principle into THRIVE’s survivor safety and economic justice work; advocate for local governments to establish cash assistance programs for survivors of violence + set up programs at CF nonprofits?  → ush for debt relief and utility forgiveness for survivors in THRIVE sites; push for survivor inclusion in workforce and economic programs (safe employment pathways); host briefings with survivor-led orgs like FreeFrom to uplift cash-based solutions and change funding narratives in the anti-violence sector.

Title: Civic Engagement in Greater Miami
MainIdea: Civically engaged communities are stronger communities, with better employment rates, schools, health, and governance.
Problems: Civic engagement as a formula can be understood as civic participation multiplied by civic infrastructure.  Miami fared below the national average and had lower rates of public participation and donation to political causes, compared to most of its urban peers. However, Miami’s civic infrastructure fares better than the national average for access to parks, but lags behind peers in digital connectivity and use of public libraries.
